name: Help Center Home
title: Help Center Home
description: The Help Center Home page provides a central entry point to support content, allowing users to search, browse categories, access device-based help, view saved articles, and reach generic support contact options using data loaded from getHelpCenterHomeData.

core_features: Load Help Center home data (categories, highlighted topics, devices, saved articles preview, and generic contact availability/options) via getHelpCenterHomeData on page initialization.
Provide a global Help Center search form that redirects users to the search results page with the entered query and an initial page index.
Display a browsable grid/list of main help categories returned by getHelpCenterHomeData, with each category linking to its dedicated category page using its code as a URL parameter.
Display highlighted topics from getHelpCenterHomeData that, when selected, trigger a search-based navigation scoped to the selected topic via the search results page.
Expose a devices entry section using device data from getHelpCenterHomeData, allowing users to navigate to the Devices page for device-specific help.
Show a saved-articles preview using savedArticlesPreview from getHelpCenterHomeData, including a summary of the number of saved items and a short list of recently saved articles.

Provide quick access to generic contact support using genericContactAvailable and genericContactOptions from getHelpCenterHomeData, redirecting to the contact support page with appropriate source parameters.
user_workflows:
Global search from Help Center home:
1. User lands on Help Center Home (index.html).
2. Page initialization triggers getHelpCenterHomeData; while data loads, the search form is still available.
3. User types a search term into the main search input.
4. User submits the search form (e.g., by pressing Enter or clicking a submit button).
5. The page validates that the query is non-empty.
6. The browser navigates to search_results.html with URL parameters: query set to the user-entered search term and page set to 1.

Browse help by category:
1. User lands on Help Center Home and getHelpCenterHomeData returns a categories array.
2. The category grid component renders one card/list item per category using the categoryCode and categoryName fields.
3. User clicks a category card.
4. The browser navigates to category.html with the URL parameter categoryId set to the clicked category’s categoryCode value.

Access highlighted topics:
1. User lands on Help Center Home and getHelpCenterHomeData returns highlightedTopics.
2. A highlighted topics component renders a clickable element for each item using topicCode and topicName.
3. User clicks a highlighted topic.
4. The browser navigates to search_results.html with URL parameters: query set to the selected topic’s topicName (or another derived string based on topicCode) and page set to 1, effectively showing results related to that topic.

Browse help by device:
1. User lands on Help Center Home and getHelpCenterHomeData returns devices.
2. A device entry component renders a list/grid of devices using deviceCode and deviceName.
3. User clicks any device tile or a general 'browse by device' entry control provided by the component.
4. The browser navigates to devices.html (no additional URL parameters), where device-specific help is managed.

Review saved articles:
1. User lands on Help Center Home and getHelpCenterHomeData returns savedArticlesPreview.
2. The saved articles preview component displays totalSavedCount and a list of latestSaved items with their titles, summaries, and categoryName values.
3. User clicks a 'View all saved articles' link in this component.
4. The browser navigates to saved_articles.html (no additional URL parameters); the full saved articles page handles listing and any further navigation to individual articles.

Reach generic contact support:
1. User lands on Help Center Home and getHelpCenterHomeData returns genericContactAvailable and genericContactOptions.
2. If genericContactAvailable is true, a contact support section shows one or more contact options based on genericContactOptions (e.g., labels, types, and estimatedWaitMinutes).
3. User clicks a primary 'Contact support' quick link or button in this section.
4. The browser navigates to contact_support.html with URL parameters source set to 'generic' and sourcePageType set to 'help_center_home'.
5. The contact_support.html page then uses its own logic to determine available contact methods and to create a SupportRequest.
interactions: On page load (initialization): call getHelpCenterHomeData with no parameters, populate in-memory state for categories, highlightedTopics, devices, savedArticlesPreview, genericContactAvailable, and genericContactOptions, and then render all dependent components.
Search input interaction: as the user types in the search field, the component updates its internal controlled value; on clear, it resets the field but does not call any API on this page.
Main search submit: on form submission, prevent default behavior (if needed), validate the query is non-empty, then redirect to search_results.html?query={encodedQuery}&page=1.
Category card click: when a user clicks a category element, read the associated categoryCode from the bound data item and navigate to category.html?categoryId={encodedCategoryCode}.

Highlighted topic click: when a user clicks a highlighted topic, derive a query string from the topic (using topicName or topicCode) and navigate to search_results.html?query={encodedDerivedQuery}&page=1.
Device tile click: when a user clicks any device entry control, navigate to devices.html; this interaction does not append additional URL parameters from this page.
Saved articles 'View all' click: when a user clicks the 'View all saved articles' control in the saved articles preview component, navigate to saved_articles.html.

Contact support link/button click: when a user clicks the main contact support control, navigate to contact_support.html?source=generic&sourcePageType=help_center_home regardless of which specific contact option is shown on this page.

Data reload interaction (optional): if a manual refresh action is provided (e.g., a retry button when getHelpCenterHomeData fails), clicking it re-invokes getHelpCenterHomeData and re-renders dependent components.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Help Center Home page does not use URL parameters for its own internal state; it always initializes with a fresh call to getHelpCenterHomeData when index.html is loaded or reloaded. The page’s client-side state consists solely of: (1) the response payload from getHelpCenterHomeData (categories, highlightedTopics, devices, savedArticlesPreview, genericContactAvailable, genericContactOptions), (2) transient UI state such as loading/error flags, and (3) transient form state such as the current value of the search input. No persistent state is stored in localStorage, sessionStorage, or similar mechanisms. Instead, any navigation that needs to preserve context does so via URL parameters passed to other pages:
- Global search: redirects to search_results.html with query and page parameters, where query is the exact string from the search input and page is set to 1.
- Category navigation: redirects to category.html with categoryId set to the selected category’s categoryCode from getHelpCenterHomeData.
- Devices navigation: redirects to devices.html without additional parameters; device-specific state is handled on that page.
- Saved articles: redirects to saved_articles.html without parameters; that page is responsible for loading the complete saved articles list.
- Contact support: redirects to contact_support.html with source='generic' and sourcePageType='help_center_home' to allow the target page to initialize a SupportRequest with the correct source context. The Help Center Home page does not create or mutate SearchQuery, SupportRequest, or other entities directly; it only influences them indirectly via navigation and URL parameters.

components.functionality: Controls initial data loading for the Help Center Home page, orchestrates the call to getHelpCenterHomeData on page initialization, manages loading and error states, and passes the resulting data to child components (search form, category grid, highlighted topics, devices entry, saved articles preview, and contact section).
event_handlers: onInit
onDataLoadSuccess
onDataLoadError
onRetryLoad

components.functionality: Provides a global Help Center search input at the top of the page. It captures a free-text search query from the user and, upon submission, navigates the browser to search_results.html with query set to the entered text and page set to 1. It does not call any interface directly or perform asynchronous search on this page.
event_handlers: onInputChange
onInputClear
onSubmit

components.functionality: Renders a list or grid of help categories using the categories array from getHelpCenterHomeData. Each category item displays its categoryName and description (if provided) and is clickable. Clicking a category navigates to category.html with the categoryId URL parameter set to that category’s categoryCode.
event_handlers: onCategoryClick

components.functionality: Displays a list of highlighted help topics using highlightedTopics from getHelpCenterHomeData. Each topic item shows topicName and description (if available). When a topic is clicked, the component derives a search query string from the topic (for example, using topicName) and navigates to search_results.html with query set to the derived string and page set to 1.
event_handlers: onTopicClick

components.functionality: Shows entry points for device-based help using the devices array from getHelpCenterHomeData. Each device entry uses deviceName and description (if available). When a user clicks any device tile or an overall 'browse by device' control, the component navigates to devices.html, where device-specific topics are handled.
event_handlers: onDeviceClick
onBrowseByDeviceClick

components.functionality: Presents a compact view of the user’s saved articles based on savedArticlesPreview from getHelpCenterHomeData. It displays the totalSavedCount and a small list of latestSaved items showing title, summary, categoryName, and savedAt values. The primary interaction is a 'View all saved articles' control that redirects users to saved_articles.html for full management of saved articles.
event_handlers: onViewAllSavedClick

components.functionality: Uses genericContactAvailable and genericContactOptions from getHelpCenterHomeData to render a contact support area. If genericContactAvailable is false, the component can display no options or an appropriate message; if true, it lists the available contact methods (by label, type, and estimatedWaitMinutes). A primary 'Contact support' action within this component navigates to contact_support.html with source set to 'generic' and sourcePageType set to 'help_center_home'.
event_handlers: onContactSupportClick

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a centered main content column. The article list appears to use two equal-width columns (approximately 6 + 6) with a central gutter.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow within a centered main content column on a full-width dark background. A horizontal top navigation (outside this spec) anchors the page. Immediately below it, the help-center-search-form acts as the hero module, centered and visually dominant. Beneath the hero, content is grouped into clear sections stacked vertically: first the category grid (browsing entry), then a highlighted topics list (mirroring the ‘Most Popular Articles’ pattern), followed by a devices entry section, saved articles preview, and finally a generic contact support section. Each section has a left-aligned heading within the same centered column, with content laid out either in two columns or a simple grid to echo the mockup’s structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow within a centered main content column on a full-width dark background. A horizontal top navigation (outside this spec) anchors the page. Immediately below it, the help-center-search-form acts as the hero module, centered and visually dominant. Beneath the hero, content is grouped into clear sections stacked vertically: first the category grid (browsing entry), then a highlighted topics list (mirroring the ‘Most Popular Articles’ pattern), followed by a devices entry section, saved articles preview, and finally a generic contact support section. Each section has a left-aligned heading within the same centered column, with content laid out either in two columns or a simple grid to echo the mockup’s structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top navigation occupies a modest band at the top (around 8–10% of the viewport height). The search hero occupies a large vertical block directly below, using generous padding above and below (roughly 20–25% of the viewport height in total), making it the primary focal area. The remaining sections share the lower half of the page, each separated by strong vertical spacing (approximately 32–48px) to create distinct content bands. Within each section, headings use modest vertical margins, while lists and grids use regular intra-item spacing to maintain readability without feeling sparse. Horizontally, all content sits in a centered column (about 70–80% of viewport width, with a max-width around 1040–1200px) with substantial left and right margins framing the content.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate and tuned for scannability. No section feels crowded: headings have clear breathing room, list items have comfortable vertical gaps, and grids preserve a generous gutter between columns. The hero search area is intentionally open, with more whitespace to emphasize the primary task. Subsequent sections slightly increase density (more items per viewport) but maintain clear separation and consistent rhythm, echoing the mockup’s balance between information and readability.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow is strongly vertical, with a subtle left-to-right pattern inside each section. Users move from the brand/navigation at the top to the centered search bar, then to its helper text directly beneath. From there, the eye continues downward to the left-aligned section headings, scanning down each section and its content: first categories, then highlighted topics (two-column list read down the left column, then the right), then device-based entry points, saved articles, and finally contact options. Consistent alignment of headings and a repeated pattern of section-band + heading + content reinforces an intuitive scroll-based exploration.

layout.overall_description: The Help Center Home is set on a full-width, dark background with a single centered content column that feels like a framed panel floating in the middle of the viewport. A compact top navigation spans the full width at the top, while all help-center content sits within the central column beneath it. The upper third of the page is dominated by a wide, centered search form with ample vertical whitespace above and below, visually anchoring the experience as the primary way to get help. Just below the search input, a short, light-weight line of explanatory text is centered and easy to scan. As you scroll down, the layout transitions into clearly defined bands of content: a left-aligned heading introduces each section, and within each band, content is arranged in simple, flat layouts—either two-column text lists or a low-profile grid—mirroring the mockup’s ‘Most Popular Articles’ treatment. The highlighted topics list uses two equal-width text columns with generous vertical spacing, echoing a clean streaming-service help index. Device help, saved articles, and contact options each occupy their own well-spaced section, all aligned to the same central column and separated by consistent vertical gaps, creating a calm, cinematic, and utilitarian help hub. The page ends with a subtle horizontal divider near the bottom, visually echoing the mockup’s terminating line and hinting at the footer below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page container spans the full browser width and height with a dark background, acting as the canvas for the entire Help Center Home. Within it, a centered main content column runs from just below the top navigation to the bottom of the page. This column occupies roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, capped at around 1040–1200px max-width, leaving generous left and right margins on large screens to create a framed, cinematic feel. All page sections (search form, category grid, highlighted topics, devices, saved articles, contact support) are stacked vertically within this column, following a simple top-to-bottom document flow. Vertical spacing between major sections is consistent and spacious—approximately 32–48px—creating clearly separated content bands while maintaining an overall cohesive flow. The bottom of the container includes a thin, full-width horizontal divider line aligned to the main column edges, visually signaling the end of the help content and echoing the mockup’s subtle footer separator.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a standard flow container; content scrolls naturally. The main content column remains centered at all viewport widths, with responsive padding (16–24px on small screens and 32–48px on large screens) to preserve the framed look.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form sits near the top of the main content column, immediately below the top navigation and any global header spacing. It occupies a large vertical block roughly equivalent to 20–25% of the visible viewport height, making it the dominant interaction area. Horizontally, the search field is centered and spans about 60–70% of the main column width on desktop, with a minimum comfortable width on small screens (e.g., nearly full width minus side padding). The search input itself is a single, flat text field with subtle borders, and a search icon positioned inside on the right side of the field. There is generous padding above the input (about 48–64px from the bottom of the nav) and below it (about 32–48px before the next section), reinforcing its hero status. Directly beneath the search box, centered horizontally and aligned with its width, a short line of explanatory text (e.g., "Search for help with your account, billing, and devices") appears with minimal vertical spacing (around 8–12px) from the input, visually reading as a caption to the field. The entire search block feels vertically balanced and slightly elevated within the upper third of the page, drawing the eye inward and downward from the top navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows with the page (non-sticky, non-floating). On narrower screens, it expands to nearly full column width while maintaining vertical spacing proportions; the input and its caption remain centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category grid appears directly below the search hero block, separated by a generous vertical gap (approximately 40–48px) to clearly distinguish it as the next section. A left-aligned section heading (e.g., "Browse by Category") sits at the top of this section, anchored to the left edge of the main content column. The heading has modest bottom margin (16–24px) before the grid. Below the heading, the categories are laid out in a low-profile grid that respects the underlying 12-column structure. On desktop, the grid uses either three equal-width columns (4+4+4) or two columns depending on the number of categories, but each tile aligns to the same left and right boundaries as the article columns elsewhere. Tiles are rectangular or pill-like, flat, and text-first (category title, optional short label), with internal padding of roughly 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal. There is a consistent horizontal gutter between columns (about 24–32px) and vertical spacing between rows (about 16–24px), giving the grid a structured but breathable feel. On smaller screens, the grid collapses to two columns and eventually to a single full-width column, while preserving comfortable spacing between items. The entire block visually reads as a clear browsing gateway immediately following search.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flow within the main column. The grid reflows responsively, decreasing the number of columns as viewport width shrinks while maintaining consistent gutters and section spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlighted topics list sits below the category grid, with a noticeable vertical gap of about 32–40px separating the two sections. It closely mirrors the mockup’s ‘Most Popular Articles’ area. At the top of this section, a bold, left-aligned heading (e.g., "Most Popular Articles" or "Highlighted Topics") stretches visually across the main content column. The heading has some breathing room above (24–32px from the previous section) and a moderate margin below (16–24px) before the list begins. Under the heading, the content area is divided into two equal-width text columns, each representing approximately 6 columns of the 12-column grid with a wide central gutter (about 32–40px). Each column contains a vertical stack of topic links presented as single-line or multi-line questions/titles. Items in each column are left-aligned and separated by consistent vertical spacing (around 12–16px), ensuring they are easy to scan without feeling crowded. The text runs edge-to-edge within each column area, with no additional card chrome or icons, keeping the section text-forward and utilitarian. The user’s eye naturally scans down the left column first, then moves to the right column due to the central gutter and equal visual weight of both columns. On smaller screens, this two-column layout gracefully collapses to a single full-width column, preserving the same vertical rhythm between items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flow section. The two-column list is responsive: it becomes a single-column stacked list on narrower viewports while maintaining the same heading alignment and section spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The devices entry section is positioned below the highlighted topics list, separated by a clear vertical gap (approximately 32–40px). A left-aligned heading (e.g., "Get Help by Device") anchors the top of the section, aligning with the same content edge as other headings. There is a small margin below the heading (16–20px) before the device options begin. The device entries are arranged in a horizontal strip that fits within the main column, visually lighter than the article lists. On desktop, devices appear as a row or two of compact tiles or chips (e.g., "TV", "Mobile", "Tablet", "Game Console"), each with subtle padding (10–14px vertical, 16–20px horizontal) and ample spacing (16–24px) between them horizontally. If there are many devices, they wrap onto a second line while maintaining equal spacing and alignment. The section height is moderate—not as tall as the search hero or the article list—keeping it clearly secondary. On small screens, device items align in one or two columns stacked vertically, maintaining comfortable vertical gaps. Overall, this area feels like a clearly defined quick-access strip that doesn’t overshadow the main search and article content but is easy to spot while scrolling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flow; devices wrap naturally within the main column. On narrower viewports, the layout stacks vertically with consistent spacing and remains left-aligned to the section heading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved articles preview appears beneath the devices section, again separated by a consistent vertical margin (around 32–40px). It occupies the full width of the main content column but presents as a slightly more compact, card-like band compared to the broader article lists. A left-aligned heading (e.g., "Saved Articles") sits at the top with a margin below it (12–16px). Below the heading, a small subset of saved items—typically 3–5 links—are displayed either as a single vertical list or as two shorter columns depending on available width and content volume. Each item is a text-only link with comfortable vertical spacing (8–12px) between items. The overall height of this section is kept modest compared to the highlighted topics list, emphasizing that it’s a quick preview rather than a full index. A subtle inline control like a "View all" link can be right-aligned on the same horizontal line as the heading or placed just below it, but all elements remain contained within the same central column. On narrow screens, the layout simplifies to a single vertical list with the heading above and any "View all" control aligned under or to the right of the heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flow section. The internal list can flex between one and two columns depending on viewport width, but it always respects the overall main column width and the left-aligned heading baseline.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The generic contact support section sits near the bottom of the page, below the saved articles preview, with the same generous vertical separation (approximately 32–48px). It reads as the final major content band before the page’s bottom divider. A prominent, left-aligned heading (e.g., "Still Need Help?" or "Contact Support") introduces the section, with a slightly larger top margin to emphasize it as the last resort action (24–32px from the previous section). Below the heading, contact options are laid out in a simple structure that prioritizes clarity over density. On desktop, this can be a two-column arrangement: the left column containing brief explanatory text (1–2 short lines) and the right column containing one to three stacked CTAs (e.g., "Chat with us", "Email support", "Call us"), each spanning the width of the right column with consistent vertical spacing (12–16px). Alternatively, multiple options can be presented as a vertical list with subtle separators. The entire block remains within the main content column and aligned to the left edge. On smaller screens, the content stacks into a single column, with the heading at top, explanatory text beneath it, and CTA buttons or links stacked vertically with comfortable spacing. The section’s bottom edge aligns visually with a thin horizontal separator line (matching the main column width) that signals transition into the site’s footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flow at the bottom of the main content column. Layout becomes single-column on small screens; the final horizontal divider line sits directly beneath this section, stretching across the main content width.

name: Search Results
title: Search Results
description: Shows help articles that match a search query and lets users refine results by topic, device, plan, rating, maturity level, and sort order, then open article details.

core_features: Parse incoming URL parameters (query, topicCodes, deviceCodes, planCodes, minRating, maturityLevel, sortBy, page, pageSize) to establish the initial search state.
Fetch available filter and sort options (topics, devices, plans, rating buckets, maturity levels, sort options) using getHelpSearchFilterOptions and bind them to filter controls.
Execute a help article search using searchHelpArticles with the current query, filters, sort, and pagination, and render the list of results.
Display search metadata, such as the current query text and the total number of matching results, based on the searchHelpArticles response.
Allow users to refine results via topic, device, plan, rating, and maturity filters, updating URL parameters and re-running searchHelpArticles.
Allow users to change sort order using sort options from getHelpSearchFilterOptions, updating the sortBy URL parameter and re-running searchHelpArticles.
Provide pagination controls (page and pageSize) that update URL parameters and trigger new searchHelpArticles requests.
Display applied filters as removable chips/badges that directly manipulate the corresponding URL parameters and trigger new searches when cleared.
Handle the no-results case by showing an appropriate empty state while still allowing users to adjust filters and query.
Navigate to article.html with articleId and lang parameters when a user clicks a search result item.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Arrive from global search
1. User enters text into the global Help Center search bar on another page (e.g., index.html) and submits.
2. Browser navigates to search_results.html with at least the query URL parameter set.
3. On page load, the page reads all current URL parameters and initializes local view state.
4. The page calls getHelpSearchFilterOptions to load available topics, devices, plans, rating buckets, maturity levels, and sort options.
5. The page then calls searchHelpArticles with queryText equal to the query URL parameter, filters derived from topicCodes/deviceCodes/planCodes/minRating/maturityLevel, sortBy from the URL (or a default derived from sortOptions if not present), and pagination from page/pageSize.
6. The user sees the results list populated along with filter controls that reflect the current URL-based state.
7. The user can click any result to navigate to article.html?articleId=...&lang=... .

Workflow 2: Refine results using filters
1. User is on search_results.html with an existing query and an initial results list.
2. User selects or deselects one or more topic, device, or plan filters from the filter controls.
3. The filter component updates the corresponding URL parameters (topicCodes, deviceCodes, planCodes) to represent the selected set, resets page to 1, and updates the browser URL via navigation or history API.
4. After the URL is updated, the page re-reads URL parameters and calls searchHelpArticles with the new filters.
5. Results list updates to reflect the new filtered result set, and applied filter chips update to match the current URL state.

Workflow 3: Change rating or maturity filters
1. User interacts with the rating filter (based on ratingBuckets from getHelpSearchFilterOptions) and selects a minimum rating.
2. Component updates the minRating URL parameter (or removes it if cleared) and resets page to 1.
3. Page triggers a new searchHelpArticles call with the updated minRating filter.
4. User optionally selects a maturity level from the maturity options; the component sets the maturityLevel URL parameter (or clears it) and again resets page to 1.
5. Another searchHelpArticles call runs, and the list refreshes to reflect the rating and maturity filters.

Workflow 4: Change sort order
1. User opens the sort dropdown populated via sortOptions from getHelpSearchFilterOptions.
2. User chooses a sort option.
3. Component sets the sortBy URL parameter to the chosen value and resets page to 1.
4. Page triggers searchHelpArticles with the same query and filters, but with the new sortBy value.
5. Results list re-renders in the new order while preserving the applied filters.

Workflow 5: Paginate through results and change page size
1. User scrolls to pagination controls at the bottom of the results list.
2. User clicks a next/previous page control or a specific page number.
3. Component updates the page URL parameter while keeping all other parameters unchanged.
4. Page triggers searchHelpArticles with the new page value and existing filters/sort.
5. If user changes the page size control, component updates pageSize in the URL, resets page to 1, and triggers another searchHelpArticles call.
6. The results list shows the appropriate subset of articles according to the new pagination settings.

Workflow 6: Remove applied filters via chips
1. User sees one or more applied filter chips representing current topic, device, plan, rating, or maturity filters.
2. User clicks the remove action on one chip.
3. Component removes the corresponding value from the appropriate URL parameter (or clears it entirely if no values remain), resets page to 1, and updates the URL.
4. Page triggers searchHelpArticles with the reduced filter set.
5. The chip disappears, and the results list updates accordingly.
6. Optionally, user clicks a 'clear all filters' action that clears all filter-related URL parameters (topicCodes, deviceCodes, planCodes, minRating, maturityLevel), resets page to 1, updates the URL, and triggers a fresh searchHelpArticles call.

Workflow 7: No results handling
1. User runs a search (via query or filters) that results in zero matches.
2. searchHelpArticles returns totalResults equal to zero and an empty results array.
3. Results list component renders an empty state instead of result items, using queryText and appliedFilters from the response to indicate the current search context.
4. Filter and sort controls remain interactive; the user adjusts query, filters, or sort.
5. URL parameters update, triggering new searchHelpArticles calls until matching results are found or the user navigates away.

Workflow 8: Navigate to an article from results
1. User identifies a relevant article in the results list.
2. User clicks the article title or summary.
3. The click handler constructs a navigation URL to article.html including articleId (from the result item) and lang (from current UI language state).
4. Browser navigates to article.html with those parameters; search_results.html does not persist any additional state beyond the URL parameters that were already present.
interactions: On page load (or when URL changes via browser navigation), parse all expected URL parameters and initialize the search state before making any API calls.

On page load, call getHelpSearchFilterOptions once to populate all filter and sort controls; bind the returned options to UI controls and pre-select values based on current URL parameters where applicable.

On page load (after or in parallel with filter options), call searchHelpArticles using queryText taken from the query URL parameter, filters derived from topicCodes/deviceCodes/planCodes/minRating/maturityLevel, sortBy from the URL or a default, and pagination from page/pageSize.

When the user submits a new search query via the query input on this page, update the query URL parameter (and reset page to 1), then navigate or update the URL and trigger a new searchHelpArticles call using the updated queryText.

When the user toggles a topic filter checkbox, recompute the topicCodes parameter value (as a comma-separated list), update the URL with the new value and reset page to 1, then re-run searchHelpArticles.
When the user toggles a device filter option, recompute deviceCodes in the URL similarly and trigger a searchHelpArticles refresh.
When the user toggles a plan filter option, recompute planCodes in the URL similarly and trigger a searchHelpArticles refresh.
When the user selects a rating bucket, set or clear the minRating URL parameter, reset page to 1, update the URL, and call searchHelpArticles.
When the user selects a maturity level, set or clear the maturityLevel URL parameter, reset page to 1, update the URL, and call searchHelpArticles.
When the user changes the sort option, update the sortBy URL parameter, reset page to 1, update the URL, and call searchHelpArticles.
When the user clicks an applied filter chip’s remove icon, remove the corresponding value from its related URL parameter, reset page to 1, update the URL, and call searchHelpArticles.
When the user clicks 'clear all filters', remove topicCodes, deviceCodes, planCodes, minRating, and maturityLevel from the URL, reset page to 1, update the URL, and call searchHelpArticles.
When the user changes page via pagination controls, update the page URL parameter, keep other parameters intact, then call searchHelpArticles with the new page.
When the user changes the page size selector, update pageSize in the URL, reset page to 1, and call searchHelpArticles.
When the user clicks a search result item, navigate to article.html with articleId and lang in the URL; no additional API call is needed on the search results page for this interaction.
When the user uses browser back/forward buttons, re-parse the URL parameters and re-run searchHelpArticles (and ensure filter controls reflect the URL state) so history navigation works correctly.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page’s functional state is fully derived from and synchronized with URL parameters and API responses, without using localStorage or other client-side persistence.

URL → State on load:
- query: If present, used as queryText for searchHelpArticles; also populates the local search input field. If absent, the search input is empty and searchHelpArticles is called with an empty string or omitted queryText, depending on implementation.
- topicCodes: If present, parsed as a comma-separated list of topic codes. These map to selected topic filters in the UI and are passed as filters.topicCodes to searchHelpArticles. If absent, no topic filter is applied in the search.
- deviceCodes: If present, parsed into an array of device codes, mapped to selected device filters and passed as filters.deviceCodes. If absent, device filtering is not applied.
- planCodes: If present, parsed into an array of plan codes and passed as filters.planCodes. If absent, no plan filtering is applied.
- minRating: If present, parsed as a number and passed as filters.minRating. It also maps to a selected rating bucket control based on ratingBuckets from getHelpSearchFilterOptions. If absent, no minimum rating filter is applied.
- maturityLevel: If present, used to select a maturity level option and passed as filters.maturityLevel. If absent, no maturity filter is applied.
- sortBy: If present, used as the sortBy argument for searchHelpArticles and as the selected value for the sort control. If absent, the page uses a default sort value derived from getHelpSearchFilterOptions.sortOptions (for example, the first option), without hardcoding any specific value.
- page: If present and valid, used as the current page index for searchHelpArticles and reflected in pagination controls. If absent or invalid, the page defaults to 1.
- pageSize: If present and valid, used as the requested page size for searchHelpArticles and reflected in the page-size selector. If absent or invalid, a default pageSize is chosen based on system configuration or a non-hardcoded default.

State → URL updates:
- Any user interaction that changes queryText, any filter (topicCodes, deviceCodes, planCodes, minRating, maturityLevel), sortBy, page, or pageSize updates the corresponding URL parameters. The page logic ensures that when filters or sort change, page is reset to 1, while page changes preserve query and filters.
- Updates to URL parameters are done via navigation or history APIs so that the address bar always represents the current state. Back/forward navigation therefore replays previous states by re-parsing URL parameters.

API interactions:
- getHelpSearchFilterOptions is called once during initialization (and may be reused across subsequent state changes) to retrieve available topics, devices, plans, rating buckets, maturity levels, and sort options. The options returned are not hardcoded in the page; instead, all filter and sort controls bind directly to this API’s response.
- searchHelpArticles is called on initial load and after any state change that affects queryText, filters, sortBy, page, or pageSize. Its arguments come directly from the parsed URL parameters and optional defaults derived from getHelpSearchFilterOptions.
- The searchHelpArticles response defines the authoritative search state: queryId, queryText, appliedFilters, sortBy, page, pageSize, totalResults, and the results array are used to render the results list, summary information, and pagination controls. No static article data is embedded in the page; all article-related fields (articleId, title, summary, categoryName, topicNames, deviceNames, planCodes, averageRating, ratingCount, lastUpdatedAt) are taken directly from the API response.

components.functionality: Provides a text input for users to view or modify the current search query and submit a new search, which updates the query URL parameter and triggers a new searchHelpArticles call.
event_handlers: onInit: Read the query URL parameter and populate the input value accordingly.
onInputChange: Update the local form state with the user’s current text but do not call APIs yet.

onSubmit: Prevent default form submission, update the query URL parameter (and reset page to 1), then navigate or update history so that the page logic re-parses URL parameters and calls searchHelpArticles with the new queryText.

onClearQuery: When the user clears the query field, remove or empty the query URL parameter, reset page to 1, update the URL, and trigger searchHelpArticles with the updated (possibly empty) queryText.

components.functionality: Displays high-level information about the current search, such as the query text and total number of results, based on the searchHelpArticles response.
event_handlers: onSearchResponse: When searchHelpArticles returns, read queryText and totalResults and update the summary display.
onURLChange: When URL parameters change and a new searchHelpArticles response is received, update the summary to reflect the latest queryText and totalResults.

components.functionality: Shows topic, device, plan, rating, and maturity filters. Options are loaded dynamically via getHelpSearchFilterOptions, and selected values are synchronized with URL parameters and used by searchHelpArticles.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getHelpSearchFilterOptions and populate internal collections of topics, devices, plans, ratingBuckets, maturityLevels, and sortOptions. Use current URL parameters to mark which options are initially selected.

onTopicFilterChange: When a topic option is toggled, compute the new set of selected topics, serialize to a comma-separated list, update topicCodes in the URL, reset page to 1, and trigger searchHelpArticles.
onDeviceFilterChange: When a device option is toggled, compute and serialize selected device codes, update deviceCodes in the URL, reset page to 1, and trigger searchHelpArticles.
onPlanFilterChange: When a plan option is toggled, compute and serialize selected plan codes, update planCodes in the URL, reset page to 1, and trigger searchHelpArticles.
onRatingFilterChange: When a rating bucket is selected or deselected, set or clear the minRating URL parameter accordingly, reset page to 1, and trigger searchHelpArticles.
onMaturityFilterChange: When a maturity level is selected or cleared, set or remove the maturityLevel URL parameter, reset page to 1, and trigger searchHelpArticles.
onClearAllFiltersClick: Remove topicCodes, deviceCodes, planCodes, minRating, and maturityLevel from the URL, reset page to 1, and trigger searchHelpArticles.
onURLSync: When URL parameters change due to back/forward navigation or other components, update the selected state of all filters to match the URL without re-calling getHelpSearchFilterOptions.

components.functionality: Provides a control (e.g., dropdown) to select the sort order for search results using sortOptions returned by getHelpSearchFilterOptions and synchronized with the sortBy URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInit: After getHelpSearchFilterOptions resolves, bind sortOptions to the control and select the option that matches the sortBy URL parameter or a default from the options list if sortBy is not set.
onSortChange: When the user selects a different sort option, update sortBy in the URL, reset page to 1, and trigger a new searchHelpArticles call.
onURLChange: When sortBy in the URL changes (via back/forward navigation or programmatic updates), update the selected sort option in the control without calling getHelpSearchFilterOptions again.

components.functionality: Displays the current filters (topics, devices, plans, rating, maturity) as removable chips, derived from URL parameters and the resolved labels from getHelpSearchFilterOptions.
event_handlers: onInitOrSearchResponse: Build a list of applied filters by combining URL parameters (topicCodes, deviceCodes, planCodes, minRating, maturityLevel) with their corresponding names/labels from getHelpSearchFilterOptions.
onRemoveChip: When a chip is removed, update the corresponding URL parameter (remove just that value or clear the parameter if no values remain), reset page to 1, and trigger searchHelpArticles.
onClearAllClick: Optionally mirror the filters-sidebar clear-all behavior by removing all filter-related URL parameters, resetting page to 1, and triggering searchHelpArticles.
onURLChange: Recompute the set of chips whenever URL parameters change (e.g., via back/forward navigation).

components.functionality: Renders the list of article search results using the results array from searchHelpArticles, showing article metadata and linking each item to its article.html page.
event_handlers: onSearchResponse: When searchHelpArticles returns, map each result object to a result item using articleId, title, summary, categoryName, topicNames, deviceNames, planCodes, averageRating, ratingCount, and lastUpdatedAt. Render all items or show an empty state if the array is empty.
onResultClick: When a user clicks a result item (e.g., title or summary), navigate to article.html with articleId set to the selected item’s articleId and lang set to the current UI language code.
onURLChange: When URL parameters change and a new searchHelpArticles response is received, clear the old results and render the new results array.

components.functionality: Handles the case where searchHelpArticles returns no results, showing context (current query and filters) and encouraging adjustments without hardcoded suggestions that conflict with dynamic data.
event_handlers: onSearchResponse: If totalResults is zero, display an empty-state message that references queryText and appliedFilters from the searchHelpArticles response while hiding the normal results list.

onFilterChangeOrQueryChange: When filters or query change and a subsequent searchHelpArticles call returns non-zero results, hide the empty-state panel and allow the results-list component to render items.

components.functionality: Provides controls to move between pages of results and optionally adjust how many results are shown per page, based on the page, pageSize, and totalResults values from searchHelpArticles.
event_handlers: onSearchResponse: Read page, pageSize, and totalResults from the response, compute total pages, and render pagination buttons and page-size selection options.
onPageChange: When the user selects a different page, update the page URL parameter, keep all other parameters unchanged, and trigger a new searchHelpArticles call.
onPageSizeChange: When the user selects a different page size, update pageSize in the URL, reset page to 1, and trigger searchHelpArticles.
onURLChange: Adjust the active page and page-size UI elements to reflect the latest page and pageSize values from the URL and the most recent searchHelpArticles response.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL parameter parsing, API calls (getHelpSearchFilterOptions and searchHelpArticles), and synchronization between components so that the whole page behaves as a single stateful search experience driven by the URL.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse URL parameters (query, topicCodes, deviceCodes, planCodes, minRating, maturityLevel, sortBy, page, pageSize); call getHelpSearchFilterOptions to populate filters; then call searchHelpArticles with values derived from the URL.
onURLChange: Whenever the browser URL changes (including via back/forward navigation), re-parse URL parameters and trigger searchHelpArticles if any relevant search parameter has changed.
onFiltersInitialized: After getHelpSearchFilterOptions returns, notify filter-related components to bind their options and select values matching current URL parameters.

onSearchParametersChange: When any component requests a change to search parameters (query, filters, sort, page, pageSize), normalize and apply those changes to URL parameters, then trigger a new searchHelpArticles call.

onSearchResponse: Distribute the searchHelpArticles response to dependent components (search-results-header, applied-filters-chips, results-list, results-empty-state, pagination-controls) so they can update their views.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a centered main content column. The article list appears to use two equal-width columns (approximately 6 + 6) with a central gutter.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing full-width dark navigation bar, the page uses a single centered content column on a dark background. At the very top of this column sits a prominent, wide search bar that mirrors the mockup’s hero search treatment. Immediately beneath it, a concise results summary anchors the beginning of the results area, followed by a horizontal strip of applied filter chips. Below that, the layout transitions into a two-column region: a narrower filters sidebar on the left and a wider results area on the right. Within the results area, the sort control appears at the top, with the results list (or empty state) flowing vertically beneath it, and pagination controls at the very bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing full-width dark navigation bar, the page uses a single centered content column on a dark background. At the very top of this column sits a prominent, wide search bar that mirrors the mockup’s hero search treatment. Immediately beneath it, a concise results summary anchors the beginning of the results area, followed by a horizontal strip of applied filter chips. Below that, the layout transitions into a two-column region: a narrower filters sidebar on the left and a wider results area on the right. Within the results area, the sort control appears at the top, with the results list (or empty state) flowing vertically beneath it, and pagination controls at the very bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The navigation retains a modest vertical footprint (~8–10% of viewport height). The search-query-bar section is given generous vertical padding (~20% of viewport height total including top and bottom spacing below the nav) so it still feels like the primary interactive focal point. The results summary and applied filters chips occupy a compact vertical band. The two-column filters + results block uses the remaining vertical space, extending as long as necessary for the results list. Horizontally, all content is constrained to a centered max-width column (roughly 1040–1200px) with generous left and right margins from the viewport edges. Within this column, the filters sidebar occupies about 1/4–1/3 of the available width, and the results area uses the remaining 2/3–3/4, echoing the mockup’s dual-column content feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density remains moderately spacious: elements are clearly separated with 24–32px vertical gaps between major sections (search bar, summary, chips, two-column region) and 16–24px spacing between related items (individual results, filter groups). The wide margins around the main content and a comfortable gutter between the filters and results keep the layout from feeling cramped, even when numerous results or chips are present. Text-heavy areas prioritize legibility with ample line height, mirroring the mockup’s scannable, content-first aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye starts at the top with the brand in the nav, then is pulled down and inward to the large, centered search bar. From there, the gaze naturally drops to the left-aligned results summary and applied filters strip. The eye then moves slightly down and left to the filters sidebar header, then across to the sort control at the top of the results column, and finally travels down through the vertical stack of results before reaching the pagination at the bottom. The flow is predominantly top-to-bottom with a subtle left-to-right sweep across the two-column region, consistent with the mockup’s strong vertical hierarchy and left-to-right reading pattern.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a full-bleed dark background with a centered main content column framed by generous side margins, echoing a premium streaming experience. A slim dark navigation bar runs full-width across the top, and immediately beneath it, a wide, horizontally centered search bar dominates the upper portion of the page, spanning roughly 60–70% of the content width. This search field is visually heavy enough to feel like a hero element, with generous top and bottom padding so it sits in a comfortable block of whitespace. Just below, left-aligned within the same central column, a compact line of text summarizes the search results (for example, “24 results for ‘Playback issues’”), followed by a horizontal band of filter chips that make the current filtering state easy to scan. Dropping further down, the layout splits into two columns: on the left, a vertically stacked filters sidebar that occupies about a quarter of the main content width, and on the right, a wider results area filling the remaining space. The filters panel hugs the left edge of the content column, while the results area aligns to the right, separated by a clear gutter that preserves the airy, readable feel. At the top of the right column, a subtle sort control aligns with the first filter section to create a clean horizontal baseline. Beneath it, a vertical list of results stretches downward, each item separated by consistent vertical spacing, with typography that stands out crisply against the dark backdrop. If there are no results, a centered empty-state panel in the right column replaces the list but preserves the same structural boundaries. At the very bottom of the results column, pagination controls are centered horizontally, providing a clear endpoint to the page’s vertical journey. Overall, the layout feels structured, minimal, and content-centric, with a strong top-to-bottom narrative and a left-focused spine for filters that supports efficient exploration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-query-bar is positioned directly below the global navigation, centered within the main content column. It sits in a visually prominent block that occupies roughly the upper 20% of the viewport height (including spacing above and below), echoing the mockup’s dominant search area. Horizontally, the input field spans about 60–70% of the content column width (capped at around 640–720px), leaving equal dark margins on both sides for a framed look. The field itself is a single-line, high-contrast input with a search icon embedded on the right side inside the field, maintaining the minimalist, flat style. There is generous padding inside the input (12–16px vertical, 20–24px horizontal) and about 48–64px of space between the bottom of the navigation bar and the top of the search bar, with another 24–32px gap below it before the search-results-header, preserving its status as the primary interaction focus.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow; anchored at the top of the main content column and scrolls away with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-results-header sits immediately beneath the search-query-bar, sharing the same central column alignment but shifting the eye from center to left. The text is left-aligned and spans roughly the same width as the search bar (about 60–70% of the content width), though it does not need to be visually bounded by a container. It appears as a single line or two lines of text indicating the number of results and the query, placed about 16–24px below the bottom edge of the search bar. There is a modest vertical margin (8–16px) below this header before the applied-filters-chips begin, preserving the clear hierarchy without consuming excessive vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, positioned in normal flow directly under the search bar; it moves with page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filters-sidebar begins roughly 24–32px below the applied-filters-chips and anchors the left side of the main two-column region. Within the centered content column, it occupies about 25–30% of the available width, approximately equivalent to 3–4 columns in a 12-column grid, leaving the remaining 70–75% for the results area. The sidebar extends vertically from the top of this region downward, containing stacked filter groups (e.g., Topic, Device, Plan) separated by 16–24px vertical gaps. Each group uses compact but readable spacing: group titles are left-aligned with 8–12px of spacing above their options. The sidebar aligns its top edge horizontally with the top of the sort-control in the right column, establishing a clear visual row. Internally, the content remains relatively airy to avoid visual clutter, but denser than the hero search block. The left edge of the sidebar aligns with the left edge of the content column, with a consistent gutter (roughly 32–40px) separating it from the results area to the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger viewports, the filters-sidebar can use a sticky behavior, remaining fixed relative to the viewport once scrolled past its initial position (e.g., pinned a short distance below the nav), so that filters stay accessible while results scroll. It remains within the bounds of the main content column and scrolls normally on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-control is positioned at the top of the results column, horizontally aligned with the top of the filters-sidebar. Within the right column, it sits flush to the right edge of the main content width, creating a top-right anchor that balances the sidebar on the left. The control itself is compact—such as a dropdown or segmented toggle—occupying roughly 15–20% of the right column width. There is a small horizontal buffer (8–16px) between the control and any text to its left (e.g., a subtle label like “Sort by”). Vertically, it has 8–12px of internal padding and sits about 0–8px below the baseline shared with the top of the first filter group, so the top of the two-column block reads as a single horizontal band: filters heading on the left, sort on the right. A 16–24px gap separates the bottom of the sort-control from the first item in the results-list or the results-empty-state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the results column; it scrolls away with the results content and does not stick independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied-filters-chips bar sits between the search-results-header and the two-column region. It spans the full width of the centered content column but is visually focused on the left, starting directly under the results summary text. Individual chips are arranged in a horizontal row that wraps to multiple lines if necessary, with 8–12px horizontal gaps and 8px vertical gaps between rows. The bar has 8–12px of vertical padding above and below the chips to clearly separate it from adjacent elements, within a total band height typically around 40–80px depending on how many chips are active. There is approximately 12–16px of space above it from the results header and 24–32px of space below it before the filters-sidebar and results/sort row begins, reinforcing its role as a transitional state indicator between the query and the filtered results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it occupies a fixed horizontal band in the normal flow and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-list occupies the main body of the right column under the sort-control. It begins approximately 16–24px below the sort-control and stretches downward, filling the width allocated to the results area (about 70–75% of the central content column, aligned to the right). Each result item is a horizontally full-width row within this column, left-aligned and text-first, with 16–24px of vertical spacing between items and 16–24px of internal padding if they are card-like. The list maintains a clean vertical rhythm: titles stand out in bold white, with any metadata or snippets in smaller, dimmer text directly beneath. The list can extend beyond the viewport height, forming the primary scrollable region of the page. The left edge of each result aligns vertically with the left edge of the sort-control’s label, maintaining a clean vertical axis, while the right edge aligns with the overall content column’s right margin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, forming the main scrollable content. Its height is flexible and grows with the number of results, pushing the pagination-controls downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-empty-state appears in the same spatial slot as the results-list, within the right column beneath the sort-control, but only when there are no results. It is centered horizontally in the results area and occupies a moderate vertical rectangle near the upper part of that column rather than the exact center of the viewport, keeping the overall flow consistent. The top of the empty-state panel sits roughly 24–32px below the sort-control. Inside the panel, a concise headline, subtext, and potentially one or two suggested actions are stacked with 12–16px vertical spacing. The panel’s width is narrower than the full column (around 60–70% of the right column width) to create a sense of focus, with dark-toned borders or subtle background differentiation to stand out against the page. A generous vertical buffer (32–48px) is left below the empty-state content before the pagination-controls, even if the panel is short, so the bottom of the page does not feel abruptly crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the results-list; it replaces the list in the same space when there are zero results. It scrolls normally with the page, but in most cases will fit within a single viewport height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls sit at the bottom of the right column, directly under the last result in the results-list or beneath the results-empty-state when present. They are horizontally centered within the right column, creating a visual anchor at the end of the scroll. There is a clear vertical separation from the content above—24–32px of space from the last result item or the bottom of the empty-state panel. The controls themselves form a compact horizontal row of page numbers and navigation arrows with 8–12px gaps between elements, using subtle borders and high-contrast text against the dark background. The entire pagination region occupies only a small vertical band (roughly 40–64px) but is visually distinct enough to signal the end of the current results page without feeling heavy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the right column; it is pushed downward as the list grows and scrolls out of view like any other element.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-state-controller is a non-visual, structural component that does not have a direct on-screen presence. It conceptually sits behind the visible layout, orchestrating the interaction between the search-query-bar, filters-sidebar, applied-filters-chips, sort-control, results-list, results-empty-state, and pagination-controls. It occupies no spatial area in the layout: it neither consumes width nor height and does not alter alignment. Its effect is observed only through state changes—updating counts in the search-results-header, toggling chips, switching between results-list and results-empty-state, and refreshing pagination based on user inputs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; exists purely in the interaction/state layer without affecting physical layout. It underpins dynamic behavior while preserving the static spatial relationships of visible components.

name: Category Landing
title: Help Category
description: Displays a single help category with its subcategories and featured articles, and lets users navigate to detailed help content.

core_features: Read the categoryId URL parameter and use it as the categoryCode argument to call getCategoryLandingData to load all data for the selected category.
Display the category name and description returned by getCategoryLandingData to provide context for the current help category.
Render a breadcrumb trail based on the breadcrumb array from getCategoryLandingData, allowing navigation back to the Help Center home or other levels.
List all subcategories returned in the subcategories array, with each subcategory item linking to subcategory.html using its subcategoryId as a URL parameter.
Display featured help articles from the featuredArticles array as clickable items that navigate to article.html with articleId and the current UI language code passed via URL parameters.
Handle loading and error states tied to the getCategoryLandingData call so that the page only renders category content after data has been successfully fetched.
Update all rendered content when the categoryId URL parameter changes (for example, via header navigation) by re-calling getCategoryLandingData with the new categoryCode.
user_workflows: View a help category: (1) User arrives on category.html with a categoryId query parameter in the URL. (2) The page initialization logic reads categoryId and calls getCategoryLandingData with this value as categoryCode. (3) While the request is pending, the page shows a loading state for the main content. (4) After a successful response, the category header displays the returned categoryName and description, the breadcrumb displays the breadcrumb trail, the subcategory list shows all subcategories, and the featured article list shows the featuredArticles for that category.

Navigate to a subcategory: (1) User scans the subcategory list rendered from getCategoryLandingData.subcategories. (2) User clicks a subcategory item. (3) The click handler reads the subcategoryId associated with the clicked item. (4) The browser navigates to subcategory.html with subcategoryId passed as a URL parameter (e.g., subcategory.html?subcategoryId={id}).

Open a featured article: (1) User reviews the featuredArticles list rendered on the page. (2) User clicks a specific featured article item. (3) The click handler reads the articleId from the clicked item and the current UI language code from shared application state or URL. (4) The browser navigates to article.html passing articleId and lang as URL parameters (e.g., article.html?articleId={id}&lang={langCode}).

Return to Help Center home via breadcrumb: (1) User sees the breadcrumb trail populated from getCategoryLandingData.breadcrumb. (2) User clicks the breadcrumb item representing the Help Center home (identified by its pageType value). (3) The click handler uses the breadcrumb item’s pageType to construct the appropriate destination URL (e.g., index.html). (4) The browser navigates to the Help Center home without additional data calls from this page.

Switch category using global navigation: (1) User clicks a category link in the header that points to category.html with a different categoryId query parameter. (2) The browser loads category.html with the new categoryId. (3) On load, the page initialization logic reads the new categoryId and calls getCategoryLandingData again. (4) The page re-renders the category header, subcategory list, featured articles, and breadcrumb based on the new response.

Handle invalid or missing categoryId: (1) User arrives on category.html without a categoryId or with a categoryId that results in an error from getCategoryLandingData. (2) The page initialization detects the missing/invalid parameter or a failed API response. (3) The page either shows an error state with an option to go back to the Help Center home or redirects directly to the Help Center home URL. (4) No subcategory or featured article lists are rendered while in this error state.
interactions: On initial page load or when the categoryId URL parameter changes, the page invokes getCategoryLandingData with categoryCode set to the categoryId value from the URL, then updates all bound components upon success.

Clicking a breadcrumb item triggers navigation to another page based on that breadcrumb’s pageType and label; for example, a home-level breadcrumb navigates to the Help Center home, and a category-level breadcrumb navigates to the corresponding category.html with the appropriate categoryId.
Clicking a subcategory in the subcategory list triggers navigation to subcategory.html with the clicked subcategory’s subcategoryId passed via the subcategoryId URL parameter.
Clicking a featured article item triggers navigation to article.html with the corresponding articleId and the current UI language code (lang) included as URL parameters.

If getCategoryLandingData returns an empty subcategories array, the subcategory list component displays an appropriate empty-state message or hides the list, based on product rules, without causing errors elsewhere on the page.
If getCategoryLandingData returns an empty featuredArticles array, the featured-article list component displays an empty state or hides the section, ensuring the page still functions correctly.

If the getCategoryLandingData call fails (for example, due to network or server errors), the page displays an error state and may include a link or button to navigate back to the Help Center home; no subcategories or articles are rendered until a successful retry or navigation away.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is derived from the categoryId URL query parameter. On page initialization, the script reads categoryId from the current URL and uses that value as the categoryCode argument to getCategoryLandingData. The response from getCategoryLandingData populates in-memory state objects: category (categoryCode, categoryName, description), subcategories (array of subcategory objects), featuredArticles (array of article objects), and breadcrumb (array of breadcrumb items). The rendering of header content, breadcrumb, subcategory list, and featured article list is conditional on this state. When the URL changes to a different categoryId (for example, via header navigation or direct linking), the page re-runs this initialization logic, calls getCategoryLandingData again with the new categoryCode, and replaces the in-memory state, causing all dependent components to re-render. No persistent client-side storage such as localStorage is used; all state is refreshed based solely on the current URL parameters and the latest getCategoryLandingData response. For outgoing navigation, the page constructs links to subcategory.html and article.html using IDs from the in-memory state and appends them as query parameters (subcategoryId or articleId, and lang for articles) to the destination URLs.

components.functionality: Initializes the category landing page by reading the categoryId URL parameter, invoking getCategoryLandingData with that value as categoryCode, and distributing the resulting data to other components while managing loading and error states.
event_handlers: onPageLoad
onUrlChange
onDataLoadSuccess
onDataLoadError

components.functionality: Displays the current help category’s name and description so users know which area of help content they are viewing.
Renders the breadcrumb trail for the current category and allows users to navigate back to higher-level pages (such as the Help Center home or previous category levels) by clicking breadcrumb items.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a centered main content column. The article list appears to use two equal-width columns (approximately 6 + 6) with a central gutter.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strictly top-to-bottom, centered layout on a full-width dark background. A global horizontal top navigation (outside this spec) sits at the very top. Directly beneath it, the category-level hero band replaces the mockup’s search module: it contains the breadcrumb and a large category title (with optional short description). Below this, the main content is divided into two stacked sections: first a full-width 'Featured Articles' block that mirrors the mockup’s 'Most Popular Articles' section (heading + two-column list), then a slightly less prominent 'Subcategories' block, also running full-width within the main content column. Loading and error states temporarily take over the central column area where the header and lists would normally appear.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strictly top-to-bottom, centered layout on a full-width dark background. A global horizontal top navigation (outside this spec) sits at the very top. Directly beneath it, the category-level hero band replaces the mockup’s search module: it contains the breadcrumb and a large category title (with optional short description). Below this, the main content is divided into two stacked sections: first a full-width 'Featured Articles' block that mirrors the mockup’s 'Most Popular Articles' section (heading + two-column list), then a slightly less prominent 'Subcategories' block, also running full-width within the main content column. Loading and error states temporarily take over the central column area where the header and lists would normally appear.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top navigation consumes a modest vertical slice (~8–10% of the viewport height). Below it, the category header area acts like a hero: a tall band with generous vertical padding top and bottom (roughly 20–25% of the visible page height on first load), making the category name the main focal point. The 'Featured Articles' section occupies the next major block with a clear top margin (about 32–48px) from the header and ample breathing room around its heading and two-column list. The 'Subcategories' section follows with similar but slightly reduced vertical padding to signal secondary importance. Horizontally, all components live inside a centered content column that spans about 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop, capped at a fixed max-width (e.g., ~1120px), leaving strong left/right gutters of dark background.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious density. The header band is open and airy, with clear separation between breadcrumb, main title, and description. Within the 'Featured Articles' and 'Subcategories' sections, items are stacked with regular vertical spacing (around 16–24px between rows) to avoid crowding. The two-column article layout increases information per viewport while preserving generous gutters between columns and between the text and the viewport edges. There are distinct vertical breaks (32–48px) between major sections to keep the page scannable.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow remains strongly vertical with a left-to-right reading pattern. The eye begins at the top-left with the global brand/nav, moves down to the breadcrumb, then immediately to the large, central category title as the hero focal point. From there, attention flows down to any short category description, then to the left-aligned 'Featured Articles' heading. The reader scans down the left column of featured articles first, then shifts right to the second column, following a Z-like motion within the section. Finally, the eye moves further down to the 'Subcategories' heading and its list. When loading or error states are present, they replace the middle of this flow and become the central focus in the main content column.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a full-width dark canvas with a clean, horizontal top navigation spanning edge to edge. Centered beneath this is a main content column, relatively narrow compared to the full viewport, giving the layout a framed, cinematic feel. The first major block in this column is a tall category header band: a subtle breadcrumb sits near the top-left of the column, followed by a large, high-contrast category title that visually anchors the page. A brief description line may sit directly under the title, centered and constrained to a comfortable measure. Below this hero area, a generous vertical gap leads into a 'Featured Articles' section: a bold, left-aligned heading at the start of the content column introduces a two-column grid of text-first article links, spaced evenly with a wide central gutter and ample line spacing. A thin, understated divider may close this section. Further down, the 'Subcategories' section continues the same centered-column logic but with slightly less visual weight, presenting a list of subcategory links that is easy to scan and clearly separated from the featured articles above. Throughout, there are no heavy boxes or illustrations—just crisp white type on black, subtle separators, and a strong vertical rhythm. When the page is loading or encounters an error, a compact, centered state sits within the main content column, replacing the header and lists so that the user’s attention is immediately drawn to what’s happening.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component defines the full-page canvas and central column structure. It occupies the entire viewport width and height with a solid dark background. Within it, a single main content column is centered horizontally using auto side margins and constrained to roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, with a fixed maximum width (around 1040–1120px) to emulate a 12-column grid. All other components (breadcrumb, category header, lists, states) stack vertically inside this column in document order. There is sufficient top padding beneath the global navigation (approximately 24–32px) before the breadcrumb appears, and consistent bottom padding (32–48px) at the end of the page for breathing room. The central column maintains generous left and right gutters on larger screens and reduces gracefully on smaller screens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the primary structural container in normal document flow; not fixed or sticky. It simply hosts the centered content column and vertical stacking of all child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb is positioned at the top of the main content column, just below the global navigation and within the same visual band as the category header. It is left-aligned to the content column’s left edge and spans only as wide as its text requires, keeping a low profile. Vertically, it sits with modest top padding from the nav (around 16–24px) and a small but clear space beneath it (about 16–24px) before the category title. The breadcrumb’s compact size and subtle styling mean it does not interrupt the visual flow but quietly orients the user before their eye moves down to the much larger category title centered below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the content flow, scrolling away with the page. It does not stick to the top; it simply anchors the top of the category header band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category header shares a visual band with the breadcrumb but dominates the vertical space beneath it, functioning like a hero section. It stretches the full width of the main content column.
