The category title is the focal point: set in large, high-contrast type, it is horizontally centered within the column, with generous top padding above it (roughly 24–32px below the breadcrumb) and bottom padding below (around 32–48px). If a category description or tagline exists, it appears directly under the title, also centered, in a narrower measure and slightly smaller type, echoing the mockup’s centered helper text under the search bar. Overall, this header block occupies roughly 20–25% of the initial viewport height, creating a broad, calm area before the denser lists that follow. The surrounding dark background remains uninterrupted, with no heavy borders—just text and subtle spacing defining the section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the normal vertical flow, extending the main content column downward. It is not sticky; its role is to establish the category context at the top of the scrollable content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured article list sits directly below the category header, separated by a substantial vertical gap (approximately 32–48px) that clearly marks a new section. At the top of this block, a section heading such as 'Featured Articles' is left-aligned with the content column’s left edge, spanning the full width of the column but occupying only one or two lines. Beneath the heading, after a modest gap (about 16–24px), the articles are arranged in two equal-width columns, similar to the mockup’s 'Most Popular Articles' layout. Each column takes roughly 50% of the content column width minus a generous central gutter (around 32–40px). Article entries are primarily text links: each one is left-aligned within its column and stacked vertically with consistent spacing (16–24px) between them. The left column is visually encountered first, then the eye moves to the right column, mirroring the mockup’s reading pattern. The entire section respects the main side gutters, with no boxes or heavy cards—just clean typography and subtle hover states if interactive. A thin, low-contrast horizontal separator line may run along the bottom of this block, aligning with the content column width, to gently delineate it from whatever follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content column, expanding vertically as more featured articles are present. On narrower screens, the two columns can naturally collapse into a single column while maintaining similar vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subcategory list appears below the featured articles section as a distinct but slightly less dominant block. It begins after a clear vertical separation (around 32–40px) from the bottom of the featured articles (or its separator line). At the top of this block, a left-aligned label or heading such as 'Subcategories' anchors the section, aligned with the content column’s left edge. Below this heading, after a small gap (16–20px), the subcategories are presented as a structured list. On desktop, they may be arranged in one or two narrow columns depending on quantity, but they occupy less vertical emphasis than the featured articles—e.g., smaller type or tighter vertical spacing (around 12–20px between rows). Each subcategory appears as a text link or minimalist row, left-aligned and free of heavy card borders, maintaining the flat, utilitarian style. The section uses similar left/right margins as the rest of the column, and concludes with comfortable bottom padding (32–48px) before the end of the page, giving the layout a clean, finished feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow, expanding or contracting based on the number of subcategories. It does not affect the position of the header or featured section beyond adding vertical length.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading state temporarily replaces the main content sections (breadcrumb, header, featured articles, and subcategories) in the central column while data is being fetched. Visually, it occupies the same horizontal space as the main content column, centered on the page, but uses minimal elements: a spinner or simple animated indicator and a short 'Loading' message beneath it. This cluster is centered horizontally and positioned roughly in the middle of the visible area beneath the global navigation, with ample dark space surrounding it (at least 64px padding on all sides within the column) to keep the focus on the indicator. The rest of the page remains uncluttered, with no article lists visible during loading, emphasizing the transient nature of this state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally displayed and effectively replaces other components within the main content column. It behaves as a centered block in normal flow, but other content is hidden or not rendered while this state is active. It scrolls with the page, though typically the content height is small enough that no scrolling is needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state also occupies the main content column in place of the usual header and lists when an error occurs. Horizontally, it is centered within the same column bounds. Vertically, it appears just below the global navigation, similar to where the category header would begin, with comfortable top padding (about 48–64px) to feel intentional but still near the top of the viewport. The error message is presented as concise text in high-contrast type, potentially with a brief explanation line beneath and an action button (e.g., 'Retry') stacked under it. These elements form a compact vertical cluster aligned to the left of the column or center-aligned depending on tone, but with generous surrounding negative space so they stand out clearly against the dark background. No other content sections are shown while this state is active, keeping attention on resolving the issue.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the normal content stack within the main content column. It is part of the standard flow, not fixed, but generally small enough to fit within the initial viewport with minimal or no scrolling. When active, other components are hidden or not rendered to avoid confusion.

name: Subcategory / Article List
title: Help Articles
description: Displays help articles for a selected subcategory or device topic, with internal search, filters, sorting, pagination, and navigation to article details.

core_features: Determine page mode (subcategory-based or device-topic-based) from URL parameters and call the appropriate interface (getSubcategoryArticleList or getDeviceTopicArticleList).
Display contextual header information (subcategory name and parent category, or device name and topic name) from the interface response.
Render a breadcrumb trail from the interface response to let users navigate back to the parent category or device help page.
Provide an internal search field to search within the current subcategory’s articles, bound to getSubcategoryArticleList.searchText.

Provide filters for minimum rating and maturity level (where available), bound to getSubcategoryArticleList.minRating, getSubcategoryArticleList.maturityLevel, and getDeviceTopicArticleList.minRating.
Provide sorting controls bound to getSubcategoryArticleList.sortBy and getDeviceTopicArticleList.sortBy.
Display a list of articles with title, summary, rating, rating count, last updated date, and applicable maturity levels (when provided).

Handle pagination for subcategory lists using getSubcategoryArticleList.page, getSubcategoryArticleList.pageSize, and getSubcategoryArticleList.totalResults, and for device-topic lists using getDeviceTopicArticleList.page and getDeviceTopicArticleList.pageSize parameters.
Navigate to the article detail page (article.html) when an article is selected, passing articleId and current language (lang) as URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Browse articles in a subcategory:
1. User arrives from category.html with subcategory.html?subcategoryId=...
2. Page-init logic detects subcategoryId and calls getSubcategoryArticleList with URL parameters (subcategoryId, searchText, minRating, maturityLevel, sortBy, page, pageSize).
3. The page displays the subcategory title, description, breadcrumb, and article list.
4. User scans the list, optionally adjusts filters or sort order.
5. User clicks an article title; browser navigates to article.html?articleId=...&lang=... .

Search within a subcategory:
1. User is on a subcategory view (subcategoryId present, no deviceCode/topicCode).
2. User types a query into the internal search field and submits.
3. The search handler updates the URL’s searchText parameter and resets page to 1.
4. Page-init logic (or a reload) calls getSubcategoryArticleList with the updated searchText and other current filter/sort URL parameters.
5. The article list updates to show only matching articles; appliedFilters.searchText is reflected in the UI.

Filter by rating and maturity in a subcategory:
1. User opens the filter controls.
2. User selects a rating bucket option and, if relevant, a maturity level option based on availableFilterOptions from getSubcategoryArticleList.
3. The filter handler updates the minRating and maturityLevel URL parameters, resets page to 1, and pushes the new URL state.
4. Page-init logic re-calls getSubcategoryArticleList; the updated article list and appliedFilters are rendered.
5. User can clear filters, which removes the relevant URL parameters and reloads the list.

Sort articles in a subcategory:
1. User opens the sort control and selects a sort option from availableFilterOptions.sortOptions.
2. The sort handler updates the sortBy URL parameter and resets page to 1.
3. Page-init logic re-calls getSubcategoryArticleList with the new sortBy value.
4. The article list re-renders in the new order.

Paginate through subcategory results:
1. User is on a subcategory view with more results than fit on the current pageSize.
2. User clicks a pagination control (Next or a specific page number).
3. The pagination handler updates the page URL parameter.
4. Page-init logic calls getSubcategoryArticleList with the new page value.
5. The article list updates to show the selected page; pagination controls are recalculated using totalResults, page, and pageSize from the response.

Browse articles for a device-specific topic:
1. User arrives from device_help.html with subcategory.html?deviceCode=...&topicCode=....
2. Page-init logic detects deviceCode and topicCode and calls getDeviceTopicArticleList with those parameters plus minRating, sortBy, page, and pageSize from the URL.
3. The page shows header information for the device and topic, breadcrumb navigation, and the article list.
4. User optionally adjusts rating filter and sort order; handlers update corresponding URL parameters and re-call getDeviceTopicArticleList.
5. User clicks an article title to navigate to article.html?articleId=...&lang=....

Switch filters in device-topic mode:
1. In device-topic view, user adjusts the minimum rating filter control.
2. The handler updates minRating and resets page to 1 in the URL.
3. Page-init logic re-calls getDeviceTopicArticleList; the list updates according to the new filter.
4. User may also change sortBy, which follows the same URL-update and re-fetch pattern.

Handle direct deep links and sharing:
1. A user opens or shares a URL like subcategory.html?subcategoryId=...&searchText=...&minRating=...&maturityLevel=...&sortBy=...&page=....
2. Page-init logic parses all URL parameters and calls getSubcategoryArticleList with them.
3. The page renders the exact filtered, sorted, and paginated article list as encoded in the URL, enabling reproducible views without relying on local storage.
interactions: On page load or on URL change, determine whether to call getSubcategoryArticleList or getDeviceTopicArticleList based on the presence of subcategoryId versus deviceCode+topicCode, then fetch and render the corresponding data.
On internal search form submit (subcategory mode only), prevent default form submission, update searchText and page in the URL, and trigger a fresh getSubcategoryArticleList call.
On rating filter change, update the minRating URL parameter and reset page to 1, then trigger the appropriate interface call (getSubcategoryArticleList or getDeviceTopicArticleList).
On maturity filter change (subcategory mode only), update maturityLevel in the URL and reset page to 1, then call getSubcategoryArticleList with the updated filters.
On sort option change, update sortBy in the URL, reset page to 1, and call getSubcategoryArticleList or getDeviceTopicArticleList accordingly.
On pagination control click in subcategory mode, update the page URL parameter and call getSubcategoryArticleList with the new page while preserving other filter and sort parameters.
On pagination control click in device-topic mode, update the page URL parameter and call getDeviceTopicArticleList with the new page and current filters/sort.

On breadcrumb item click, navigate to the target page type using its URL pattern and any identifiers available from the breadcrumb data; this causes a full context switch away from subcategory.html if the target is another page.
On article card or title click, navigate to article.html with articleId set to the clicked article’s ID and lang taken from the current UI language context.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s canonical state is encoded entirely in URL parameters. On every load or history navigation event: (1) The code parses URL parameters. (2) If both deviceCode and topicCode are present, the page enters device-topic mode and ignores subcategoryId for data fetching; it calls getDeviceTopicArticleList({ deviceCode, topicCode, minRating, sortBy, page, pageSize }). (3) Else if subcategoryId is present, the page enters subcategory mode and calls getSubcategoryArticleList({ subcategoryId, searchText, minRating, maturityLevel, sortBy, page, pageSize }). (4) If neither mode can be determined, the page may display an error state or navigate to a higher-level help page; this behavior is triggered by the absence of required mode-defining URL parameters. (5) The responses are not cached in localStorage; the page relies on re-reading the URL and re-calling the appropriate interface whenever filters, search, sort, or pagination change. (6) All user interactions that change filters, searchText, sortBy, page, or pageSize update the URL parameters using history.pushState or history.replaceState before re-invoking the relevant interface, ensuring that the back/forward browser buttons correctly traverse past states.

components.functionality: Determines whether the page should display a subcategory-based or device-topic-based article list based on URL parameters, invokes the appropriate interface, and exposes the resulting data to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse URL parameters (subcategoryId, deviceCode, topicCode, searchText, minRating, maturityLevel, sortBy, page, pageSize); decide mode; call getSubcategoryArticleList or getDeviceTopicArticleList with these parameters; store the response in in-memory state for rendering.
onHistoryChange: When the browser back/forward is used, re-parse URL parameters and re-call the appropriate interface to keep view in sync.

onModeSwitchRequest: If URL changes from subcategory mode to device-topic mode or vice versa, clear mode-specific filters that no longer apply (e.g., maturityLevel in device-topic mode) and trigger the corresponding interface call.

components.functionality: Displays the current context of the list: either subcategory name and its parent category name (subcategory mode) or device name and topic name (device-topic mode).

Renders breadcrumb links representing the navigation path (e.g., from Help Center home to Category to Subcategory, or from Devices to a specific Device help page to Topic), and handles navigation when a breadcrumb item is clicked.
event_handlers: onBreadcrumbClick: Read the clicked breadcrumb item’s pageType and any associated identifiers (derived from breadcrumb metadata or current URL); construct the target URL for that page type and navigate using window.location or a router, without altering the current article list in-place.

components.functionality: Allows users to search within the current subcategory’s articles by entering text; only active in subcategory mode.
event_handlers: onSearchInputChange: Update the search input field’s internal value but do not fetch yet (debounce logic may be added at implementation level).

onSearchSubmit: Prevent default form submission, set or update the searchText URL parameter, reset page to 1, update the browser history entry, and trigger page-init-controller to re-call getSubcategoryArticleList with the new searchText.
onClearSearch: Remove searchText from the URL, reset page to 1, and re-call getSubcategoryArticleList without a search filter.

components.functionality: Provides controls to filter the article list by minimum rating in both modes and by maturity level where supported (subcategory mode). Reads available filter options from the API where provided and updates URL state on user interaction.
event_handlers: onRatingBucketSelect (subcategory mode): When the user selects a rating bucket option derived from availableFilterOptions.ratingBuckets, update the minRating URL parameter to the selected bucket’s value, reset page to 1, push the new URL state, and re-call getSubcategoryArticleList.

onRatingMinChange (device-topic mode): When the user changes the rating filter control (e.g., a numeric input or slider), update the minRating URL parameter, reset page to 1, push the new URL, and re-call getDeviceTopicArticleList.

onMaturityLevelSelect (subcategory mode): When the user selects a maturity level from availableFilterOptions.maturityLevels, update the maturityLevel URL parameter, reset page to 1, push state, and re-call getSubcategoryArticleList.
onClearFilters: Remove filter-related URL parameters (minRating, maturityLevel where applicable), reset page to 1, and re-call the currently active interface.

components.functionality: Lets users select how the article list is ordered (e.g., by last updated date or by rating), and updates sorting parameters before re-fetching data.
event_handlers: onSortChange (subcategory mode): When the user selects a sort option from availableFilterOptions.sortOptions, update the sortBy URL parameter, reset page to 1, push the new URL state, and re-call getSubcategoryArticleList.

onSortChange (device-topic mode): When the user changes sort order using the available sort options in the UI (mapped to supported sortBy values), update the sortBy URL parameter, reset page to 1, push state, and re-call getDeviceTopicArticleList.

components.functionality: Displays the list of articles returned by the active interface, showing each article’s title, summary, rating data, last updated date, and applicable maturity levels when provided. Allows selection of an article to navigate to its detail page.
event_handlers: onArticleClick: Read the selected article’s articleId from the bound list item, read current UI language (lang) from application context, and navigate to article.html?articleId=...&lang=....
onArticleHover: Optional interaction to highlight the article; no data changes or interface calls are triggered.

components.functionality: Manages pagination for subcategory-based article lists using page, pageSize, and totalResults, allowing navigation between pages of results.
event_handlers: onPageSelect: When the user selects a specific page number, update the page URL parameter, push state, and re-call getSubcategoryArticleList with the new page while preserving searchText, minRating, maturityLevel, sortBy, and pageSize.
onNextPage: Increment the page URL parameter (if not already at the last page based on totalResults), push state, and re-call getSubcategoryArticleList.
onPreviousPage: Decrement the page URL parameter (if page > 1), push state, and re-call getSubcategoryArticleList.
onPageSizeChange: When the user selects a different pageSize, update the pageSize URL parameter, reset page to 1, push state, and re-call getSubcategoryArticleList.

components.functionality: Provides next/previous page navigation for device-topic article lists using the page and pageSize URL parameters, even though total result count is not returned.
event_handlers: onNextPage (device-topic mode): If the current list length equals the pageSize value from the URL, assume more pages may exist; increment the page URL parameter, push state, and re-call getDeviceTopicArticleList.
onPreviousPage (device-topic mode): If the current page value from the URL is greater than 1, decrement page, push state, and re-call getDeviceTopicArticleList.
onPageSizeChange (device-topic mode): Update pageSize in the URL, reset page to 1, push state, and re-call getDeviceTopicArticleList.

components.functionality: Displays a concise summary of the currently applied search, filter, and sort parameters, and allows quick removal of individual filters.
event_handlers: onRemoveSearchFilter: Remove searchText from the URL (subcategory mode only), reset page to 1, push state, and re-call getSubcategoryArticleList.
onRemoveRatingFilter: Remove minRating from the URL for the active mode, reset page to 1, push state, and re-call the relevant interface.
onRemoveMaturityFilter: Remove maturityLevel from the URL (subcategory mode), reset page to 1, push state, and re-call getSubcategoryArticleList.

onResetAll: Clear all filter/sort/search URL parameters while preserving the mode-defining parameter(s) (subcategoryId or deviceCode/topicCode), reset page and pageSize to defaults, and re-call the appropriate interface.

components.functionality: Shows informational messages when there are no articles for the current filters or when the mode cannot be determined due to missing URL parameters.
event_handlers: onRetryFetch: When a transient fetch error is detected by the hosting application, re-invoke the last called interface (getSubcategoryArticleList or getDeviceTopicArticleList) with the current URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a centered main content column. The article list appears to use two equal-width columns (approximately 6 + 6) with a central gutter.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strong top-to-bottom, centered layout on a full-width dark background. At the very top sits the contextual header band, immediately followed by a left-aligned breadcrumb trail. Below that, a large, centered search block dominates the upper-middle of the viewport. Under the search block, an applied-filters summary bar spans the main content width. The page then transitions into a two-column main content region: a narrower left filter sidebar and a wider right article/results column. Within the article column, a small control row (sort and optional meta info) sits above the article list. The articles themselves are displayed in two vertical text columns. Pagination controls sit at the bottom of this area, with an additional device-topic pagination row below. Empty or error states occupy the article-list region while preserving the rest of the structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strong top-to-bottom, centered layout on a full-width dark background. At the very top sits the contextual header band, immediately followed by a left-aligned breadcrumb trail. Below that, a large, centered search block dominates the upper-middle of the viewport. Under the search block, an applied-filters summary bar spans the main content width. The page then transitions into a two-column main content region: a narrower left filter sidebar and a wider right article/results column. Within the article column, a small control row (sort and optional meta info) sits above the article list. The articles themselves are displayed in two vertical text columns. Pagination controls sit at the bottom of this area, with an additional device-topic pagination row below. Empty or error states occupy the article-list region while preserving the rest of the structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the header context band and breadcrumb area together use roughly 8–12% of the viewport height. The search module below is given a generous 20–25% of the viewport height, with substantial padding above and below its input field to make it the primary focal point. The filters-summary bar is slim, taking minimal height but stretching full content width. The main content area (filters + articles) occupies the remaining vertical space, with clear separations (32–48px) between the search block, filters summary, and the results area. Horizontally, the main content is constrained to a centered max-width (around 1040–1200px), leaving generous side margins (about 10–12% of viewport width on each side). Within this, the filter sidebar takes approximately 25–30% of the content width, and the article column takes 70–75%. Inside the article column, the article list itself is split into two equal-width text columns with a wide central gutter.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate and optimized for readability. Major sections (header/breadcrumb, search, filters summary, results) are separated with 32–48px vertical gaps. Interactive elements such as the search input, filter chips, and pagination controls use 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. Article list items are spaced with 12–16px vertical separation so they are easy to scan without feeling sparse. The two-column article layout increases information density without crowding, thanks to adequate column gutter and side margins.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow begins at the top-left with the contextual header and breadcrumb, grounding the user in the help center hierarchy. The eye then moves downward and inward to the large, centrally aligned search bar, which acts as the primary interaction point. Immediately below, the applied-filters summary keeps users aware of their current context, leading the eye down into the main results zone. Within that zone, attention moves from left to right: first to the filter sidebar, then to the sort control at the top-right of the results column, and finally down into the two-column article list. Reading proceeds down the left article column and then to the right column, before reaching the pagination controls at the bottom. Empty or error states take over the article-list area but follow the same vertical flow, replacing the two-column list with a centered message while leaving header, search, filters, and sidebar intact.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a full-bleed dark background with a centered, max-width content column that feels similar to a streaming platform’s help overlay. At the top, a low-profile contextual header bar and breadcrumb subtly frame the experience without overpowering it. Below, a wide, horizontally centered search field dominates the upper portion of the screen, with generous breathing room above and below and a short explanatory line of text directly underneath, echoing the cinematic, content-first tone. Beneath this, a slim applied-filters bar runs across the main content width, followed by a two-column main region: a compact, vertically oriented filter sidebar on the left and a broader article/results area on the right. The results area begins with a quiet control row holding sort options, then opens into a two-column stack of article links styled like streamlined text lists. Each article title sits on its own line with comfortable vertical spacing, forming two balanced columns separated by a wide gutter, framed by broad side margins. At the bottom of the results column, pagination controls are centered and understated. When there are no results, that article region is replaced with a centered empty/error state while the rest of the layout remains stable. Overall, the composition feels structured, dark, and utilitarian, with a strong vertical reading path and a clear emphasis on search and scannable article content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is an invisible structural layer that wraps the entire page viewport. It establishes the full-width dark background, sets the centered max-width content container (approximately 1040–1200px wide), and defines the vertical stacking order of main sections: header context bar at the top, breadcrumb beneath, then the search block, followed by the applied filters bar, and finally the split main content area (filter sidebar + article column) with pagination at the bottom. It ensures consistent horizontal centering and the left/right margins that create the framed, cinematic feel of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a full-page container in normal document flow, anchoring all other components to a centered max-width layout on a full-width dark background.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header context bar appears as a low-height horizontal band at the very top of the page content, immediately beneath any global site navigation (if present). It spans the full browser width, but its content is constrained to the centered main column. Within this band, the subcategory or device topic name is left-aligned, occupying roughly the left half of the content width, with any optional contextual controls or tags on the right. Vertically, it has about 16–24px of padding above and below the text, making its total height roughly 64–80px (around 8–10% of the viewport height on typical desktop). The bar visually anchors the page but remains slim so that attention quickly moves downward to the search area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the page content; scrolls away with the page (not sticky by default), providing initial context without competing with the search module.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation sits directly below the header context bar, inside the same centered content width. It is left-aligned, forming a thin row of small, high-contrast text links that trace the path back to higher-level help categories. There is a modest vertical gap of about 8–12px below the header bar and another 16–24px below the breadcrumb before the search form begins. Horizontally, the breadcrumb occupies only the left portion of the content column, leaving ample empty space to the right, reinforcing the uncluttered, linear flow toward the central search area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in normal document flow, nested at the top of the content column between the header context bar and the search form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subcategory search form is the primary focal block in the upper-middle of the page. It is centered horizontally within the main content column and placed with generous top margin (about 32–48px below the breadcrumb). The search input itself is a single, wide text field spanning roughly 60–70% of the content width, with a max width of about 720–800px to keep line length comfortable. It sits visually centered in the viewport horizontally. The field has 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside, with a search icon embedded at the right end of the input. The entire search block, including its padding above and below, occupies roughly 20–25% of the viewport height, making it feel dominant and easy to target. Directly beneath the input, there is a brief explanatory line of helper text, center-aligned and constrained to the same width as the input, with about 8–12px of vertical spacing between the input and text. Below the helper text, a larger vertical gap (32–40px) separates this module from the applied filters summary bar, maintaining its own self-contained visual block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and central in the layout; remains in normal flow but designed to be visually dominant within the upper third of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied filters summary appears as a low-profile horizontal bar directly beneath the search block, spanning the full width of the centered content container but not the full browser width. It sits approximately 32–40px below the search helper text, visually bridging the search area and the results area. The bar is left-aligned within the content column: filter chips or labels begin at the left edge, with any clear/reset action aligned to the far right. Vertically, it is compact—about 32–40px tall overall—with 8–12px of vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside. When no filters are applied, the bar either collapses entirely or reduces to a very subtle, low-height row, preserving the whitespace and hierarchy while not drawing attention away from the search or articles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, sitting between the search module and the main two-column content area, expanding or collapsing in height based on the number of applied filters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter sidebar begins directly below the applied filters summary, forming the left column of the main content region. On desktop, it occupies roughly 25–30% of the centered content width (about 3–4 of 12 grid columns), starting flush with the left edge of the content container. Its top aligns horizontally with the top of the article results area on the right, creating a clean, unified row. The sidebar runs vertically alongside the article list, extending downward as needed. Inside, filter groups are stacked vertically with 24–32px separation, each group label left-aligned and filters (checkboxes, toggles, or pills) indented slightly. There is 16–24px horizontal padding from the sidebar’s inner edge to its content, and 16–20px vertical padding at the top to keep content from touching the applied-filters bar. The sidebar maintains ample breathing room so it feels like a utilitarian control rail, not a cramped panel. On narrow viewports, it collapses above the article column as a full-width, toggleable filter drawer, preserving the linear vertical flow established by the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, behaves like a left rail in normal flow but can become "sticky" within the viewport: once the user scrolls past the search block, the sidebar’s inner content may stick with a small top offset (e.g., 16–24px below the browser top), while the article list scrolls independently. On mobile, it stacks above the article column and may collapse into an accordion or modal filter panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control sits at the top of the right-hand article/results column, horizontally aligned with the top of the filter sidebar. The article column occupies about 70–75% of the content width (roughly 8–9 of 12 grid columns) and begins at the right edge of the sidebar with a clear gutter of 32–40px between them. Within this area, the sort control appears as a subtle row at the top-right: the label (e.g., "Sort by") and dropdown are right-aligned, with any result count or view options left-aligned in the same row. The row has 8–12px vertical padding and sits 8–16px above the article section heading or the first row of articles. The control is visually understated, using flat styling that aligns with the minimalist dark theme, so it supports but does not overshadow the article titles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the article column’s normal flow, pinned to the top of the results area. It scrolls away as the user moves down the article list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article list occupies the main portion of the right-hand article column, starting directly below the sort-control row with about 16–24px of vertical spacing. At its top, mirroring the mockup’s ‘MOST POPULAR ARTICLES’ pattern, there is a left-aligned section heading such as "Articles in this subcategory". This heading sits within the article column, aligned to its left edge, with a 16–24px gap beneath it before the list items begin. The list itself is arranged in two equal-width vertical columns of text-only links, similar to a streaming service’s text grid but purely typographic. Each column is approximately half the width of the article column, with a generous central gutter of about 32–40px. Article titles are set as single-line (or wrapped) links, left-aligned within each column; they are stacked vertically with 12–16px of space between each item. Users typically read down the left column first, then move to the right column, mirroring the flow in the mockup. The entire list is framed by the article column’s left and right edges, which themselves sit comfortably within the main content margins, maintaining a balanced, content-focused center area on the dark background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrollable in normal document flow within the article column. As the user scrolls, the list extends downward, with the two-column structure maintained down the page until pagination breaks the content into separate pages.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subcategory pagination controls appear directly below the article list, within the right-hand article column. There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px between the last row of article titles and the pagination row, clearly separating navigational controls from the content. The pagination is horizontally centered within the article column, which itself is centered in the main content, creating a visually balanced control band. Pagination elements (previous/next arrows, page numbers) are laid out in a single horizontal line, with 8–12px of horizontal spacing between items and 8–12px of vertical padding above and below the row. The styling is flat and minimal, using high-contrast text or numerals that remain secondary to the content but easy to find at the bottom of the list. This band occupies a small vertical footprint but clearly marks the end of the current article set.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, located at the bottom of the article list in normal flow. It moves downward as content grows and scrolls out of view as the user navigates up the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The device-topic pagination appears below the subcategory pagination, separated by a modest vertical gap of about 16–24px. It spans the width of the article column but is visually lighter and more contextual than the primary subcategory pagination. Layout-wise, it can present as a small horizontal set of controls (e.g., "Previous device topic" on the left and "Next device topic" on the right) or as a centered set of labeled buttons indicating adjacent device topics. The row is thinner than the main pagination, with about 8–12px of vertical padding. It remains aligned to the article column’s horizontal grid, so it feels like a continuation of the same content frame. This placement signals that these controls are a broader-level navigation option after a user reaches the end of the current subcategory’s articles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, following directly after subcategory pagination in normal flow. It scrolls with the rest of the content, appearing at the very bottom of the main article area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-or-error-state component occupies the same spatial region as the article list and its related controls within the right-hand article column. When activated (e.g., no articles match current filters or an error occurs), it replaces the article heading, two-column list, and pagination controls with a single, centralized message. The block begins at the same vertical position where the article heading would normally start—just below the sort-control row—and uses a generous amount of vertical padding (24–40px above and below the main message) to create a self-contained, easily noticeable panel. The content within this state (icon, title, description, and any action buttons) is center-aligned horizontally within the article column, with a max width similar to a single article column (about 50–60% of the article area) so text remains readable. The left filter sidebar, applied-filters summary, and search form remain visible and unchanged around it, reinforcing that users can adjust their search or filters to recover from the empty/error state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the article-list and pagination within the article column; it conditionally takes over that space in normal flow while keeping the overall page structure intact.

name: Devices
title: Devices
description: Page that lists all supported device types and routes users to device-specific help topics.

core_features: Fetch and display the list of supported devices using the getDevicesOverview interface.
Render each device as a clickable item that navigates to the Device Help Topics page for that device via a URL parameter.
Provide consistent navigation paths from header, footer, and in-page links to device-specific help flows.
user_workflows: 1) User opens Devices page -> 2) Page initializes and calls getDevicesOverview -> 3) Devices list is populated with the returned devices -> 4) User clicks a device item -> 5) Browser navigates to device_help.html with the selected deviceCode in the query string.

1) User returns to Devices page from a device_help.html page -> 2) Devices page loads and calls getDevicesOverview again (or uses already-loaded data, depending on implementation) -> 3) User can select another device to open a different device_help.html view.

1) User opens Devices page from header/footer navigation -> 2) Devices page loads and fetches device list via getDevicesOverview -> 3) User identifies their device and clicks it -> 4) User is taken directly into device-specific topics (e.g., subtitles, streaming issues) on device_help.html for that device.
interactions: On page load, a data-loading interaction triggers getDevicesOverview with no parameters and transitions the page through loading, success, or error states.
On device item click, the page reads the deviceCode from the clicked item’s bound data and performs a client-side navigation to device_help.html, appending ?deviceCode=<deviceCode> to the URL.
On error state retry click, the page re-triggers the getDevicesOverview call to attempt loading the list of devices again.

On breadcrumb or navigation link click to Devices, the browser navigates to devices.html without additional parameters, causing a fresh evaluation of the page and, if implemented, a new getDevicesOverview call.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Devices page does not require any incoming URL parameters to function. On every initial load of devices.html (regardless of any extraneous query parameters present), the page invokes getDevicesOverview to obtain the current list of supported devices and renders them. Each rendered device item exposes its deviceCode as part of its bound data; clicking the item constructs a URL for device_help.html using a query parameter, for example: device_help.html?deviceCode=<deviceCode-from-API>. The state of which device the user is interested in is therefore represented only in the navigation URL of the target page and not stored in localStorage or session state on this page. Navigation back to devices.html does not depend on URL parameters and will simply re-render using a fresh or cached result from getDevicesOverview.

components.functionality: On initialization of devices.html, this component calls getDevicesOverview to retrieve the full list of supported devices, manages loading and error states, and exposes the resulting devices array to child components for rendering.
event_handlers: onInit
onLoadSuccess
onLoadError
onRetryClick

components.functionality: Receives the devices array from devices-overview-loader and renders each device as a selectable item (e.g., tile or list row) with its name and description. When a device item is clicked, it navigates the browser to device_help.html using the bound deviceCode as a URL query parameter.
event_handlers: onDeviceClick
onEmptyStateRender

components.functionality: Displays a simple navigational path that includes the Devices page (for example, a link back to the Help Center home and a non-clickable label for the current page). Clicking the Help Center link navigates the user back to the help center home without modifying any device-related state.
event_handlers: onBreadcrumbClick

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a centered main content column. The article list appears to use two equal-width columns (approximately 6 + 6) with a central gutter.
layout.layout_pattern: Inside the existing dark, full-width help-center shell (with horizontal top navigation already in place), the Devices page uses a centered main content column on a dark background. At the top of this column sits the breadcrumb trail, left-aligned and relatively compact, acting as the orientation element equivalent to the helper text under the search bar in the mockup. Below that, the main interactive/content block is the devices grid, which visually takes the role of the 'Most Popular Articles' two-column list: a structured, scannable list of device types in two columns. The data loader component occupies the same physical space as the devices grid, appearing on top of or in place of the grid while data is loading.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Inside the existing dark, full-width help-center shell (with horizontal top navigation already in place), the Devices page uses a centered main content column on a dark background. At the top of this column sits the breadcrumb trail, left-aligned and relatively compact, acting as the orientation element equivalent to the helper text under the search bar in the mockup. Below that, the main interactive/content block is the devices grid, which visually takes the role of the 'Most Popular Articles' two-column list: a structured, scannable list of device types in two columns. The data loader component occupies the same physical space as the devices grid, appearing on top of or in place of the grid while data is loading.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Following the mockup, the top navigation (outside these components) consumes ~8–10% of vertical height. The main content area below starts with a relatively modest vertical block for the breadcrumb (about 40–64px of vertical space including top and bottom padding), ensuring it is present but not dominant. The devices grid then occupies the bulk of the remaining viewport height, similar to the 'Most Popular Articles' section, with generous top margin above it (32–48px from the breadcrumb) and comfortable padding inside the content column. Horizontally, all components sit within a centered column spanning roughly 70–80% of the viewport width (max-width around 1040–1120px), leaving substantial left/right margins that echo the framed, cinematic feel of the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page favors clarity over density, echoing the moderately spacious feel of the mockup. The breadcrumb is compact but not cramped (8–12px vertical padding). The devices grid uses comfortable spacing around each device tile: 16–24px vertical gaps between rows and a generous central gutter between the two columns. Text and icons within each grid item have 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, maintaining the 8px-based rhythm. This yields a layout that surfaces many device types in the viewport, but with clear separation and no visual crowding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s top-to-bottom, left-to-right pattern. The eye starts at the top-left with the global brand/navigation (outside this spec), then moves directly down into the main content where the breadcrumb provides immediate context. From the breadcrumb, the eye continues downward to the dominant devices grid section, starting at the top-left of that grid and then scanning down the left column before moving to the right column, exactly as with the two-column article list. During loading states, the data loader temporarily becomes the focal point within the same grid area, centered both vertically and horizontally, then hands visual priority back to the grid once content is loaded.

layout.overall_description: The Devices page sits on a full-width, near-black background with a slim, horizontal top navigation bar spanning the entire viewport. Below this, the main content is contained within a centered, medium-width column that occupies about 70–80% of the viewport, leaving generous dark margins on both sides. At the top of this column, a subtle breadcrumb appears left-aligned, providing a clear path back through the help center while remaining visually light and unobtrusive. After a comfortable vertical gap, the main devices section begins: a left-aligned section title such as 'DEVICES' or 'SUPPORTED DEVICES' (part of the page template) introduces a two-column grid of device types, visually analogous to the 'Most Popular Articles' list in the mockup. Each device type appears as a clean, text-focused tile or row with white text on the dark background, separated by consistent vertical spacing and a noticeable gutter between the two columns. This grid stretches down the page, forming the primary content block the user scans. When data is loading, the grid area is occupied or overlaid by a centered loader element, maintaining the same column width and central alignment, ensuring the user’s attention remains anchored in the main content region. The entire composition feels cinematic and restrained: strong vertical flow, high contrast typography, and sufficient breathing room between the breadcrumb, section heading, and the device grid to maintain hierarchy and legibility.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb is positioned at the top of the main content column, directly below the global navigation bar and any page-level title. It sits left-aligned within the centered content area, starting approximately 32–40px below the bottom edge of the navigation. The breadcrumb text line itself occupies only the top 1/6 to 1/8 of the visible main content section, spanning roughly 60–70% of the content column width (so it does not feel stretched), with the remaining space to the right left empty. There is 8–12px of vertical padding above and below the breadcrumb text to keep it readable but compact. The breadcrumb does not reach the full viewport width; it respects the main content max-width (about 1040–1120px) and is horizontally centered as part of that column, leaving generous dark margins on both sides. Below the breadcrumb, a vertical gap of roughly 32–48px separates it from the start of the devices list section, making the breadcrumb clearly a separate, lighter-weight orientation band rather than part of the grid itself.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls naturally with the page as part of the main document flow; no sticky or floating behavior. It always precedes the devices grid in the vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The devices list occupies the main body of the page, starting just below the breadcrumb with a generous top margin of about 32–48px to mirror the separation seen between the search area and the 'Most Popular Articles' section in the mockup. Within the centered content column (about 70–80% of viewport width, max 1040–1120px), the list is left-aligned and introduced by a section heading such as 'SUPPORTED DEVICES' aligned to the left edge of the column. This heading sits on its own line, with a 16–24px gap beneath it before the grid begins. The grid itself follows a two-column layout on desktop: each column occupies roughly 45% of the content column width, with a 10% central gutter between them, evoking the two equal-width columns in the mockup’s article list. Each device entry appears as a simple, flat tile or row with white text on the dark background, optionally with a rightward chevron icon, and uses internal padding of approximately 12–16px vertically and 16–24px horizontally. Vertically, each row within a column is separated from the next by about 16–24px, keeping the overall density moderate and scannable. The left column items stack downward; the right column mirrors this stack, so users naturally read down the left column first, then move to the right. On narrower viewports (e.g., mobile), this grid gracefully collapses into a single column that still respects side margins (16–24px from the viewport edges), preserving the centered, framed feel. The bottom of the devices list tapers into standard section spacing, with 32–48px of padding or margin before any subsequent footer or separator, echoing the subtle horizontal line that ended the article section in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content block within the main document flow. It expands vertically as more devices are listed but remains constrained to the centered content column. On load, it either appears in place beneath the breadcrumb or sits immediately behind the loader overlay while data is being fetched.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The devices overview loader occupies the same horizontal footprint as the devices list, centered within the main content column below the breadcrumb. When active, it appears starting roughly 32–48px under the breadcrumb (in the exact slot where the devices grid will eventually be), ensuring the user’s gaze stays anchored in the primary content area. There are two valid presentation modes depending on the implementation: (1) Inline replacement: the loader is a vertical stack of skeleton rows or a centered spinner plus a short loading label, spanning about 40–60% of the content column width and horizontally centered, with generous vertical padding (40–64px above and below) so it feels like a substantial, intentional state rather than an afterthought. (2) Overlay: the loader sits as a semi-transparent overlay directly on top of the future devices grid area, darkening it slightly while a spinner or skeleton tiles are centered both vertically and horizontally within that area. In both modes, the loader remains within the same max-width bounds (1040–1120px) and maintains the same left alignment as the eventual grid heading, echoing the strong central focus from the mockup’s search module. When loading completes, the loader disappears entirely, revealing the devices list without shifting the overall layout of surrounding elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual and temporary. It occupies (or overlays) the devices list’s space during data fetch. It is not fixed to the viewport; it scrolls with the page if content above pushes it down. If implemented as an overlay, it uses absolute positioning within the devices list container to cover that area while preserving the underlying layout.

name: Device Help Topics
title: Device Help Topics
description: Page that shows help topics for a specific device, based on a deviceCode URL parameter and the getDeviceHelpTopics interface. Users can select a topic to view related help articles on a subcategory page and use breadcrumbs to navigate back to higher-level help pages.

core_features: Read the deviceCode URL parameter on load to determine which device’s help topics to display.
Call getDeviceHelpTopics with the current deviceCode to retrieve device information, the ordered list of help topics, and breadcrumb metadata.
Display the current device’s name and description so users can confirm they are viewing help for the correct device.
Render an ordered list of help topics returned by getDeviceHelpTopics, using the API-provided topic names and order.
Allow users to click a topic to navigate to subcategory.html while preserving device context via URL parameters (deviceCode and topicCode).
Render breadcrumb navigation using the breadcrumb array from getDeviceHelpTopics, enabling navigation back to Help Center home, Devices, or other higher-level pages.
Handle loading states while getDeviceHelpTopics is in progress and display an error state if the interface call fails or returns no topics for the selected device.
React to changes in the deviceCode URL parameter (for example, via internal navigation) by re-calling getDeviceHelpTopics and updating the page without requiring a full page reload.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Arrive from Devices page to view help topics for a specific device
1) User selects a device on devices.html, which navigates to device_help.html with a deviceCode query parameter.
2) device_help.html reads the deviceCode from the URL.
3) The page calls getDeviceHelpTopics with the deviceCode.
4) While waiting, a loading state is shown where the topics list will appear.
5) On success, the page displays the device name and description from the response.
6) The page renders the ordered list of topics from the response, each as a clickable element.
7) The breadcrumb trail from the response is displayed at the top of the page for navigation context.

Workflow: Navigate from a device help topic to its related articles list
1) On device_help.html, the user identifies a topic relevant to their issue in the topics list.
2) The user clicks the topic item.
3) The click handler reads the current deviceCode from the URL and the topicCode from the clicked topic’s data.
4) The browser navigates to subcategory.html with URL parameters deviceCode and topicCode set to those values.
5) subcategory.html can then show an article list filtered by the device and topic (handled on that page).

Workflow: Use breadcrumb navigation to move to a higher-level help page
1) On device_help.html, the user sees a breadcrumb trail built from the breadcrumb array returned by getDeviceHelpTopics.
2) The user clicks a breadcrumb item.
3) The click handler inspects the breadcrumb item’s pageType and label and maps it to the appropriate target URL (for example, help center home, devices page, or another page).
4) The browser navigates to that target URL, optionally including required URL parameters if applicable.
5) The new page loads, providing the broader or different help context the user selected.

Workflow: Handle missing or invalid deviceCode
1) User navigates directly to device_help.html without a deviceCode parameter, or with a value that causes getDeviceHelpTopics to fail.
2) On load, the URL parameter manager does not find a valid deviceCode, or the getDeviceHelpTopics call returns an error or an empty topics list.
3) The page displays an error or fallback message component indicating that device-specific help cannot be loaded.
4) The component offers navigation options such as a link back to the Devices page or Help Center home.
5) If the user then navigates with a valid deviceCode, the page re-runs getDeviceHelpTopics and shows the correct device help topics.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load
- Trigger: Browser loads device_help.html.
- Behavior: Parse deviceCode from the URL. If present, invoke getDeviceHelpTopics(deviceCode). Show loading indicator until the response is received. On success, populate device header, topics list, and breadcrumbs from the response.

Interaction: Change of deviceCode via internal navigation (e.g., link that points to another device_help.html with a different deviceCode)
- Trigger: User clicks a link that navigates to device_help.html with a different deviceCode value.
- Behavior: URL parameter manager detects that deviceCode has changed. The page clears previous device and topics data, re-enters loading state, calls getDeviceHelpTopics with the new deviceCode, and then renders the new data.

Interaction: Topic click
- Trigger: User clicks a topic row or tile in the topics list.
- Behavior: The topic element exposes topicCode from the getDeviceHelpTopics response. The click handler reads the current deviceCode from the URL and the topicCode from the element, then navigates the browser to subcategory.html with deviceCode and topicCode as URL parameters.

Interaction: Breadcrumb click
- Trigger: User clicks a breadcrumb item.
- Behavior: The breadcrumb item is bound to one element of the breadcrumb array returned by getDeviceHelpTopics. The click handler uses the breadcrumb entry’s pageType (and optionally label) to resolve the correct target page URL and any required query parameters, then navigates there. If the breadcrumb points back to the Devices page, it navigates without a deviceCode parameter; if it points to the current page, it may keep the deviceCode.

Interaction: Retry after error
- Trigger: getDeviceHelpTopics fails (e.g., network error) and the user clicks a retry button in the error state component.
- Behavior: The handler reads the current deviceCode from the URL, re-calls getDeviceHelpTopics, and toggles the UI from error state back to loading, then to success once data is available.

Interaction: Non-interactive states
- Loading: While getDeviceHelpTopics is in progress, a loading component is displayed instead of topics.
- Empty: If getDeviceHelpTopics succeeds but returns no topics, an empty-state message is shown, optionally with a navigation link back to higher-level help pages.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the deviceCode URL parameter. On every load or navigation to device_help.html, the page reads deviceCode from the query string. If a deviceCode is present, getDeviceHelpTopics is called with that value to retrieve device-specific data. The response populates three logical state segments: device (deviceCode, deviceName, description), topics (an ordered list of topic entries, each with topicCode and topicName), and breadcrumb (an ordered list of breadcrumb entries with label and pageType). The UI components bind directly to this state. When the user clicks a topic, the page constructs a navigation URL for subcategory.html that includes deviceCode (from the current URL state) and topicCode (from the selected topic). When the user clicks a breadcrumb, navigation is determined by the breadcrumb entry’s pageType, and any necessary parameters for the target page are appended to the URL according to internal routing rules. No state is persisted in localStorage or other client storage; navigation and reloading are always driven by URL parameters and fresh calls to getDeviceHelpTopics.

components.functionality: Parses query parameters from the current URL, exposes deviceCode to other components, and listens for URL changes to trigger data reloading when deviceCode changes.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read deviceCode from the URL and notify data-fetch components.
onUrlChange: Detect changes to deviceCode and trigger a refresh sequence for dependent components.

components.functionality: Calls getDeviceHelpTopics with the current deviceCode, manages loading and error states, and exposes the resulting device, topics, and breadcrumb data to presentation components.
event_handlers: onDeviceCodeAvailable: When url-parameter-manager provides a valid deviceCode, call getDeviceHelpTopics(deviceCode) and set internal loading state to true.
onGetDeviceHelpTopicsSuccess: Store the returned device, topics, and breadcrumb data; set loading=false and error=null; notify bound components to re-render.
onGetDeviceHelpTopicsError: Store error details; set loading=false; expose error state to the error component.
onRetryRequest: Re-invoke getDeviceHelpTopics using the current deviceCode when triggered by the error component.

components.functionality: Shows the current device’s name and description so the user is aware which device the topics apply to.
event_handlers: onDeviceDataChange: Re-render the header whenever the device object from getDeviceHelpTopics changes.

components.functionality: Renders the breadcrumb trail based on the breadcrumb array returned by getDeviceHelpTopics, and handles navigation when the user clicks a breadcrumb item.
event_handlers: onBreadcrumbClick: Receive the clicked breadcrumb’s pageType (and label if needed), map pageType to a target URL (e.g., Help Center home, Devices, or another relevant page), and navigate the browser to that URL.
onBreadcrumbDataChange: Rebuild the breadcrumb UI when the breadcrumb array from getDeviceHelpTopics changes.

components.functionality: Displays an ordered list of device help topics returned by getDeviceHelpTopics, each as a selectable item that leads to a subcategory/article list page.
event_handlers: onTopicsDataChange: Sort or arrange the topics according to the order property, then render each as a clickable list item.
onTopicClick: Use the current deviceCode from the URL and the clicked item’s topicCode to navigate to subcategory.html with deviceCode and topicCode as query parameters.

components.functionality: Represents a single topic row or tile within the topic list, showing the topic name and handling click events for navigation.
event_handlers: onClick: Emit a topicClick event to topic-list with the topicCode associated with this item.

components.functionality: Shows a loading message or spinner while getDeviceHelpTopics is in progress and hides itself when data has loaded or an error occurs.
event_handlers: onLoadingStateChange: Show when loading=true and hide when loading=false.

components.functionality: Displays an error message if getDeviceHelpTopics fails or if no valid deviceCode is available, and provides a retry action.
event_handlers: onErrorChange: When an error is present, render an appropriate message and hide the topics list; when error clears, hide the error display.
onRetryClick: Trigger device-help-data-loader.onRetryRequest so the API call is retried with the current deviceCode.
onFallbackNavigationClick: Optionally navigate the user to a higher-level page (e.g., Devices or Help Center home) if they choose not to retry.

components.functionality: Handles the case where getDeviceHelpTopics succeeds but returns an empty topics array, informing the user that no topics are available for the selected device.
event_handlers: onTopicsDataChange: If loading=false, error=null, and topics is empty, show the empty-state message and hide the topic list.
onNavigateBackClick: Optionally navigate the user back to a broader help page (e.g., Devices) using standard navigation logic.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a centered main content column. The article list appears to use two equal-width columns (approximately 6 + 6) with a central gutter.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom layout anchored in a centered main content column on a full-width dark background. Global navigation (outside this spec) sits at the very top. Directly beneath, the breadcrumb establishes context in the upper-left of the content column. Below that, a prominent device context header occupies a hero-like block, analogous to the search area in the mockup. Under the header, a section heading introduces the list of help topics, which are displayed in two vertical text-only columns similar to the 'Most Popular Articles' pattern. Loading, error, and empty states all occupy the same region as the topic list beneath the device header.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom layout anchored in a centered main content column on a full-width dark background. Global navigation (outside this spec) sits at the very top. Directly beneath, the breadcrumb establishes context in the upper-left of the content column. Below that, a prominent device context header occupies a hero-like block, analogous to the search area in the mockup. Under the header, a section heading introduces the list of help topics, which are displayed in two vertical text-only columns similar to the 'Most Popular Articles' pattern. Loading, error, and empty states all occupy the same region as the topic list beneath the device header.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve a modest vertical band under the global nav for breadcrumbs (roughly 8–10% of the initial viewport height including its surrounding whitespace). Allocate a larger, visually dominant block to the device context header (about 20–25% of the viewport height) with generous top and bottom padding, making it the main focal region. The remaining vertical space is dedicated to the help topics section, with a clear gap between header and topics (about 32–48px). Horizontally, keep the main content constrained to a centered 12-column grid (roughly 70–80% of the viewport width with comfortable left/right margins), with the two-column topic layout each consuming roughly 5–6 columns separated by a wide central gutter.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious feeling: no visual clutter, but no empty wasteland. Breadcrumb links are compact yet readable. The device header has comfortable breathing room around its title and any supporting text, without feeling like a massive hero banner. Topic list items are stacked with regular 16–24px vertical spacing and light internal padding so they are easily scannable. Section gaps (breadcrumb to header, header to topics, topics to page bottom) follow the 32–48px spacing, preserving the rhythm seen in the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from top-left to bottom-right in a strong vertical flow. After the global nav, the user first notices the breadcrumb in the upper-left, which orients them within the help hierarchy. The eye is then drawn down and slightly inward to the bold, centered device context header. From there, gaze naturally drops to the left-aligned topics section heading and then flows through the two-column list, typically reading down the left column of topics before moving to the right. When loading, error, or empty states are active, they appear in the list area immediately below the header, preserving this vertical progression and keeping the user’s focus where they expect content to appear.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a full-bleed, dark charcoal background with a centered main content column that feels like a focused panel against the edges of the viewport. At the very top (outside this spec) is the site-wide horizontal navigation; directly beneath it, the Device Help Topics page begins. A subtle breadcrumb row, left-aligned within the centered column, anchors the user in the help hierarchy without competing for attention. Below that, a generous vertical block presents the device context header: the device name appears in bold, high-contrast white text, centered within the column, with a succinct supporting line below in a softer gray. This header block has ample top and bottom padding, occupying a visually significant slice of the initial viewport and establishing the device-specific context as the primary focal point. Underneath, with a clear 32–48px gap, a left-aligned section heading introduces the available help topics for that device. The main content area below is a two-column grid of purely textual topic links, mirroring the mockup’s ‘Most Popular Articles’ pattern. Each column is evenly sized, separated by a wide central gutter, and the topics within each column are stacked with balanced vertical spacing, making the list feel dense enough to be useful yet airy enough to scan quickly. The entire composition moves the user’s attention smoothly from navigation to context to actionable topics in a clean, cinematic, dark UI. When data is loading, when errors occur, or when no topics exist, those states appear in the same region as the topic list beneath the header, maintaining the same centered column and spacing so the page feels stable and predictable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component has no visual footprint. It operates at the page level, logically positioned ‘above’ the visible layout. Conceptually, it sits behind the global navigation and main content column, reading the deviceCode from the URL before anything else renders. It does not occupy space in the grid or affect spacing; instead, it simply prepares the state that drives what appears in the device context header and topic list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; it behaves as an invisible, page-scoped controller that initializes before the main content is laid out.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is also non-visual. It lives just below the URL parameter manager in the logical hierarchy and just above the main content in terms of render flow. It does not occupy any part of the 12-column grid. Instead, it governs whether the topic list region shows a loading indicator, an error state, an empty state, or the actual list, all within the area directly beneath the device context header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; it orchestrates the content state of the main column without affecting layout dimensions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The device context header appears in the upper portion of the main content column, immediately below the breadcrumb navigation. It spans the full width of the centered content column (approximately 70–80% of the viewport width, constrained to the 12-column grid) and uses generous vertical padding—around 32–48px above and below its content—creating a hero-style block. The primary device title is horizontally centered within this block, in large, bold white text. Directly beneath the title, there is a single line (or at most two lines) of helper text in a smaller, softer gray, also center-aligned. The block sits with roughly 24–32px of vertical space above it (from breadcrumbs) and about 32–48px of space below it before the topics section begins. On wider screens, this creates a strong, balanced focal area that occupies roughly 20–25% of the initial viewport height; on smaller screens it compresses vertically but maintains centered alignment and the same proportional paddings. The entire header feels like a clean, content-first panel without illustrations or heavy decoration, matching the minimalist, cinematic tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page scroll; it scrolls away naturally with the rest of the content and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation sits at the top of the page’s main content column, immediately beneath the global site navigation bar. It is left-aligned within the centered column, starting at the first grid column on the left and spanning the width required by the breadcrumb links (typically 4–8 columns, leaving generous empty space to the right). Vertically, it uses modest padding: about 16–24px of space from the bottom of the global nav and 16–24px of space below before the device context header begins. The text is small to medium in size with high-contrast white and muted gray for inactive segments, separated by subtle chevrons or separators. The row height is compact, around 32–40px total, keeping it visually secondary yet clearly visible at the top. On large screens, the breadcrumb line occupies roughly the top 8–10% of the viewport when combined with its surrounding whitespace; on smaller screens it continues to align left but wraps gracefully if necessary, maintaining the same vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it scrolls off-screen as the user moves down, remaining anchored to the top of the main content column without sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic list occupies the main body area immediately beneath the device context header. It is contained within the same centered content column (about 70–80% of viewport width, aligned to the 12-column grid). At the top of this component, a left-aligned section heading such as ‘Help Topics’ or ‘Topics for This Device’ sits flush with the left grid edge, using bold white text similar in style to the mockup’s ‘MOST POPULAR ARTICLES’ heading. There is a clear gap of roughly 32–40px between the bottom of the device context header and this heading. Below the heading, after another 16–24px gap, the topics themselves are laid out in a two-column grid. Each column roughly spans 5–6 grid columns, with a central gutter equivalent to about 1–2 columns, giving a generous space between the two stacks. On desktop and larger tablet screens, both columns are visible side by side, with the left column starting at the left grid boundary and the right column aligned to the right boundary of the content column. Topics are stacked vertically in each column with approximately 16–24px of vertical spacing between items, producing a stable rhythm. On narrow viewports (e.g., mobile), the layout collapses to a single column, keeping the left-aligned heading and stacking all topic items vertically in one list while preserving similar spacing. The list area expands vertically based on the number of topics, but overall maintains a comfortable use of space without crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section that grows vertically to fit its content; on smaller screens it responsively shifts from a two-column to a single-column layout while remaining within the same centered content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each topic list item appears as a text-only link within one of the two topic columns. It occupies the full width of its column, with no bullet or icon by default, echoing the simple question-style links from the mockup. The clickable text is high-contrast white, left-aligned, and may wrap to multiple lines if long. Each item has modest vertical padding (about 8–12px above and below the text) to increase tap and click target size without appearing like a full card. The space between consecutive items in a column is about 16–24px, creating a consistent vertical rhythm. There is no visible border, but a subtle change on hover/focus—such as a faint underline or slight increase in text brightness—gives feedback while preserving the flat, minimalist feel. Items in the left column align in a clean vertical line with the section heading above, while items in the right column mirror that structure to the right side of the content column. On mobile, each item simply becomes one entry in the single stacked list, preserving left alignment and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static elements within the topic-list grid; they do not float or overlap and simply reflow as the list changes from two columns to one on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator appears in place of the topic list content area whenever data is being fetched. It is positioned directly beneath the device context header, aligned within the same centered content column. Instead of rendering the section heading and two-column list, the loading state occupies that vertical region with a vertically and horizontally centered indicator. On most screens, this means a spinner or subtle progress element sits at the horizontal center of the content column, roughly midway between the bottom of the header and the lower edge of the current viewport. A brief loading label in small, high-contrast text may appear below the spinner. There is still clear spacing (about 24–32px) between the header and the top edge of the loading indicator, preserving the breathing room of the normal layout. The background remains the same dark tone, and no additional panels or overlays are introduced, so the layout feels consistent while the user waits.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Transient and mutually exclusive with the topic list, error, and empty states; it occupies the same block area as the topic-list and scrolls with the page, without overlaying the header or breadcrumbs.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state replaces the topic list region when loading fails.
