It sits directly beneath the device context header within the centered content column, using the same horizontal alignment and width as the normal topics area. Instead of the section heading and two columns, the error message appears as a left-aligned block of text starting at the left grid edge. A concise error title in bold white text is followed by a brief explanation in softer gray and an inline or stacked action such as a ‘Retry’ button or link. The block has comfortable vertical padding—about 24–32px above and below its content—and it begins roughly 32px below the header, echoing the spacing used for the standard topics heading. The overall height is smaller than the full topic list but leaves ample empty dark space around it, underlining the issue without feeling visually heavy. On smaller screens, the error text simply wraps within the same column width, maintaining left alignment and consistent margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the topic list, loading, and empty states; it occupies the same static content area beneath the header, scrolling normally with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state appears in the topic list region when the device has no available help topics. It occupies the same position directly beneath the device context header, inside the centered content column, and replaces the standard section heading and columns. A succinct headline such as ‘No topics available for this device yet’ is left-aligned at the left grid boundary in medium-weight white text. Below it, one or two short lines of softer gray explanatory text provide guidance or suggest alternative actions (e.g., returning to a higher-level category). This text block sits about 32px below the header, respecting the same vertical gap as the topics heading would, and includes about 24–32px of internal vertical padding so it does not feel cramped. The surrounding space below remains mostly empty, reinforcing the minimalist aesthetic and making it immediately clear that the list is intentionally blank rather than still loading. On narrower screens the text simply wraps but stays left-aligned within the same margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the topic list, loading, and error states; it is a static, scrollable block occupying the same content area beneath the header.

name: Article Detail
title: Article Detail
description: Displays a full help article with sections, instructions, language switching, save controls, feedback, related articles, and contextual support options based on the requested articleId and language.

core_features: Load full article data (title, bodyHtml, sections, metadata, CTAs, related articles, contact options) by calling getArticleDetail with URL parameters articleId and lang (mapped to language).
Display article content including headings, overview, and structured sections (e.g., instructions, troubleshooting, payment steps) using getArticleDetail.bodyHtml and getArticleDetail.sections.

Allow users to switch article language using a language selector bound to getArticleDetail.language and getArticleDetail.availableLanguages; changing language updates the lang URL parameter and re-calls getArticleDetail.
Enable saving/unsaving the article via a Save/Bookmark control bound to getArticleDetail.isSaved and using saveArticle and unsaveArticle to update the saved state.
Provide an article feedback control (e.g., helpful / not helpful) that calls submitArticleFeedback with articleId and helpful, and displays acknowledgement using the response.
Show related articles using getArticleDetail.relatedArticles and navigate to the selected article by updating article.html?articleId={relatedId}&lang={currentLang}.

Expose contextual in-article CTAs (e.g., contact support, payment settings, profile settings, external article/page) using getArticleDetail.ctas and navigate via URL parameters to the appropriate target page.
Display a contextual 'Contact support about this issue' button when getArticleDetail.hasContactSupportButton is true, navigating to contact_support.html with source=article and the current articleId.

Show per-article contact options (chat, phone, email, web form) from getArticleDetail.contactOptions at the bottom of the article for applicable articles, and navigate to contact_support.html including the selected contactOptionId.
Display breadcrumb navigation from getArticleDetail.breadcrumb to orient users and allow navigation back to higher-level help pages.
user_workflows: Read a help article: (1) User navigates to article.html?articleId={id}[&lang={code}]; (2) Page calls getArticleDetail(articleId, language) using articleId from URL and lang (or default if absent); (3) Article header, breadcrumbs, metadata, and bodyHtml render; (4) Sections and instructional steps render from getArticleDetail.sections; (5) User scrolls and optionally jumps between sections via the table of contents.

Save or unsave an article: (1) After getArticleDetail resolves, the Save control reflects getArticleDetail.isSaved; (2) User clicks the Save/Bookmark control; (3) If isSaved is false, page calls saveArticle(articleId); on success, the control updates to saved state and may display a confirmation; (4) If isSaved is true, page calls unsaveArticle(articleId); on success, the control updates to unsaved state; (5) No local storage is used; the UI state is driven by API responses.

Switch article language: (1) User opens the language selector showing getArticleDetail.availableLanguages and highlighting getArticleDetail.language; (2) User selects a different language code; (3) Page updates the URL lang parameter (keeping the same articleId) without altering other query parameters; (4) Page calls getArticleDetail again with the updated language; (5) The article title, bodyHtml, sections, and other localized strings re-render in the selected language.

Submit article feedback: (1) User reaches the feedback section at the bottom of the article; (2) User clicks the 'helpful' or 'not helpful' option; (3) Page calls submitArticleFeedback(articleId, helpful=true/false); (4) On success, UI shows a thank-you message and may show updated helpful/not helpful counts from the response (currentHelpfulCount, currentNotHelpfulCount); (5) The feedback controls can optionally be disabled or visually marked as submitted for that session.

Navigate to related help content: (1) User scrolls to the Related articles section populated from getArticleDetail.relatedArticles; (2) User clicks a related article item; (3) Page navigates to article.html?articleId={selectedRelatedArticleId}&lang={currentLang}; (4) New page load calls getArticleDetail with the new articleId and current language, repeating the main article workflow.

Contact support from an article: (1) For articles with getArticleDetail.hasContactSupportButton=true, user sees a 'Contact support about this issue' CTA; (2) User clicks the CTA; (3) Browser navigates to contact_support.html with URL parameters source=article and articleId={currentArticleId} (contactOptionId omitted or left empty); (4) Alternatively, user scrolls to the bottom contact options list populated from getArticleDetail.contactOptions; (5) User clicks a specific contact option; (6) Browser navigates to contact_support.html?source=article&articleId={currentArticleId}&contactOptionId={selectedContactOptionId}.

Follow in-article CTAs for payment or profile settings: (1) User reads a section that includes a CTA from getArticleDetail.ctas (e.g., to payment_settings or profile_settings); (2) User clicks the CTA; (3) For payment settings CTAs, browser navigates to payment_settings.html?fromArticleId={currentArticleId}; (4) For profile settings CTAs, browser navigates to profile_settings.html?fromArticleId={currentArticleId}; (5) For CTAs targeting another help article, browser navigates to article.html?articleId={targetArticleId}&lang={currentLang}.
interactions: On page load or when articleId/lang URL parameters change, trigger getArticleDetail with articleId from URL and language equal to lang (or a default decided by the backend) and re-render all article-bound components.

Clicking a breadcrumb item navigates the user to the corresponding page type (e.g., category, subcategory, help home) using URLs determined from the breadcrumb item’s pageType and label; no additional APIs are called from this page.
Interacting with the language selector (dropdown or equivalent control) fires an onLanguageChange handler that updates the lang URL parameter and re-invokes getArticleDetail.

Clicking the Save/Bookmark icon invokes an onClick handler which decides between saveArticle and unsaveArticle based on current isSaved; UI state (icon state and accessible label) updates according to the API response.
Clicking a section in the table of contents triggers scrolling or jumping to the element with the matching anchor from getArticleDetail.sections[].anchor.

Clicks on in-body CTAs from getArticleDetail.ctas trigger navigation to the appropriate target (contact_support.html, payment_settings.html, profile_settings.html, article.html, or external URL) using URL parameters specified by the CTA’s targetType and targetArticleId.
Clicking the 'Contact support about this issue' primary button (when present) triggers navigation to contact_support.html?source=article&articleId={currentArticleId}.
Clicking a specific contact option in the bottom contact options list navigates to contact_support.html?source=article&articleId={currentArticleId}&contactOptionId={selectedContactOptionId}.

Clicking the 'helpful' or 'not helpful' feedback buttons invokes submitArticleFeedback with the appropriate helpful flag and updates the feedback UI based on the response (e.g., thank-you state, counts).
Clicking a related article item navigates to article.html with articleId set to the selected related articleId and lang preserved as the current language.

If getArticleDetail or any action interface (saveArticle, unsaveArticle, submitArticleFeedback) returns an error, the respective component shows an error state or message and allows retry where appropriate.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s primary state is driven by URL parameters articleId (required) and lang (optional). On initial load, the page reads articleId and lang from the URL query string. If lang is missing, the page passes a null/undefined language value to getArticleDetail and relies on the backend to return content in the article’s default_language, also returning the resolved language in getArticleDetail.language. Whenever articleId or lang in the URL changes (for example, via navigation to a related article or through the language selector), the page re-calls getArticleDetail(articleId, language) and rebinds all components to the new data. Save state (isSaved) is not stored locally; it is derived from getArticleDetail.isSaved and the results of saveArticle/unsaveArticle. The UI reflects the latest known state from the last successful response. Feedback submission state is also not persisted in URL or local storage; it exists only in memory for the current page lifetime, with the server as the source of truth for cumulative feedback counts. Navigation to other pages (contact_support.html, payment_settings.html, profile_settings.html, article.html for related articles) is implemented purely by constructing appropriate URLs with query parameters as specified; no additional interfaces are invoked from article.html beyond getArticleDetail, saveArticle, unsaveArticle, and submitArticleFeedback.

components.functionality: Fetches full article detail based on URL parameters (articleId and lang) and exposes the response to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: read articleId and lang from URL; call getArticleDetail(articleId, language)
onUrlParamChange: when articleId or lang changes, call getArticleDetail(articleId, language) again and update bound data
onLoadError: display an error state or message if getArticleDetail fails

components.functionality: Displays the hierarchical breadcrumb trail for the current article and allows navigation back to higher-level help pages.
event_handlers: onClickBreadcrumbItem: navigate to the target page determined by breadcrumbItem.pageType and label using appropriate URL parameters

components.functionality: Shows the article title, optional summary, category/subcategory labels, meta information (e.g., estimated read time, last updated), and includes the language selector and save control.
event_handlers: onLanguageSelectorOpen: show availableLanguages with the current language highlighted
onLanguageChange: update the lang URL parameter to the selected language and trigger article-detail-loader.onUrlParamChange
onClickSaveToggle: delegate to save-article-toggle.onClickSaveToggle to perform save/unsave behavior

components.functionality: Allows the user to switch the article’s language among the languages available for this article.
event_handlers: onChange: when user selects a language, update the URL lang parameter (preserving articleId and other query parameters) and trigger a reload via article-detail-loader.onUrlParamChange

components.functionality: Displays and toggles the saved/unsaved state of the current article in the user’s Saved articles list.
event_handlers: onClickSaveToggle: if isSaved is false, call saveArticle(articleId); on success, update local isSaved state to true and adjust control label/state
onClickUnsave: if isSaved is true, call unsaveArticle(articleId); on success, update local isSaved state to false and adjust control label/state
onSaveError: show an error indication if saveArticle fails while leaving the UI in the previous consistent state
onUnsaveError: show an error indication if unsaveArticle fails while leaving the UI in the previous consistent state

components.functionality: Lists article sections and allows quick navigation within the article by clicking section titles.
event_handlers: onClickSectionLink: scroll or jump to the corresponding section element identified by sections[i].anchor

components.functionality: Renders the full article body, including rich text content, headings, and any inline links from bodyHtml.
event_handlers: onInternalAnchorClick: handle jumps to anchors within the page for any links embedded in bodyHtml

onInlineLinkClick: for links recognized as Help Center article links, navigate to article.html with the appropriate articleId and current lang; for external links, open as regular navigation or in a new tab depending on link attributes

components.functionality: Displays structured sections of the article (e.g., instructions, troubleshooting, payment steps) using the sections metadata, including numbered step lists.
event_handlers: onSectionRender: ensure sections are rendered in order based on sections[].order and available anchors are attached to DOM elements for in-page navigation

components.functionality: Renders contextual call-to-action buttons and links defined for the article, such as contacting support, going to payment settings, opening profile settings, or navigating to other help articles or external pages.
event_handlers: onClickCTA: when a CTA is clicked, inspect ctas[i].targetType and perform navigation as follows: (a) if 'contact_support', navigate to contact_support.html?source=article&articleId={currentArticleId}; (b) if 'payment_settings', navigate to payment_settings.html?fromArticleId={currentArticleId}; (c) if 'profile_settings', navigate to profile_settings.html?fromArticleId={currentArticleId}; (d) if 'external_help_article', navigate to article.html?articleId={ctas[i].targetArticleId}&lang={currentLang}; (e) if 'external_page', navigate to the provided target URL in the same or new tab according to openInNewTab

components.functionality: Displays a primary 'Contact support about this issue' button when the article is configured to offer direct contact support.
event_handlers: onRender: show the button only if hasContactSupportButton is true

onClick: navigate to contact_support.html?source=article&articleId={currentArticleId} (contactOptionId not set or left empty); any use of defaultSupportTopic and defaultErrorCode is passed implicitly through that page’s logic, not via additional interfaces from this page

components.functionality: Shows available contact methods (e.g., chat, phone, email, web form) at the bottom of applicable articles, including estimated wait times and availability.
event_handlers: onRender: only display contact options entries where contactOptions[i].available is true; indicate default contact method when isDefault is true
onClickContactOption: navigate to contact_support.html?source=article&articleId={currentArticleId}&contactOptionId={selectedContactOptionId}

components.functionality: Enables users to rate the article as helpful or not and shows confirmation and optional aggregate feedback data.
event_handlers: onClickHelpful: call submitArticleFeedback(articleId, helpful=true); on success, show a thank-you message and optionally display currentHelpfulCount/currentNotHelpfulCount from the response
onClickNotHelpful: call submitArticleFeedback(articleId, helpful=false); on success, show a thank-you message and optionally display currentHelpfulCount/currentNotHelpfulCount from the response
onFeedbackError: show an error indication if submitArticleFeedback fails while allowing the user to retry

components.functionality: Displays a list of related articles and allows navigation to them.
event_handlers: onClickRelatedArticle: navigate to article.html?articleId={selectedRelatedArticleId}&lang={currentLang} using getArticleDetail.language for currentLang

components.functionality: Shows additional metadata about the article such as created date, last updated date, and possibly applicable plans or devices, to contextualize the information.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a centered main content column. The article list appears to use two equal-width columns (approximately 6 + 6) with a central gutter.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit inside a centered main content column on a full-width dark background, mirroring the mockup’s structure under the global top navigation. The flow is strictly vertical: breadcrumbs at the very top of the content area, followed by a prominent article header block (title + language/save controls), then a compact section table of contents, the main article content (body then structured sections), then contextual CTAs and support options, and finally feedback, related articles, and metadata. The only multi-column treatment is reserved for the related articles list, which intentionally mirrors the mockup’s two-column list of links.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit inside a centered main content column on a full-width dark background, mirroring the mockup’s structure under the global top navigation. The flow is strictly vertical: breadcrumbs at the very top of the content area, followed by a prominent article header block (title + language/save controls), then a compact section table of contents, the main article content (body then structured sections), then contextual CTAs and support options, and finally feedback, related articles, and metadata. The only multi-column treatment is reserved for the related articles list, which intentionally mirrors the mockup’s two-column list of links.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, centered with generous left/right margins (about 15–20% of the viewport each side). Vertically, the header + TOC cluster forms a visually dominant block similar in weight to the mockup’s search area, with comfortable padding above and below. Major sections (header, TOC, main body, CTAs/support, feedback, related articles, metadata) are separated by 32–48px. The article body itself consumes the majority of vertical space through scrolling, while the related-articles block and feedback panel are more compact but still well-spaced. The loader, when active, temporarily occupies the entire viewport as a dim overlay.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page keeps a moderately spacious density: typography is high-contrast and easy to read, with line lengths constrained to the central column. Paragraphs, headings, and list items have 8–16px vertical spacing, and major blocks have 32–48px separation to reduce visual fatigue. The two-column related-articles list increases information density near the bottom without feeling crowded, thanks to a wide central gutter and consistent line spacing.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye starts at the top-left with the breadcrumbs, then drops immediately to the bold article title. From there, it naturally shifts to the right side of the same horizontal band where the language selector and save toggle live. The gaze then moves downward to the centered section table of contents (acting like the mockup’s search bar as the primary navigational control), then flows straight into the article body and structured sections, scrolling down. After reading, attention is guided to CTAs and the prominent contact-support button, then to the feedback panel. Finally, the eye reaches the ‘Related Articles’ section, scanning its two columns top-down on the left and then the right, before ending at the subtle metadata at the bottom.

layout.overall_description: The Article Detail page appears as a dark, cinematic help view framed by a wide, full-bleed black background. In the center sits a single, tall content column, roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, leaving generous empty margins on both sides so the eye stays focused on the copy. At the top of this column, small, understated breadcrumbs anchor the user’s sense of location. Immediately beneath, a bold, left-aligned article title dominates, occupying a shallow but prominent header band. On the right side of this band, aligned in the same row, a compact language selector and bookmark-style save toggle provide utility without overpowering the title. Below this header, a centered ‘On this page’-style section table of contents echoes the mockup’s large search bar: a wide, horizontally centered module with links to key sections, framed by ample vertical whitespace. Scrolling down, the main article content unfolds in a single column with comfortable line spacing and clear section breaks; structured sections such as steps or FAQs appear as clearly separated sub-blocks. After the content, a cluster of article-specific CTAs and a prominent contact-support button provide clear next actions, followed by a neat list of alternative contact options. Near the bottom, a compact feedback panel sits centered in the column, visually distinct with subtle borders. Finally, a ‘Related Articles’ section mirrors the mockup’s ‘Most Popular Articles’ pattern: a left-aligned heading above two well-spaced columns of text-only article links, ending with a thin horizontal divider and a small metadata line that quietly notes update information. The overall impression is focused, minimal, and functional, with a strong top-to-bottom, left-to-right reading flow and plenty of breathing room.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loader appears as a full-viewport dark overlay on top of the entire page, including the global navigation and centered content column. It slightly dims the background with a semi-opaque black layer while keeping the underlying structure faintly visible. In the exact center of the viewport, a small, minimalist spinner or progress indicator is displayed, occupying roughly 5–10% of the viewport width and height. The overlay stretches from the very top edge of the browser window to the bottom, and from the left edge to the right, fixed in place so it does not scroll with the content, effectively locking attention on the loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed, full-screen overlay that sits above all other components and remains centered regardless of scroll position while loading is in progress.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumbs sit at the top of the main content column directly under the global navigation area, aligned to the left edge of the column. They occupy the full width of the column horizontally but are visually compact, forming a single line or two of text. There is a 32–40px top margin from the bottom of the global nav to the breadcrumbs, giving them breathing room without feeling detached. Below the breadcrumbs, there is about 16–24px of vertical space before the article header begins, so they read as a subtle pre-title hint rather than a main focal point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the normal document flow, always appearing as the first element within the centered content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article header occupies the full width of the centered content column and forms a shallow, horizontally oriented band just below the breadcrumbs. On the left side of this band, spanning approximately 70% of the column width, the article title is displayed in a large, bold, white font against the dark background, with 16–24px padding above and below the text to give it a hero-like feel without becoming as tall as a full hero section. The top of the header sits about 16–24px below the breadcrumbs. Any optional subtitle or brief summary sits directly below the title, left-aligned, with 8–12px spacing. The rightmost 30% of the header band is reserved for utility controls (language selector and save toggle), aligned to the top-right or vertically centered relative to the title. The header visually anchors the page as the first major block, similar in importance to the mockup’s search area, but compressed vertically to fit the article context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block, full width of the main content column, serving as a main anchor at the top of the page’s content flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The language selector is positioned inside the same horizontal band as the article header, aligned to the far right edge of the main content column. It occupies a compact rectangular area, approximately 15–20% of the column width, and sits either in line with the title’s first baseline or slightly lower, visually forming a right-justified control cluster. There is about 8–16px of horizontal space between the language selector and the save-article toggle to its left (or right, depending on order), creating a tight, utilitarian control row. Vertically, it shares the same top margin and bottom padding as the header, so both controls feel like part of one cohesive header bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, positioned within the header’s horizontal layout, aligning to the right side and adjusting responsively to stay in the utility area above the main article content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-article toggle appears adjacent to the language selector within the article header band, forming a compact, right-aligned control pair. It is slightly smaller than the language selector, taking up around 10–15% of the column width. The toggle is aligned to the same vertical axis as the language selector, with 8–16px of horizontal gap between them, so they read as a unified utility cluster. It shares the same top margin and bottom padding as the header block, sitting at the far right of the title area on larger screens and stacking below the language selector on narrow viewports while still remaining within the top-right area of the header region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored in the header’s right-side utility area, participating in responsive wrapping behavior with the language selector while staying above the article content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section table of contents is placed directly beneath the article header, visually echoing the mockup’s large, centered search bar. It is horizontally centered within the main content column and spans roughly 60–70% of the column’s width, leaving visible margins on both sides inside the column. The TOC appears as a low-profile block with subtle borders or a faint background tint, containing a vertical list of section links. There is a 24–32px gap between the bottom of the header and the top of this TOC block, giving it room to stand out. Each TOC link has 8–12px vertical spacing, and the block itself has 12–16px of internal padding on all sides. Below the TOC, another 32–40px of space separates it from the start of the article body, establishing it as a distinct navigational module before the main reading content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered within the main content column, remaining in the normal scroll flow and not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article body content begins below the section TOC and occupies the full width of the main content column, constrained for readability to a comfortable line length. It starts approximately 32–40px beneath the TOC block. All text is left-aligned, with paragraphs spanning about 80–90% of the column width to preserve generous side margins within the column. Line height is slightly loose, using 1.4–1.6x line spacing with 8–12px vertical spacing between paragraphs. Section headings within the body have 16–24px of top margin and 8px bottom margin, clearly breaking up content without feeling crowded. Any inline lists are indented slightly with consistent bullet spacing. The body extends vertically as needed, forming the main scrollable reading area of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the central portion of the page’s scrollable content within the centered column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The structured sections follow directly after the main body content within the same centered column. They begin approximately 32–40px below the last paragraph of the body, visually signifying a new segment. Each structured section (for example, step-by-step instructions or FAQs) is treated like a subtle block or card: left-aligned, spanning 100% of the column width, with 16–24px of vertical padding inside and 16–24px margin between individual sections. Headings in these sections are slightly more prominent than in-body subheadings, but still subordinate to the main article title. The overall group of structured sections reads as a visually coherent sequence under the article, while maintaining the page’s minimal, flat aesthetic without heavy shadows or outlines.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked directly below the main body content in the normal vertical flow, expanding downward as more structured sections are present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article CTAs block appears beneath the structured sections as a distinct action-oriented band. It starts roughly 32–48px below the final structured section. The block spans the full width of the main content column but usually contains one or several compact buttons or text links aligned to the left. If there are multiple CTAs, they are arranged in a horizontal row with 16–24px of horizontal spacing between them, wrapping to the next line on smaller screens. The CTAs sit on the same dark background as the page, with emphasis coming from button styling rather than a separate card. There is 16–24px of vertical padding within the block (top and bottom) to ensure they feel comfortably tappable and not crowded against adjacent content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, placed directly in the content flow, with responsive wrapping of buttons/links as the main column width changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary contact-support CTA is positioned immediately below the article-ctas block, taking on the role of the single, most prominent action in the lower half of the page. It is left-aligned within the content column and typically appears as a larger, high-contrast button that spans about 30–40% of the column width on desktop and up to nearly full-width on smaller viewports. There is about 16–24px of vertical gap between the CTAs block and this primary button, so it reads as a separate but related step. Additional vertical padding of roughly 12–16px inside the button and generous horizontal padding echo the spacing standards of the mockup’s input field, making it feel substantial and easy to click.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored under the article CTAs within the main content column; scales in width responsively but always remains in the normal scroll flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-options footer sits below the primary contact-support CTA as a more detailed list of alternative support channels. It begins around 24–32px beneath the primary CTA. The block spans the full width of the main content column, containing a vertically stacked list of options (e.g., chat, email, community) each on its own line or row. Items are left-aligned with consistent 8–12px spacing between them, and the overall block has 16–24px of padding at the top and bottom to visually separate it from surrounding content. The design remains simple and text-forward, with small icons optionally aligned to the left of each option but no heavy borders or visual noise, preserving the minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked directly under the primary support CTA within the vertical content flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback panel appears below the contact options, acting as a transitional module between the support actions and the related-articles section. It starts about 32–40px under the last contact option. The panel is horizontally centered within the main content column and spans roughly 60–70% of its width, similar in width to the earlier TOC, creating visual symmetry. It appears as a low-profile, flat card with a subtle border or slightly lighter dark background. Inside, feedback controls (e.g., ‘Was this helpful?’ with Yes/No buttons) are center-aligned, with 12–16px of padding on all sides and 8–16px spacing between the question text and the response buttons. This panel stands out as a distinct, self-contained unit while still maintaining the overall dark, minimalist look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered within the main column, occupying a visually distinct but compact area in the normal scroll order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-articles section sits beneath the feedback panel and is intentionally styled to echo the mockup’s ‘Most Popular Articles’ layout. It begins about 40–48px below the feedback panel. At the top of this section, a bold, uppercase heading such as ‘RELATED ARTICLES’ is left-aligned at the main content column’s left edge. Beneath this heading, after a 16–24px gap, the list of related articles is displayed in two equal-width columns within the same central content column. Each column occupies roughly 45–48% of the column width, with a wide central gutter of about 32–40px between them. Each related article title is a text-only link, left-aligned within its column, with 12–16px vertical spacing between items to prevent crowding. The user’s eye moves down the left column and then scans the right column, mirroring the pattern from the mockup. At the very bottom of this section, a thin horizontal separator line runs across the full width of the main content column, creating a clean visual endpoint for the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the full width of the main content column, with an internal two-column layout for its items that collapses to a single column on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article metadata footer appears directly below the related-articles section, after the thin horizontal separator line, acting as a quiet closing note. It is left-aligned within the main content column and spans its full width, but the content itself (e.g., ‘Last updated’, ‘Article ID’, language note) is typically confined to a single line or two of small, low-emphasis text. There is about 16–24px of vertical space between the separator line and the metadata text, and another 24–32px of bottom padding before the end of the page to ensure it does not feel cramped against the viewport edge. The typography is smaller and slightly dimmer than body text, clearly signaling that this is tertiary information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, always appearing as the final component in the main content column’s vertical flow.

name: Contact Support
title: Contact Support
description: Page for contacting support about streaming, billing, downloads, parental controls, and technical issues, using topic selection, contact method selection, and a unified request form.

core_features: Load contact support configuration based on how the page was opened (generic vs article-specific) using getGenericContactSupportConfig or getContactSupportConfigForArticle.
Display and manage a Topic selector dropdown backed by configuration data, allowing users to confirm or change the support topic.
Provide input fields for subject and detailed description, bound to a SupportRequest draft state.
Provide an error code input field that can be prefilled from article context and always remains editable by the user.
List available contact methods (chat, email, phone, web form) with availability and estimated wait times from configuration, and allow the user to select one.
Conditionally trigger either startSupportChat or submitSupportRequest based on the selected contact method when the user submits the form.
Validate required inputs (topic, contact method, description, and other method-specific requirements) before invoking interfaces.
Display an inline confirmation/status banner showing the result of submitSupportRequest or startSupportChat, including any message returned.
Provide navigation back to the Help Center (index.html) via breadcrumb or explicit link after the user submits or cancels a support request.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Generic Contact Support from Help Center:
1) User clicks a 'Contact support' link in the header from a non-article page (for example, the Help Center home). The application routes to contact_support.html with URL parameters source=generic and sourcePageType set by the originating page.
2) On page initialization, the support-context-loader component reads source=generic and sourcePageType from the URL and calls getGenericContactSupportConfig(sourcePageType).
3) When the configuration returns, the Topic dropdown is populated from the topics array, and the defaultTopic is preselected. Contact methods are populated from the contactMethods array, and the defaultContactMethod (or the first method with isDefault=true) is preselected.
4) The user optionally changes the topic, confirms or switches the contact method, and enters a subject and detailed description. If relevant, the user may also enter an error code.
5) The user clicks the primary action button. If the selected contact method is chat, the page validates required fields and invokes startSupportChat(topic, subject, description, errorCode, sourcePageType, sourceArticleId=null). If the selected contact method is not chat, the page invokes submitSupportRequest(topic, subject, description, contactMethod, errorCode, sourcePageType, sourceArticleId=null).
6) After a successful response, the submission-status-banner displays a confirmation using the returned message, status, and requestId. The user may then click 'Back to Help Center' or a breadcrumb link to navigate to index.html.

Workflow 2 – Contact Support from a Specific Article:
1) While viewing an article, the user clicks a CTA such as 'Contact support about this issue' or selects a specific contact option. The application routes to contact_support.html with URL parameters source=article, articleId, and optionally contactOptionId.
2) On page initialization, the support-context-loader reads source=article, articleId, and contactOptionId from the URL and calls getContactSupportConfigForArticle(articleId, contactOptionId).
3) When the configuration returns, the page sets sourcePageType to the value from the config (expected to be 'article') and stores sourceArticleId from the response. The Topic dropdown is populated from the topics array, with defaultTopic preselected, and the error code field is prefilled with defaultErrorCode (if provided). Contact methods are populated from the contactMethods array, and the preselectedContactMethod is selected if available.
4) The user reviews the prefilled topic and error code, optionally changes the topic, edits or adds a subject and description (for example, describing how the problem occurs on their device), and may modify the error code if needed.
5) The user clicks the primary action button. If the selected contact method is chat, the page invokes startSupportChat(topic, subject, description, errorCode, sourcePageType='article', sourceArticleId). If the selected contact method is not chat, the page invokes submitSupportRequest(topic, subject, description, contactMethod, errorCode, sourcePageType='article', sourceArticleId).
6) On success, the submission-status-banner shows confirmation details. The user can follow offered next steps (for example, a link back to the article via browser back, or a 'Back to Help Center' link to index.html).

Workflow 3 – Switching Contact Methods Before Submitting:
1) After configuration has loaded in either generic or article context, the user changes the selected contact method in the contact-method-selector (for example, from email to chat, or from chat to phone).
2) The support-submit-actions component updates its internal state to reflect the newly selected contact method. It may change the label of the primary action button (for example, 'Start chat' vs 'Submit request') and update displayed wait-time and availability information.
3) If the newly selected contact method is not available (available=false in the configuration), the component disables the primary action button and shows an inline explanation using the contactMethods metadata. The user can select another available contact method.
4) Once a valid, available contact method is selected, the user clicks the primary action button. The page validates inputs and calls either startSupportChat or submitSupportRequest accordingly, including the current topic, subject, description, errorCode, sourcePageType, and sourceArticleId (if any).
5) After submission, the inline status updates and the user can leave the page via breadcrumb navigation to index.html.
interactions: On page initialization, the support-context-loader reads URL parameters (source, sourcePageType, articleId, contactOptionId) and calls either getGenericContactSupportConfig or getContactSupportConfigForArticle. The resulting configuration populates page state for topics, contact methods, and defaults.

When the user selects a topic in the topic-selector dropdown, the component updates the current SupportRequest.topic in page state, which will be used in subsequent calls to submitSupportRequest or startSupportChat.

When the user selects a contact method in the contact-method-selector, the component updates SupportRequest.contact_method in page state and notifies the support-submit-actions component so it can adjust button labeling, disabled states, and any method-specific messaging.
When the user types in the subject field, the support-details-form updates SupportRequest.subject in real time.

When the user types in the description text area, the support-details-form updates SupportRequest.description in real time, and may display character count or validation warnings if the description is too short based on internal validation logic.
When the user edits the error code field, the support-details-form updates SupportRequest.error_code in page state, overriding any prefilled defaultErrorCode from article context.

When the user clicks the primary action button in support-submit-actions and the selected contact method is chat, the component validates that topic and description are present (and any other required fields), and then calls startSupportChat with the current SupportRequest state and context (sourcePageType and sourceArticleId).

When the user clicks the primary action button and the selected contact method is not chat, the support-submit-actions component validates required fields and calls submitSupportRequest with the current SupportRequest state.

When submitSupportRequest returns, the submission-status-banner displays the success or failure state using the success flag, requestId, status, and message, and the page may disable form inputs or keep them editable depending on the returned status.

When startSupportChat returns, the submission-status-banner displays the current status and message. The chatSessionId and requestId can be passed to an external chat container (outside of this page's scope) via an event or callback for establishing the live chat view.
When the user clicks the 'Back to Help Center' link or the Help Center breadcrumb, the page navigates to index.html without preserving the contact-support-specific URL parameters.

If configuration loading fails (for example, getGenericContactSupportConfig or getContactSupportConfigForArticle returns an error), the support-context-loader triggers an error state that the submission-status-banner or a dedicated error display component can show, and disables form interactions until the user retries or navigates away.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is primarily determined by URL parameters and the results of the configuration interfaces. On every load of contact_support.html, the page reads the following parameters: source, sourcePageType, articleId, and contactOptionId. If source is 'article', the page ignores any sourcePageType value from the URL for configuration purposes and instead calls getContactSupportConfigForArticle(articleId, contactOptionId). The response's sourcePageType and sourceArticleId values are stored in internal state and are used when calling submitSupportRequest or startSupportChat. If source is not 'article' (or is missing), the page treats the flow as generic and calls getGenericContactSupportConfig using sourcePageType from the URL. The returned sourcePageType is stored as part of the SupportRequest draft state for analytics and routing purposes. In both cases, the page initializes a SupportRequest draft with topic and contact_method set from defaultTopic, defaultContactMethod, or preselectedContactMethod from the configuration. The error code field is initialized from defaultErrorCode only when provided by the article-specific configuration. Subsequent user interactions (changing topic, changing contact method, typing subject/description/error code) update this in-memory SupportRequest draft; these changes are not persisted to URL parameters to keep URLs stable and simple. When the user submits, the current SupportRequest draft values, along with sourcePageType and sourceArticleId derived from configuration, are passed into submitSupportRequest or startSupportChat. After a successful submission, the SupportRequest.status can be updated locally to reflect the returned status. Navigating back to index.html clears the context because no state is stored outside the current page and URL; re-entering the Contact Support page always re-derives state from the incoming URL parameters and configuration interfaces.

components.functionality: Determines how the Contact Support page was opened by reading URL parameters (source, sourcePageType, articleId, contactOptionId), calls either getGenericContactSupportConfig or getContactSupportConfigForArticle, and normalizes the returned configuration into a common internal state structure used by other components.
event_handlers: onInit
onConfigLoaded
onConfigLoadError

components.functionality: Displays navigation breadcrumbs including a link back to the Help Center root (index.html). After a successful submission or at any time, the user can click the breadcrumb or 'Back to Help Center' link to leave the page.
event_handlers: onBackToHelpCenterClick

components.functionality: Renders a Topic dropdown populated from the configuration's topics list, selects the defaultTopic on load, and updates the SupportRequest.topic field when changed by the user.
event_handlers: onTopicChange

components.functionality: Displays available contact methods (chat, email, phone, web_form) from the configuration's contactMethods list, including their labels, availability, and estimatedWaitMinutes. It manages which method is currently selected and updates SupportRequest.contact_method accordingly.
event_handlers: onContactMethodChange

components.functionality: Shows auxiliary information for the currently selected contact method, such as estimated wait time and whether the method is currently available. It responds to changes from contact-method-selector and adjusts messaging and disabled states in other components.
event_handlers: onSelectedContactMethodUpdate

components.functionality: Provides input fields for subject, description, and error code. It initializes the error code field from configuration (defaultErrorCode) when present and keeps all fields synchronized with the SupportRequest draft state.
event_handlers: onSubjectInput
onDescriptionInput
onErrorCodeInput

components.functionality: Receives validation results from support-submit-actions and displays inline validation messages for missing or invalid fields (topic, contact method, description, and any other required fields) without submitting to any interface.
event_handlers: onValidationResultUpdate

components.functionality: Renders the primary action button (text determined by the selected contact method) and orchestrates request submission. For chat, it calls startSupportChat with topic, subject, description, errorCode, sourcePageType, and sourceArticleId. For non-chat contact methods, it calls submitSupportRequest with topic, subject, description, contactMethod, errorCode, sourcePageType, and sourceArticleId. It performs client-side validation before invoking interfaces and emits validation results and submission outcomes.
event_handlers: onPrimaryActionClick
onBeforeSubmitValidate
onSubmitSuccess
onSubmitError

components.functionality: Displays status information after configuration loading or submission events. After submitSupportRequest or startSupportChat, it shows success or error messages, the requestId, and the status returned from the interface. It can also show configuration load errors triggered by support-context-loader.
event_handlers: onConfigLoadError
onSubmitSuccess
onSubmitError

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a centered main content column. The article list appears to use two equal-width columns (approximately 6 + 6) with a central gutter.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strictly top-to-bottom, centered content flow on a full-width dark background. Immediately under the global site header, the breadcrumb navigation anchors the user in the help center hierarchy. Beneath that, a potential submission status banner appears when relevant. The main focal region mimics the mockup’s large, centered search area by using the topic selector as the hero module, visually centered and wider than typical form controls. Below this, a section heading introduces the contact options area, which uses a two-column layout for the contact method selector (left) and supporting metadata (right), echoing the mockup’s two-column article list. Under the two-column section, a full-width details form spans the main content column, with validation messages directly above it and the submit actions anchored at the bottom of the form. A thin horizontal separator or generous bottom padding visually closes the page.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strictly top-to-bottom, centered content flow on a full-width dark background. Immediately under the global site header, the breadcrumb navigation anchors the user in the help center hierarchy. Beneath that, a potential submission status banner appears when relevant. The main focal region mimics the mockup’s large, centered search area by using the topic selector as the hero module, visually centered and wider than typical form controls. Below this, a section heading introduces the contact options area, which uses a two-column layout for the contact method selector (left) and supporting metadata (right), echoing the mockup’s two-column article list. Under the two-column section, a full-width details form spans the main content column, with validation messages directly above it and the submit actions anchored at the bottom of the form. A thin horizontal separator or generous bottom padding visually closes the page.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies roughly the top 8–10% of the viewport height. The breadcrumb and optional status banner use a relatively small vertical footprint, acting as a light prelude to the main content. The topic selector area then takes up a larger vertical block, approximately 20–25% of the viewport height, with generous padding above and below, making it the dominant interactive element. The contact options section and its two-column layout consume the mid-page area with clear vertical separation from the topic selector (about 32–48px) and from the details form below (another 32–48px). The details form and submit actions occupy the remaining vertical space, with comfortable but not excessive spacing between form groups (about 24px) and ample bottom margin (48–64px) before the final horizontal separator or page end.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately spacious: no element feels cramped, but the page still presents a substantial amount of information. Vertical spacing between stacked elements (breadcrumb, status banner, topic selector, headings, form sections) mostly follows a 24–32px rhythm. Within the two-column contact section, the column gutter is wide enough (approximately 32–40px) to keep each column legible. The main content column is narrower than the viewport (roughly 60–70% of page width on desktop, capped at around 1040–1120px), maintaining generous side margins and a cinematic framed feel. Form fields are grouped logically with about 16–24px between related controls, preserving readability without making the page feel sparse.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow is strongly vertical and mirrors the mockup’s top-down pattern. The eye moves from the global header to the breadcrumb in the top-left of the content column, then quickly down to the central hero-like topic selector, which acts as the main focal point, similar to the original search bar. Any helper text around the topic selector is read next. From there, attention is pulled down to a bold, left-aligned section heading introducing the contact options, then naturally scans down the left column (contact methods) before moving across to the right column (metadata), echoing the mockup’s left-then-right article scan. Finally, the eye flows down into the details form, reads any validation messages near the top of that section, and ends at the submit action bar, which anchors the bottom of the main content. When present, the submission status banner briefly intercepts the flow just under the breadcrumb, then the user returns to the standard top-to-bottom journey.

layout.overall_description: The Contact Support page sits on a full-width, near-black background, with all interactive elements contained inside a centered main content column that feels like a narrow, focused stage. At the very top of this column, directly beneath the site’s horizontal navigation, a subtle breadcrumb trail appears in the top-left, signaling location without drawing much visual weight. Just below it, a status banner space is reserved to surface any success or failure state after submission as a full-width strip across the content column. As the eye moves downward, it arrives at a visually dominant, centrally aligned topic selector block that echoes a streaming service’s powerful global search: it spans roughly two-thirds of the column, is horizontally centered, and surrounded by generous vertical padding so it occupies a significant portion of the upper viewport. This block is the main decision point about what kind of issue the user has. Beneath this hero area, the layout tightens into a more structured section with a left-aligned heading introducing contact options. Immediately below this heading, the content splits into two equal columns: on the left, a vertical stack of contact method choices; on the right, explanatory metadata like availability and expected response times. The wide gutter between these columns and the dark background keep the two sets of information distinct yet clearly paired. Below the two-column region, the layout recombines into a single, full-width stream for the support details form: fields are cleanly stacked with optional two-column rows for shorter inputs, all aligned to the same content column edges. If any validation messages are present, they appear as a concise block just above the form, ensuring issues are caught before the user scans down to the submit actions. Finally, at the bottom of the form, a low-profile but clear action bar holds the primary submit button, slightly right-weighted within the column. A thin, subtle horizontal divider or a deep bottom margin separates this interactive area from whatever footer content follows, mirroring the mockup’s understated terminal line and leaving the page feeling orderly and deliberate.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support context loader appears as an overlay that covers the entire main content column, starting just below the global site navigation and extending down to the bottom of the visible support page content. It dims the underlying dark background slightly (e.g., by adding a translucent black layer) while keeping the overall page structure still faintly visible beneath, preserving the sense of location. At the center of the viewport—both horizontally and vertically relative to the visible area—a minimalist spinner or progress indicator is displayed, with a small label if needed. The overlay respects the same horizontal bounds as the centered content column on large screens, but on smaller screens it effectively fills the full viewport width, echoing the app-like feeling of a streaming service interface. When the loader is active, all other interactive components (topic selector, contact method columns, form fields, and actions) are visually present but non-interactive beneath the overlay, reinforcing that the loader state is temporarily primary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a centered overlay fixed relative to the viewport, covering the full height of the contact support content area. It scrolls with the page only when the user has already scrolled before it appears, maintaining its center position in the visible window until loading completes, then disappears entirely without leaving extra spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation is positioned at the top-left of the main content column, immediately below the global header and aligned with the column’s left edge. It stretches horizontally only as wide as its text requires, leaving generous empty space to its right within the dark canvas. Vertically, it occupies a compact bar of about 24–32px height (including padding) with roughly 16px of top padding from the bottom edge of the global header and about 8–12px of space below before any status banner or the topic selector block. The typography is small and understated, using a subdued shade of white or gray that keeps it legible but clearly secondary to the larger, bolder elements below. On narrow viewports, the breadcrumb text wraps gracefully within the same left-aligned column, but it always remains the first visible support component in the vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and non-sticky. It scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page, without reserving fixed vertical space once it leaves the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission status banner occupies a full-width strip within the centered content column, sitting directly below the breadcrumb navigation and above the main topic selector area. It spans the same width as the rest of the content column, with consistent left and right alignment, and uses a low-profile height driven by its message content—typically one to two lines of text with 12–16px vertical padding, resulting in a total height of roughly 40–64px. When visible, it inserts about 16px of space below the breadcrumb and leaves another 16–24px of space beneath it before the topic selector, ensuring it is clearly detached from both elements. The banner’s background uses a slightly lighter or tinted tone against the dark page (for success, warning, or error) with a thin top and bottom border, making it immediately noticeable without overwhelming the hero region below. If no status needs to be shown, this banner collapses entirely and the topic selector moves up, maintaining a tight vertical rhythm at the top of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and non-sticky. It appears and disappears dynamically in the stack between breadcrumb and topic selector, pushing adjacent components up or down but never overlaying them.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic selector is the visual and interactive centerpiece of the page, occupying the same conceptual role as the large search bar in the mockup. It is positioned below the breadcrumb and any status banner, centered within the main content column both horizontally and visually within the upper third of the viewport. On desktop, the primary interactive control (for example, a dropdown or segmented set of topic pills) spans approximately 60–70% of the column’s width, with a maximum width around 640px to keep it readable, and is horizontally centered so equal dark space appears on either side. A concise page title and/or label for the control is placed directly above it, aligned to center or soft-left within the same width, while an optional one-line helper or explanatory text sits just below, also centered, echoing the mockup’s search helper line. The entire topic selection block is surrounded by generous vertical padding—roughly 48–64px above and 40–56px below—so that this region takes up about 20–25% of the visible page height when first loaded, clearly drawing the eye and signaling that selecting a topic is the first major step. On smaller screens, the topic selector grows to nearly 100% of the content width with 16–24px horizontal padding from the viewport edges, maintaining the centered, dominant feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, responsive, and non-sticky. It remains part of the normal scroll flow, shrinking proportionally in height as screen size decreases, and expands to full column width on mobile while keeping its centered hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact method selector lives within the main contact options section, directly below a left-aligned section heading such as ‘Contact options’, which itself is positioned under the topic selector with about 32–48px of vertical separation. Beneath this heading, the content splits into two equal-width columns across the main content column: the contact method selector occupies the entire left column, starting flush under the heading’s baseline. It spans approximately 50% of the content column’s width, with a wide central gutter of about 32–40px separating it from the metadata column on the right. Within the left column, available contact methods (e.g., chat, email, phone) are presented as a vertical stack of text-first items—cards or radio rows—with 16–24px spacing between each, mirroring the mockup’s text-only list style but adapted for interactive selection. Each method aligns left within the column and uses comfortable internal padding (around 12–16px vertically and 16–24px horizontally), creating a clear, scannable list from top to bottom. This left column becomes the primary focal area of the two-column region, with the right column read after the left, following the mockup’s left-to-right scanning behavior. On tablet and mobile widths, the two columns stack, and the contact method selector shifts to full width above the metadata, keeping its role as first in this section’s flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as part of a responsive two-column grid: on wider screens it occupies the left half of the section; on narrower screens it drops into a full-width block above the metadata panel, maintaining consistent left alignment with the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact method metadata panel is paired with the selector in a two-column layout and occupies the entire right column in the contact options section. It aligns vertically so that its top edge is level with the top of the contact method selector in the left column, creating a cohesive horizontal band across the page. The panel spans roughly the remaining 50% of the content column width, separated from the selector by a wide gutter (about 32–40px) that ensures each column remains visually distinct. Inside this right column, explanatory text—such as operating hours, expected response times, or eligibility notes—flows in a vertical stack with 8–16px spacing between lines or paragraphs, maintaining a compact but readable density. The metadata text is left-aligned, echoing the utilitarian, content-first feel, and the panel height flexes to match its content without unnecessary padding. This column rarely exceeds the height of the contact method list, so the eye naturally reads down the left column of options first, then moves right for clarifying details, mirroring the mockup’s article reading pattern. On narrow screens, this panel collapses beneath the contact method selector, becoming a full-width text block of supporting information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shares a responsive two-column grid with the contact method selector: on large screens, it is fixed in the right half of the section; on smaller screens, it stacks below and spans full width, following the selector in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support validation messages component sits at the top of the details form section, acting as a slim, full-width band inside the main content column that immediately precedes the form fields. It is positioned below the contact options section with about 32–48px of vertical spacing to clearly indicate a transition from choosing a method to specifying details. When visible, it spans the same left and right boundaries as the form, aligning perfectly with the column edges. The message block uses a compact vertical footprint—typically one or two lines of text with 8–12px padding above and below—resulting in a total height of around 32–48px, enough to be noticeable without feeling bulky. It may include a subtle colored accent (border or icon) against the dark theme to distinguish error or warning states, but the typography remains in keeping with the minimalist style. The messages are stacked so that they are encountered before users re-engage with the individual form fields below, ensuring that validation feedback is integrated into the natural downward reading flow. When there are no validation issues, this band disappears entirely and the top of the form fields moves up to close the gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, appearing only when needed and occupying full width above the form. It does not overlay content; instead it pushes the form down while present and collapses without leaving residual space when hidden.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support details form occupies a full-width block within the centered content column and sits directly below the validation messages (or below the contact options section when there are no messages). It is separated from the section above by a generous margin of roughly 32–48px, creating a clear visual boundary between choosing a contact mode and describing the issue. Within this block, form fields are organized in a clean, linear sequence that favors readability: shorter fields such as account information or subject can be arranged in a loose two-column layout per row on desktop (each field taking about 48–50% of the column width with a 16–24px gutter), while larger fields such as detailed descriptions or attachments span the full form width. Vertical spacing between field groups maintains a consistent 16–24px rhythm, preventing visual crowding. All fields align to a common left column edge and stay within the same horizontal bounds as the topic selector above, reinforcing the unified column structure. The form’s total vertical height is flexible, growing as necessary without breaking the top-to-bottom flow. On small screens, all fields stack into a single column at 100% width within the content margins, preserving the sense of a continuous, scrollable form area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and scrollable. The form grows vertically with content, and any internal field-level layout (one or two columns) is responsive, collapsing to a single column on narrow viewports while remaining anchored to the same main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support submit actions component forms a low-profile action bar at the bottom of the details form, acting as the clear endpoint of the page’s main interaction flow. It is positioned directly beneath the final form field with approximately 24px of vertical space separating it from the last input group. The bar uses the full width of the content column, aligning its left and right edges with the form fields above. Within this area, the primary submit button is right-aligned, with any secondary actions (such as cancel or reset) placed to its left, maintaining a compact horizontal cluster. This cluster sits with 8–16px of padding from the bottom of the component, ensuring it feels anchored but not cramped. The action bar itself has minimal visual decoration—relying on button styling rather than a heavy background—but it leaves a generous margin of about 48–64px below before the page’s concluding horizontal separator or footer begins, mirroring the airy spacing at the bottom of the mockup. On mobile, the actions can center or remain right-aligned while still respecting the content margins, ensuring a clear, uncluttered call to action at the end of the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, fixed to the bottom of the form section and non-sticky. It scrolls naturally with the page; it does not remain pinned to the viewport, but it is visually emphasized by its position as the final element in the main vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support submit actions component forms a low-profile action bar at the bottom of the details form, acting as the clear endpoint of the page’s main interaction flow. It is positioned directly beneath the final form field with approximately 24px of vertical space separating it from the last input group. The bar uses the full width of the content column, aligning its left and right edges with the form fields above. Within this area, the primary submit button is right-aligned, with any secondary actions (such as cancel or reset) placed to its left, maintaining a compact horizontal cluster. This cluster sits with 8–16px of padding from the bottom of the component, ensuring it feels anchored but not cramped. The action bar itself has minimal visual decoration—relying on button styling rather than a heavy background—but it leaves a generous margin of about 48–64px below before the page’s concluding horizontal separator or footer begins, mirroring the airy spacing at the bottom of the mockup. component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, fixed to the bottom of the form section and non-sticky. It scrolls naturally with the page; it does not remain pinned to the viewport, but it is visually emphasized by its position as the final element in the main vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission status banner occupies a full-width strip within the centered content column, sitting directly below the breadcrumb navigation and above the main topic selector area. It spans the same width as the rest of the content column, with consistent left and right alignment, and uses a low-profile height driven by its message content—typically one to two lines of text with 12–16px vertical padding, resulting in a total height of roughly 40–64px. The banner’s background uses a slightly lighter or tinted tone against the dark page (for success, warning, or error) with a thin top and bottom border, making it immediately noticeable without overwhelming the hero region below. If no status needs to be shown, this banner collapses entirely and the topic selector moves up, maintaining a tight vertical rhythm at the top of the page.

name: Saved Articles
title: Saved Articles
description: Page that lists all help center articles the user has saved, with tools to open, sort, and remove saved items.

core_features: Load and display the list of saved articles using the getSavedArticlesList interface, respecting the current sortBy URL parameter.
Allow users to change the sort order (e.g., by date saved or by category) and reload the list with the updated sortBy value.
Enable users to open a saved article in the Article Detail page (article.html) by clicking the article title or summary, passing articleId and the current UI language.
Allow users to remove individual articles from their saved list using the unsaveArticle interface and update the list and saved count accordingly.
Provide basic list management behaviors such as handling empty states when no saved articles are returned.
user_workflows: View saved articles: 1) User navigates to saved_articles.html (optionally with sortBy in the URL); 2) Page reads sortBy from the URL (or uses a default if missing); 3) Page calls getSavedArticlesList(sortBy); 4) The returned articles are rendered in the saved articles list with title, summary, category name, and saved date.

Change sort order: 1) User interacts with the sort control (e.g., selects a different sort mode); 2) Sort control updates the sortBy URL parameter and triggers a route change or reload; 3) On load, the page reads the new sortBy from the URL; 4) Page calls getSavedArticlesList(sortBy); 5) The list re-renders according to the new order or grouping.

Open a saved article: 1) User clicks on the title or summary of a saved article in the list; 2) Click handler reads the articleId from the item data and the current UI language from the page context; 3) Browser navigates to article.html with articleId and lang as URL parameters; 4) Article Detail page loads the requested article.

Remove a single saved article: 1) User clicks an 'Unsave' or equivalent control on a specific saved article item; 2) Click handler calls unsaveArticle(articleId) for that item; 3) On success, the component removes the item from the in-memory list and may update any shared saved-count indicator using the returned totalSavedCount; 4) If the resulting list is empty, the page switches to its empty state view.

Bulk clean-up of saved articles (sequential unsaving): 1) User optionally selects multiple articles using item-level controls or uses a page-level action to clear visible items; 2) For each selected article, the page calls unsaveArticle(articleId) one by one; 3) After each successful response, the corresponding item is removed from the list; 4) When all selected items are processed, the page reflects the final state of the list and updates any saved-count indicator using the latest totalSavedCount.
interactions: On initial page load or when the URL changes, the page reads the sortBy URL parameter and triggers getSavedArticlesList with that value to populate the list.

When the user changes the sort selection in the sort control, the component updates the sortBy value in the URL (e.g., via query string) and reloads or re-renders the page based on the new sortBy, which in turn triggers getSavedArticlesList.

When the user clicks on a saved article’s title or summary area, the click handler navigates to article.html, adding articleId from the clicked item and the current UI language code as lang in the query string.

When the user clicks the 'Unsave' control on a list item, the click handler calls unsaveArticle with that item’s articleId, then, if success is true, removes the item from the local list and refreshes any saved-count indicator using totalSavedCount.
If unsaveArticle returns success as false, the page displays an error state or message to the user using the returned message, without removing the article from the list.

If getSavedArticlesList returns an empty articles array, the list component hides the list and shows an empty state message and, optionally, a navigation link back to the Help Center home or other discovery entry points.

If getSavedArticlesList returns articles grouped by category based on the sortBy mode, the list component respects the implied grouping when rendering, using only the interface’s data (e.g., categoryName) for grouping labels.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is controlled via the sortBy URL parameter. On every navigation to saved_articles.html, the page reads sortBy from the query string. If sortBy is missing or invalid, the page uses a configured default sort mode and passes that value into getSavedArticlesList. The response from getSavedArticlesList (including the effective sortBy and the articles array) becomes the in-memory state used to render the list. When the user changes the sort selection, the sort control updates the sortBy value in the URL (e.g., saved_articles.html?sortBy=<value>), which causes the page to re-run getSavedArticlesList with the new sortBy and re-render the list accordingly. No saved-articles state is persisted via localStorage or other client storage; the source of truth is always the API response for the current sortBy. Navigation to article.html includes articleId and lang parameters in the URL, but does not alter the saved_articles.html state itself. The unsaveArticle interface is used to mutate the backend saved state; after a successful unsaveArticle call, the page updates its in-memory list and may also update UI elements such as a saved-count indicator using the returned totalSavedCount, without changing the sortBy parameter.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page load, reads URL parameters, calls getSavedArticlesList with the current sortBy value, and provides the resulting data to child components. It also listens for sort changes and triggers list reloads.
event_handlers: onPageLoad
onUrlChange
onSortChange

components.functionality: Abstracts the getSavedArticlesList interface call. Given a sortBy value (from the URL or a child event), it calls getSavedArticlesList(sortBy), stores the returned sortBy and articles in a shared state, and exposes loading and error statuses to other components.
event_handlers: onLoadStart
onLoadSuccess
onLoadError

components.functionality: Displays a control (such as a dropdown or segmented buttons) for selecting the sort order of saved articles. It initializes its selected option from the current sortBy URL parameter or the loader’s returned sortBy.
