When the user selects a different option, it updates the sortBy value in the URL and notifies the page controller to reload data.
event_handlers: onChange
onInitializeFromUrl

components.functionality: Renders the list of saved articles provided by the data loader. For each article in the getSavedArticlesList response, it shows a row with title, summary, categoryName, and savedAt, and embeds item-level controls such as navigation to the article detail and unsaving. It also handles grouped rendering if the current sortBy mode implies grouping (e.g., by category). When the articles array is empty, it hides the list rows and shows an empty-state view.
event_handlers: onItemClick
onUnsaveClick
onEmptyStateRender

components.functionality: Represents a single saved article entry. It receives articleId, title, summary, categoryName, and savedAt as props from the list. Clicking the title or summary triggers navigation to article.html with articleId and lang. Clicking the unsave control invokes the unsaveArticle interface and, on success, notifies the parent list to remove this item.
event_handlers: onTitleClick
onSummaryClick
onUnsaveClick
onUnsaveSuccess
onUnsaveFailure

components.functionality: Displays a dedicated view when getSavedArticlesList returns no saved articles. It may include explanatory text and a navigation action (for example, a button or link) that takes the user back to the Help Center home or another discovery page via a normal navigation link.
event_handlers: onNavigateToHelpCenter

components.functionality: Optionally provides bulk management actions for the list, such as unsaving multiple selected articles. It reads the currently selected articleIds from the list and calls unsaveArticle(articleId) for each selected item in sequence, updating the list and any saved-count indicator after each successful response.
event_handlers: onBulkUnsaveClick
onBulkUnsaveProgress
onBulkUnsaveComplete

components.functionality: Centralizes calls to the unsaveArticle interface. When invoked with an articleId (from a single item or from the bulk actions toolbar), it executes unsaveArticle(articleId), interprets the result, updates the total saved count in shared state using totalSavedCount when success is true, and propagates success or failure events back to the invoking component.
event_handlers: onInvokeUnsave
onUnsaveSuccess
onUnsaveError

components.functionality: Synchronizes the saved count and active state of any global 'Saved articles' navigation link or badge with this page. After unsaveArticle operations, it reads totalSavedCount from the response and updates any shared display for the saved-articles count. It also marks the current page as active in the header navigation when the URL matches saved_articles.html.
event_handlers: onPageLoad
onUnsaveSuccess

components.functionality: Handles navigation from a saved article item to article.html. Given an articleId from a clicked list item and the current UI language code from the global context, it constructs the target URL (article.html with articleId and lang as query parameters) and performs the navigation.
event_handlers: onNavigateToArticle

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a centered main content column. The article list appears to use two equal-width columns (approximately 6 + 6) with a central gutter.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a strictly vertical, top-to-bottom flow mirroring the mockup: global header/navigation at the very top (outside this page), followed by a centered main content column. Within the main column, the first visible element is the page-level context ("Saved Articles" heading and brief helper text) centered horizontally. Directly beneath that is the primary interaction band for this page: a horizontally arranged tools row containing bulk actions on the left and sort control on the right, spanning most of the content width. Below this tools row sits a left-aligned section heading introducing the list of saved items. The saved articles themselves are displayed in a two-column list similar to the mockup’s article layout: two equal-width vertical columns of text-first items with a central gutter, all within the centered content column. If there are no saved items, an empty state occupies the list area instead.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a strictly vertical, top-to-bottom flow mirroring the mockup: global header/navigation at the very top (outside this page), followed by a centered main content column. Within the main column, the first visible element is the page-level context ("Saved Articles" heading and brief helper text) centered horizontally. Directly beneath that is the primary interaction band for this page: a horizontally arranged tools row containing bulk actions on the left and sort control on the right, spanning most of the content width. Below this tools row sits a left-aligned section heading introducing the list of saved items. The saved articles themselves are displayed in a two-column list similar to the mockup’s article layout: two equal-width vertical columns of text-first items with a central gutter, all within the centered content column. If there are no saved items, an empty state occupies the list area instead.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global top navigation consumes around 8–10% of the viewport height. The Saved Articles header plus its helper text and the tools row beneath it together occupy a visually substantial block—roughly 20–25% of the viewport height—making this control area the focal interactive zone. The remaining vertical space is devoted to the saved articles list (or empty state), with generous margins above the list heading (approximately 32–48px) and consistent spacing between list items (16–24px). Horizontally, all page-specific content is constrained to a centered column with a max width of about 960–1120px, leaving wide left and right margins against the full-width dark background. Within this column, the two list columns split the usable width approximately 50/50 with a clear central gutter.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately spaced: the page avoids cramming controls together while still showing a meaningful number of items per viewport. The page header text and helper line are given ample breathing room above and below (24–48px gaps), and the tools row has comfortable padding around controls. Each saved article item has enough vertical padding (approximately 12–16px top and bottom) and a subtle separation line, ensuring scannability without feeling sparse. The two-column layout increases information density horizontally but maintains legibility thanks to generous gutters and margins.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey begins at the top-left with the global brand/nav, then drops down into the centered "Saved Articles" page header. From there, the eye moves down slightly to the tools row—the primary action area—where it first notices the left-side bulk actions (when active) and then the right-aligned sort control. The next strong anchor is the left-aligned section heading above the list, which shifts attention slightly left within the column. After reading the heading, the eye moves down into the two-column list, reading vertically down the left column of saved items first, then scanning the right column. In each item, attention moves from the title on the left to the secondary 'Unsave' affordance on the right. Overall flow is top-to-bottom, left-to-right, with clear anchors at each stage to guide progression through the content.

layout.overall_description: The Saved Articles page sits on a full-width dark background with a centered main content column, echoing a streaming-style help center. Below the global navigation, the user encounters a spacious header zone in the middle of the screen: the title “Saved Articles” is centered at the top of this zone in high-contrast white text, with a short explanatory line underneath in a slightly dimmer tone, both surrounded by generous vertical whitespace. Directly beneath this, but still within the same visual block, a horizontal tools row spans most of the content width: bulk actions sit to the left, and a compact sort control to the right, together forming the main interaction band. After a distinct vertical gap, a left-aligned section heading like “ALL SAVED ARTICLES” anchors the beginning of the content section. Below the heading, the layout splits into two equal-width vertical columns of saved article links, separated by a comfortable gutter. Each item is a text-first row with the article title aligned to the left and a subtle unsave control aligned to the far right, all resting within lightly padded rows on the dark canvas. When there are no saved items, the two-column list is replaced by a centered empty state message that occupies the middle of the content column, keeping the same vertical structure. The entire page feels focused and cinematic, with strong vertical flow and an emphasis on clarity and readability over visual ornamentation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the main content column beneath the global navigation. It positions all Saved Articles content within a centered container that spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width up to a max width of about 960–1120px, leaving substantial dark margins on the left and right. Vertically, it introduces a top padding of approximately 48–64px below the global header, creating a clean visual break, and a bottom padding of around 64px before the end of the page. Within this container, it arranges the content in a linear stack: first the page header (title and helper text), then the tools row (bulk actions and sort control), then the section heading, followed by either the two-column saved-articles-list or the empty-state block. The controller ensures that on smaller screens the two columns collapse into a single column while preserving generous vertical spacing and the overall top-to-bottom flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a normal scrolling container for the page content, centered in the viewport. It does not fix or float; instead, it provides the structural frame for all visible components within the standard document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader occupies the same general vertical region reserved for the list of saved articles, just below the section heading. When data is loading, it temporarily replaces the saved-articles-list with a centered loading treatment. Visually, this appears as either a slim, subtle centered spinner or one to two rows of skeleton placeholders that align with the two-column layout: two rectangular skeleton blocks per row, each matching the approximate width and height of a saved-article-item. These skeleton or loading elements are horizontally centered within the main content column and maintain the same left and right margins and column gutter as the final list. The top of the loading indication sits about 24–32px below the section heading, mirroring the spacing that normal list items would use.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual in the final, loaded state; it dynamically swaps the saved-articles-list area with a loading indication but does not alter the overall layout frame or scroll behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved articles list fills the main content column area below the section heading (e.g., “ALL SAVED ARTICLES”). Its top edge begins roughly 24–32px beneath the heading, creating a clear separation. The list is arranged as two equal-width vertical columns: the left column starts aligned with the left edge of the content column, and the right column begins after a central gutter of about 32–40px. Each column takes up approximately 45–48% of the content width, ensuring comfortable margins between columns and the container edges. Items stack vertically within each column, with roughly 16–24px of vertical spacing between them (achieved via padding and/or subtle dividers). The left column generally fills from top to bottom before the user scrolls downward to reveal more rows that continue in both columns. On smaller screens, the layout simplifies to a single vertical column centered within the same max-width container, with items taking full column width. The list extends downwards as long as needed and is the main scrollable body of the page content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard scrollable content block within the page controller. It expands vertically according to the number of items without fixed height, and does not use sticky headers inside the list; it simply follows the page’s normal scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each saved article item appears as a horizontally oriented, text-led row within one of the two list columns. The item spans the full width of its column, with internal padding of approximately 12–16px top and bottom and 16–24px left and right. The primary article title is left-aligned at the top of the row in high-contrast text and can wrap onto two lines if needed while maintaining the same left edge alignment as other items. On the far right of the same row, aligned to the vertical center, sits a compact unsave affordance (e.g., an icon button or text link like “Remove”), visually separated from the title by flexible space so the two never feel cramped. Optionally, a small line of metadata (e.g., category or last updated) can appear beneath the title in a dimmer text color, left-aligned and taking one line of space, leaving the right side reserved for the unsave control. Between items, a very subtle divider line or a slightly darker bottom border runs full column width, keeping rows distinguishable without adding visual noise. On hover or focus, the entire row darkens slightly to indicate interactivity, but the basic geometry remains a flat, unobtrusive card aligned tightly to the column grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the list columns, each item grows vertically based on content but maintains its horizontal alignment. It does not float or stick; on click, it may trigger navigation or an unsave action but the spatial positioning remains within the normal flow except for a brief removal animation when unsaved.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state replaces the saved-articles-list area whenever the user has no saved items. It is positioned within the same main content column, directly below where the list’s section heading appears. Instead of two columns, the empty state is a single, centered block. The top of this block begins roughly 24–32px beneath the section heading, matching the spacing used for the list. The content is center-aligned and stacked: a concise heading (e.g., “No saved articles yet”) at the top, followed by a short explanatory line in slightly dimmer text, and optionally a subtle inline link back to popular help topics or search. The block’s width is constrained to about 50–60% of the main content column, keeping it visually tight and focused in the middle. There is generous vertical padding above and below the empty state text (32–48px) so that the surrounding dark background and central composition communicate a calm, uncluttered message. On taller viewports, the empty state will appear roughly in the upper-middle of the screen as the user scrolls slightly past the header and tools row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same structural space as the list and scrolls normally. It does not fix or float; instead, it conditionally renders in place of the list content based on data, without changing the surrounding layout margins or headings.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bulk actions component lives in the tools row immediately beneath the page header, aligned to the left edge of the main content column. It shares a horizontal line with the right-aligned sort control. When visible, it may include elements such as a subtle checkbox for 'Select all' and a primary text or icon button for 'Remove selected', arranged from left to right with around 8–16px spacing between controls. The entire bulk actions area occupies roughly 40–50% of the content column width on desktop, leaving at least a 16–24px gap between its rightmost control and the sort control. Vertically, it is given 8–12px of vertical padding within the tools row to match other control paddings. When no items are selected, the bulk actions area either collapses to a minimal 'Select multiple' affordance or is visually subdued, but its left alignment and general footprint ensure that the tools row still feels anchored. On small screens, bulk actions appear above the sort control in a stacked arrangement, preserving a 16–24px vertical gap and left alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the standard flow within the tools row. It may expand or contract slightly as selection state changes, but it does not become sticky; as users scroll past the tools row, bulk actions scroll out of view with the rest of the header controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The unsave handler has no direct visual footprint; it operates behind the saved-article-item elements. When the user activates an unsave control within an item, this handler processes the action and then either removes the item from the list or triggers a subtle visual transition (such as the row fading and collapsing vertically). Its presence is felt through the disappearance of items and potential updates to bulk selection states, but it does not introduce new visible elements or change the alignment of existing components beyond removing the corresponding row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial in the static layout. It only affects layout indirectly by causing saved-article-item components to be removed or updated within the list, with the surrounding items naturally closing the gap under the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component synchronizes the Saved Articles page with the global navigation state. Visually, its effect is limited to ensuring that the appropriate item in the top navigation (e.g., a 'Saved' or 'My Articles' link) appears in its active state. That active styling appears in the global, full-width nav bar at the top of the screen, above the page controller. The component itself does not render any content inside the main content column and does not occupy space between the page header, tools row, or list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual within the page’s main content area. It influences only the styling of an existing nav item in the fixed global header and does not change sizing, spacing, or scroll behavior within the Saved Articles page body.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article navigation component does not have its own dedicated visual block; instead, it wires click or tap actions on each saved-article-item to open the corresponding article detail view, typically in the same tab. From a layout perspective, it ensures that the entire row or at least the title portion of each item behaves as a link, with hover and focus states already defined by the item’s styling. It does not add additional buttons or links beyond the existing text treatments, so the clean, text-first, two-column layout remains uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and inline with existing items. It does not anchor or reposition anything; it simply defines interaction behavior for components already placed within the standard grid-based layout.

name: Payment Settings
title: Payment Settings
description: Page for viewing current payment settings, updating the billing country, seeing payment method details, and returning to the Help Center with context from related help articles.

core_features: Load current payment settings and selectable options by calling getPaymentSettingsView on page initialization.
Display a read-only summary of the current billing country and payment method using data from getPaymentSettingsView.currentSettings.
Provide a billing country dropdown bound to the current billing country, populated from getPaymentSettingsView.billingCountryOptions.
Allow the user to update the billing country by submitting the selected value to setBillingCountry and reflecting the result in the UI.
Show contextual help guidance using getPaymentSettingsView.helpArticleHints, optionally emphasizing the article that matches the fromArticleId URL parameter.
Offer navigation back to the Help Center home or the originating article, determined by the presence of the fromArticleId URL parameter.
user_workflows: View current payment settings: User opens payment_settings.html (optionally with fromArticleId in the URL); page controller parses URL parameters; page calls getPaymentSettingsView; once data is returned, the summary component shows current billing country and payment method details; help hints component lists any related help articles.

Change billing country: User opens payment_settings.html; getPaymentSettingsView loads current settings and available billingCountryOptions; billing-country selector pre-selects the current billing country; user picks a different option; user submits the change; page calls setBillingCountry with the selected billingCountry; on success, summary and selector components update to the new billing country and updatedAt timestamp, and a success message is shown; on failure, an error message is shown without changing the displayed current billing country.

Follow help guidance from an article: User clicks a CTA in an article that links to payment_settings.html with fromArticleId; payment settings page loads and calls getPaymentSettingsView; help hints component compares fromArticleId with helpArticleHints.articleId values and highlights the matching hint if any; user reviews summary, optionally changes billing country; user then uses navigation controls to return either to the originating article (using article.html with the fromArticleId) or to the Help Center home.

Return to Help Center after reviewing or updating settings: After viewing or updating the billing country, user clicks the back navigation control; if fromArticleId is present in the URL, the primary back action navigates to article.html with the same fromArticleId; if fromArticleId is absent, the back action navigates to index.html.
interactions: On page load, the controller parses URL parameters (including fromArticleId if present) and invokes getPaymentSettingsView; all dependent components wait for this data before rendering their bound values.
Changing the billing country dropdown value updates the local selected billingCountry state but does not immediately persist it until the user submits the form.

Submitting the billing country form triggers a call to setBillingCountry with the currently selected billingCountry; while the request is in progress, the form may be disabled to prevent duplicate submissions.

On successful setBillingCountry response, the UI updates the displayed billing country, the last-updated timestamp, and shows a success message derived from the response.message; on failure, an error message derived from response.message is shown without modifying the currentSettings view.

Clicking a related help article title in the help hints list opens the corresponding article page using a URL that includes the hint's articleId as a parameter, without additional API calls from this page.
Clicking the back navigation control uses the current URL state: if fromArticleId is present, the browser navigates to article.html with that ID; otherwise, it navigates to index.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page reads URL parameters on initialization. If a fromArticleId parameter is present, it is stored in page-level state and used to: (1) determine the target of the primary back navigation (article.html?articleId=<fromArticleId> instead of index.html), and (2) optionally highlight a matching help hint whose articleId equals fromArticleId. The URL itself is not modified by internal interactions, so fromArticleId remains available during the session. Payment settings data (currentSettings, billingCountryOptions, paymentMethodTypeOptions, helpArticleHints) is fetched solely via getPaymentSettingsView and kept in memory; it is never persisted to or derived from URL parameters. When setBillingCountry returns successfully, the page updates its in-memory representation of the current billing country and updatedAt fields based on the response, without changing URL parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes the Payment Settings page, parses URL parameters (including fromArticleId), fetches payment settings data via getPaymentSettingsView, and coordinates updates after billing country changes.
event_handlers: onInit
onBillingCountryUpdated

components.functionality: Displays read-only information from currentSettings, including billing country, payment method type, masked payment identifier if available, and last updated timestamp.
event_handlers: onDataLoaded
onBillingCountryUpdated

components.functionality: Provides a dropdown populated from billingCountryOptions, pre-selects the current billing country from currentSettings, and submits changes by calling setBillingCountry.
event_handlers: onChangeBillingCountrySelection
onSubmitBillingCountry
onSetBillingCountrySuccess
onSetBillingCountryError

components.functionality: Shows details of the current payment method using currentSettings.paymentMethodType and cardLast4, and may display a non-interactive note referencing related help article hints for changing payment methods.
event_handlers: onDataLoaded

components.functionality: Lists contextual help hints from helpArticleHints returned by getPaymentSettingsView and optionally highlights the item whose articleId matches the fromArticleId URL parameter.
event_handlers: onDataLoaded
onClickHelpHint

components.functionality: Displays transient success or error messages based on the result of setBillingCountry, including any message returned by the interface.
event_handlers: onSetBillingCountrySuccess
onSetBillingCountryError

components.functionality: Provides a back navigation control that routes either to the originating article (if fromArticleId is present in the current URL) or to the Help Center home page (index.html) when clicked.
event_handlers: onClickBack

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a centered main content column. The article list appears to use two equal-width columns (approximately 6 + 6) with a central gutter.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom layout that mirrors the mockup: a full-width dark header/navigation at the very top, followed by any transient status banner, then a centered main content column. Within that column, stack elements in this order: back-to-help navigation (if placed at top), page title and payment settings summary, then a visually dominant billing country selector form (acting as the ‘hero’ interactive element), followed by the payment method details panel. Below these, introduce a full-width section heading for related help article hints, with a two-column list of links beneath it. End the page with a thin horizontal separator line spanning the main content column. Keep navigation and structural elements at the top, interactive controls in the middle, and help/reading content at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom layout that mirrors the mockup: a full-width dark header/navigation at the very top, followed by any transient status banner, then a centered main content column. Within that column, stack elements in this order: back-to-help navigation (if placed at top), page title and payment settings summary, then a visually dominant billing country selector form (acting as the ‘hero’ interactive element), followed by the payment method details panel. Below these, introduce a full-width section heading for related help article hints, with a two-column list of links beneath it. End the page with a thin horizontal separator line spanning the main content column. Keep navigation and structural elements at the top, interactive controls in the middle, and help/reading content at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve a modest vertical slice (~8–10% of viewport height) for the top navigation within the page controller. Allocate a generously padded vertical block (~20–25% of viewport height on first view) around the billing-country-selector-form so it becomes the dominant interactive focal point, similar to the large search area in the mockup. The payment-settings-summary and payment-method-details-panel share the remaining mid-page vertical space with moderate spacing. The help-article-hints-list occupies the lower portion of the page, with a clear vertical gap from the form/details area and enough height to accommodate a two-column list without feeling cramped. Horizontally, keep all content inside a centered main content column (max-width ~1120px, roughly 70–80% of viewport width) with ample left and right margins against the dark background to preserve the framed, cinematic feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious density: no dense tables or tightly packed rows. Use the 8px spacing scale to create clear separation: ~32–48px between major sections (nav → summary/form, form → details, details → help articles), ~16–24px between related blocks (summary → form, form → helper text, heading → list), and ~12–16px between individual items within lists. Inputs and panels should have 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal internal padding. The two-column help article list increases the amount of information per viewport while keeping enough vertical spacing between links to remain easily scannable.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the strong vertical, left-to-right flow observed in the mockup. The eye starts in the top-left with brand/navigation (within the page controller), then moves slightly down to the page title and summary. From there, attention is pulled inward to the large, centered billing country selector form (the main interactive focus), then drops to any helper text just below it. Next, the eye moves down to the payment method details panel for more granular information. Finally, the gaze travels further down to the bold, left-aligned heading for related help articles and scans the two-column list of links from top to bottom, typically reading down the left column before moving to the right. A subtle horizontal rule at the very bottom gives a sense of closure.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a full-width, nearly black background, with a slim but solid horizontal navigation/header bar across the top. Below this, a centered main content column—roughly two-thirds of the viewport width and capped at about 1120px—floats in the middle of the dark canvas, framed by generous side margins. Inside this column, the content flows downward in a clean stack. Near the top, a simple page title and brief payment settings summary are left-aligned, setting context without clutter. Just beneath them, a spacious, horizontally centered billing country selector form dominates the mid-screen: a single wide input control with plenty of breathing room above and below, mirroring the prominence of the search bar in the reference mockup. A short helper line sits directly under the field, also centered. Drop further and the layout snaps back to a left-aligned payment method details panel, presenting key billing information in a tidy block. The lower part of the page transitions into a clear section break with a bold, left-aligned heading for related help articles, under which two neat columns of text-only links span the width of the main column, separated by a generous gutter. After scanning these links, a subtle, centered “Back to Help Center” navigation link appears, and the page concludes with a thin horizontal divider line that visually caps the content against the dark background, echoing the utilitarian, streaming-service-style aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component defines the overall scaffold of the page. It renders a full-width, dark background that spans the entire viewport and a horizontal top navigation/header bar fixed at the very top of the page. The header sits flush with the top edge, stretches 100% of the viewport width, and is approximately 56–72px tall (about 8–10% of the viewport height), containing the streaming service logo on the left and navigation links on the right, all aligned on a single baseline. Centered beneath this header, the controller establishes the main content column: a single column with a max-width of roughly 1120px, horizontally centered, leaving generous left and right margins (approximately 10–15% of viewport width on each side). All other components are arranged in a vertical stack inside this column, following a linear top-to-bottom flow. At the very bottom of the main column, the controller draws a subtle, 1px horizontal separator line that runs the full width of the column, visually terminating the page content against the dark background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Page-level container with static positioning. The top navigation/header is anchored at the top of the document flow and scrolls out of view as the user scrolls down. The main content column remains centered and responsive, shrinking on smaller screens while maintaining consistent padding from viewport edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the main content column, the payment settings summary appears near the top as the first substantive content block beneath any status message and the page title. It is left-aligned with the column, starting roughly 32–40px below the header/status area to create a clear separation. The summary content (such as plan name, next billing date, and current billing country) sits within a horizontally full-width text area but effectively occupies about 60–70% of the column, with text lines not stretching uncomfortably wide. Vertical padding within this block is moderate (about 16–24px top and bottom), creating a compact but comfortable summary band. A subtle bottom divider (such as a faint 1px rule or a clear 24–32px gap) separates this summary from the more visually prominent billing country selector area that follows, signaling that the summary is contextual information leading into the main control section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the main column. It participates in the normal vertical flow: when present, it pushes subsequent components down; it does not overlay or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The billing country selector form is the hero interactive element and is placed directly below the payment settings summary, with a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px to visually distinguish it as the next key action. This form occupies the visual center of the page: the main control (a dropdown or searchable select) is horizontally centered within the main content column and spans about 55–65% of the column width, making it noticeably wider than a typical small field but still leaving margins on both sides. Above the control, a concise label or heading is centered and sits about 8–12px above the field. The field itself has generous internal padding (approximately 12–16px vertically and 16–24px horizontally) and a flat, high-contrast style in keeping with the dark, minimalist theme. Directly beneath the control, a short explanatory or helper line of text is centered and placed about 8–12px below the field, mimicking the search-helper-text pairing in the mockup. The entire form block—including label, field, and helper text—is surrounded by substantial vertical whitespace: about 32–40px above and 32–48px below the field cluster, so that this section occupies a large contiguous band around the middle of the initial viewport, clearly signaling it as the primary area of interaction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that remains in the normal scroll flow. It does not stick or float; instead, its prominence comes from its size, central alignment, and surrounding whitespace.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment method details panel is positioned immediately below the billing country selector form, separated by a comfortable 32–40px vertical gap to indicate a new but closely related section. It returns to a left-aligned layout pattern: the panel’s content (e.g., masked card number, expiration date, billing address) is aligned with the left edge of the main content column and typically fits within about 60–70% of the column width, keeping line lengths readable. The panel reads like a simple, text-dominant card: it may have a faint top border or a slightly darker background tint to distinguish it from the underlying page background without feeling boxy. Internally, items within this panel are stacked vertically with about 12–16px spacing between rows of data and about 16–24px padding at the top and bottom. The right side of the panel remains mostly open, preserving a feeling of lightness and preventing the section from appearing dense. This panel consumes a moderate vertical height—enough to show a few lines of detail while still leaving clear space before the section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component that follows the form within the vertical flow. It expands vertically as needed to accommodate its content but does not alter its position relative to other sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help article hints list is placed below the payment method details panel and reads as a dedicated lower-page section similar to the ‘MOST POPULAR ARTICLES’ block in the mockup. It begins after a noticeable vertical gap of approximately 40–48px, signaling a shift from settings data to supporting help content. At the top of this section, a bold, all-caps (or otherwise visually strong) heading such as ‘RELATED HELP ARTICLES’ is left-aligned with the main content column. This heading sits about 8–12px above the article list. Below the heading, the list of help article hints is arranged into two equal-width text columns within the main content column, following an implicit 12-column grid with two 6-column text areas. Each column occupies roughly 45–48% of the main column width, with a clear central gutter of about 4–8% between them. Within each column, individual article links are stacked vertically, each on its own line, with about 12–16px of vertical space between items. The text is flat and minimal—no thumbnails or icons—matching the utilitarian style of the mockup. The entire hints section typically occupies the lower third of the page content area, with a clear top margin from the details panel and a smaller bottom margin leading into the back navigation and final separator line.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content section that flows with the page. On narrower screens, the two columns can stack into a single column while preserving vertical spacing between items; on wide screens, it maintains its two-column layout aligned to the centered main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status message banner, when present, appears immediately below the top navigation/header and above the main content blocks (summary and form) inside the main content column. It spans the full width of the main content column, aligning flush with the left and right edges of that column. Vertically, it is relatively slim, roughly 48–64px tall depending on message length, with internal padding of about 12–16px top and bottom and 16–24px left and right. The banner maintains the dark theme but uses a slightly more pronounced background tone or colored accent (e.g., subtle tinted bar or thin left border) to stand out without breaking the minimalist aesthetic. There is a small gap (~16px) between the navigation and the banner and another gap (~24–32px) between the bottom of the banner and the payment settings summary, so that the banner feels clearly inserted above the core content rather than merged into it. This positioning makes it highly visible upon page load without overpowering the billing country selector visually.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered block that occupies a static position in the vertical stack when visible, pushing the rest of the content downward. It does not overlay content or remain sticky; it scrolls out of view with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-help navigation link is placed near the bottom of the main content column, immediately following the help article hints list. It sits about 24–32px below the last row of article links, acting as a soft footer action for users who have finished scanning the related help content. The link is horizontally centered within the main content column, presenting as a single line of text (optionally prefixed with a simple left arrow glyph) with no enclosing button, consistent with the flat, text-first style. It occupies minimal vertical space—essentially a text row with about 8–12px of padding above and below to avoid feeling cramped. Directly beneath this link, the page controller’s thin horizontal separator line spans the width of the main content column, visually closing the page. This placement ensures that the user sees the link naturally after reading the help hints, providing a clear path back to the broader Help Center.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline/navigation element at the end of the content flow. It scrolls with the page and does not stick to the viewport edges.

name: Profile Settings
title: Profile Settings
description: Manage profile-level parental control settings, including maturity levels and PIN requirements, for streaming profiles referenced from Help Center articles.

core_features: Load and display all available profiles with their current maturity levels and PIN-related settings using getProfileSettingsView.
Allow users to select a profile and view its detailed parental control settings, including maturity level, minimum age (if provided), and PIN requirement state.
Provide a maturity level selector bound to maturityLevelOptions so users can change a profile’s maturity level via updateProfileMaturityLevel.
Provide controls to enable or disable a PIN requirement for a selected profile via updateProfilePinRequirement, reflecting the updated state in the UI.
Provide a form to conceptually set or update PIN status and PIN hint for a profile via updateProfilePin (without handling real credential flows).
Support navigation back to the Help Center home (index.html) and, when available, back to the originating Help article using the fromArticleId URL parameter.
Keep the currently selected profile in sync with the profileId URL parameter so that deep links from articles can open a specific profile’s settings directly.
user_workflows: Workflow 1: Open Profile Settings from a Help article: 1) User clicks a CTA in an article that opens profile_settings.html and may include fromArticleId and profileId URL parameters. 2) Page initializes, reads fromArticleId and profileId from the URL. 3) Page calls getProfileSettingsView to retrieve profiles and maturity options. 4) After data loads, the profile whose ID matches profileId (if provided and valid) is selected; otherwise the first available profile is selected. 5) User reviews maturity and PIN settings for that profile and makes any changes needed. 6) User optionally clicks a contextual link that navigates back to article.html with id equal to fromArticleId.

Workflow 2: Change a profile’s maturity level: 1) User opens profile_settings.html (from article or footer). 2) Page calls getProfileSettingsView and renders the profile list and maturityLevelOptions. 3) User selects a profile from the profile list; the page updates the profileId URL parameter to that profile’s ID. 4) In the maturity selector for the selected profile, user chooses a different maturity level from the options provided. 5) The page calls updateProfileMaturityLevel with the selected profileId and chosen maturityLevel value. 6) On success, the component updates the displayed maturity level and last-updated timestamp using the returned profile object and may show a non-static confirmation message based on the response message. 7) If the call reports failure, the component reverts to the previous maturity level and displays the returned message as an error state.

Workflow 3: Enable or disable a profile PIN requirement: 1) User selects a profile in the profile list. 2) In the PIN requirement section, user toggles the control to the desired state. 3) If enabling and the profilePinSet value for the profile indicates no PIN is set, the page prompts the user to first configure PIN settings via the PIN management form. 4) When the user confirms the toggle, the page calls updateProfilePinRequirement with the profileId and the desired pinRequiredForProfile value. 5) On success, the UI reflects the new requirement state and updatedAt timestamp from the response. 6) On failure, the UI reverts the toggle and displays the returned message.

Workflow 4: Set or update a profile PIN and hint (conceptual): 1) User selects a profile and opens the PIN management form (for example, via an edit or set PIN control). 2) User sets whether a PIN is considered set (pinSet) and provides or updates a PIN hint string, without entering or displaying the actual PIN value. 3) User submits the form. 4) The page calls updateProfilePin with profileId, pinSet, and pinHint. 5) On success, the UI updates the profile’s PIN status and hint using the returned profile object and displays feedback based on the returned message. 6) If the profile previously had PIN-required disabled and pinSet is now true, the user may optionally toggle the PIN requirement on via the separate PIN requirement control.

Workflow 5: Navigate back to Help Center or source article: 1) User clicks the header link labeled for navigation to the Help Center. 2) The page navigates to index.html, removing profile_settings-specific parameters. 3) If the page was opened with fromArticleId and the user clicks a contextual link or button to return to the article, the page navigates to article.html with id set to fromArticleId while preserving or clearing other query parameters as defined. 4) The browser back button can also be used, relying on the user’s navigation history.
interactions: On page load, an initialization interaction reads URL parameters (fromArticleId, profileId) and triggers a single call to getProfileSettingsView; loading and error states are managed until this call resolves.
In the profile list, clicking a profile item sets that profile as the active selection in page state, updates the profileId URL parameter accordingly, and refreshes the detail panel for that profile.

In the maturity selector, changing the selected maturity level triggers client-side validation (ensuring the selection matches one of the maturityLevelOptions values) and then calls updateProfileMaturityLevel; the UI is updated or reverted based on the success field in the response.

In the PIN requirement toggle, toggling the switch checks the current profilePinSet state; if no PIN is set and the user is enabling the requirement, the system may display a confirmation or guidance message and route the user to the PIN management form before calling updateProfilePinRequirement.

In the PIN management form, submitting changes performs basic validation on the pinHint (for example, non-empty when pinSet is true, based solely on runtime rules) and then calls updateProfilePin; the profile’s PIN status and hint in the UI bind directly to the returned profile fields.
In the header navigation, clicking the Help Center link navigates to index.html without calling additional interfaces, relying only on standard navigation behavior.

If a contextual link for returning to the source article is visible (when fromArticleId is present), clicking it navigates to article.html using the fromArticleId value as a URL parameter, without invoking any interface on this page.

When the selected profile changes, any unsaved in-progress edits in forms are either cleared, persisted in in-memory state, or confirmed with the user, without persisting them via localStorage or other browser storage; only confirmed submissions call the appropriate update interfaces.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s state is driven primarily by URL query parameters and the data returned from getProfileSettingsView. On initial load, the page reads fromArticleId and profileId from the URL: fromArticleId is stored in transient state and used only to conditionally render a navigation control and build a link to article.html; it is never modified by this page. The profileId parameter, if present, determines which profile is initially selected after getProfileSettingsView returns; the page searches the returned profiles array for a matching profileId and selects it if found, otherwise it falls back to a default selection (such as the first profile in the array) without assuming any specific ordering. When the user selects a different profile from the list, the page updates its internal selected-profile state and synchronizes the profileId query parameter (e.g., profile_settings.html?profileId={selectedProfileId}&fromArticleId={originalArticleId}) via client-side navigation or history updates, so that reloading the page or sharing the URL preserves the selection. No other persistent storage mechanism such as localStorage or sessionStorage is used; all state beyond the URL parameters and current in-memory UI state is re-derived on each page load from a fresh call to getProfileSettingsView. Calls to updateProfileMaturityLevel, updateProfilePinRequirement, and updateProfilePin do not directly alter URL parameters; instead, they update the in-memory representation of the relevant profile using the response.profile data. Any error messages or transient confirmation states are held only in component state and are cleared as appropriate when the user navigates away, changes profiles, or reloads the page.

components.functionality: Provides top-level navigation controls allowing users to return to the Help Center home and, when applicable, to the originating Help article. It reads the fromArticleId URL parameter to determine whether to display a contextual 'Back to article' control and builds navigation URLs accordingly.
event_handlers: onClickHelpCenterLink
onClickBackToArticleLink

components.functionality: Initializes the Profile Settings page by calling getProfileSettingsView, manages loading and error states, and passes the loaded profiles and maturityLevelOptions data to child components. Also determines the initially selected profile using the profileId URL parameter and exposes that selection to other components.
event_handlers: onInitLoadProfileSettings
onLoadSuccess
onLoadError

components.functionality: Displays the list of profiles returned by getProfileSettingsView and allows the user to select which profile’s parental control settings to view and edit. The selected profile is highlighted and communicated to the rest of the page so that detail components can update accordingly.
event_handlers: onProfileItemClick
onSelectedProfileChange

components.functionality: Shows a concise summary of the currently selected profile, including its name, current maturity level, minimum age (if provided), PIN requirement status, and whether a PIN is set. It updates in real time when updateProfileMaturityLevel, updateProfilePinRequirement, or updateProfilePin responses modify the selected profile.
event_handlers: onSelectedProfileChange
onProfileUpdated

components.functionality: Provides controls (for example, a dropdown or radio group) for changing the maturity level of the selected profile. It uses the maturityLevelOptions data for available values and labels, validates that the selected option is allowed, and submits changes using updateProfileMaturityLevel. It reflects success or error messages and updates the selected profile’s data based on the response.
event_handlers: onMaturityLevelChange
onMaturityLevelSubmit
onMaturityUpdateSuccess
onMaturityUpdateError

components.functionality: Shows and controls whether a PIN is required to access the selected profile. It binds to the selected profile’s pinRequiredForProfile value and calls updateProfilePinRequirement when the user toggles the control. If enabling the requirement while no PIN is set, it can coordinate with the PIN management form to prompt the user to configure a PIN status and hint first.
event_handlers: onPinRequirementToggle
onPinRequirementUpdateSuccess
onPinRequirementUpdateError
onRequirePinButNotSet

components.functionality: Allows users to conceptually set or update whether a PIN is configured for the selected profile and to manage the PIN hint text. On submission, it calls updateProfilePin with the selected profileId, the chosen pinSet state, and the pinHint. The form updates the UI with the returned profilePinSet, profilePinHint, and updatedAt values from the response and surfaces any response message as feedback.
event_handlers: onOpenPinForm
onPinFormChange
onPinFormSubmit
onPinUpdateSuccess
onPinUpdateError

components.functionality: Coordinates the selected-profile-summary, maturity-level-selector, pin-requirement-toggle, and pin-management-form components for the currently selected profile. It ensures that updates from any of the update interfaces are propagated consistently to all child components and that switching profiles resets or preserves internal form states as appropriate.
event_handlers: onSelectedProfileChange
onChildComponentProfileUpdate
onResetDetailForms

components.functionality: Keeps the profileId and fromArticleId URL parameters in sync with the currently selected profile and the initial navigation context. It reads parameters on load, updates profile selection accordingly, and modifies the URL (for example, via history APIs) when the selected profile changes, without triggering full page reloads unless required.
event_handlers: onInitReadUrlParams
onSelectedProfileChange
onUrlParamsUpdate

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a centered main content column. The article list appears to use two equal-width columns (approximately 6 + 6) with a central gutter.
layout.layout_pattern: Follow the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow. A full-width dark navigation header sits at the very top. Directly beneath it, a vertically generous, centered ‘primary interaction’ zone is used for profile selection (analogous to the original large search bar), with the selected profile’s summary immediately below as helper/confirmation text. Below that, a full-width section heading introduces the main settings area. The settings themselves are arranged in a two-column layout inside a centered content column: the left column focuses on maturity level settings, and the right column focuses on PIN requirements and PIN management. The page ends with a thin horizontal separator line at the bottom of the main content block.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Follow the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow. A full-width dark navigation header sits at the very top. Directly beneath it, a vertically generous, centered ‘primary interaction’ zone is used for profile selection (analogous to the original large search bar), with the selected profile’s summary immediately below as helper/confirmation text. Below that, a full-width section heading introduces the main settings area. The settings themselves are arranged in a two-column layout inside a centered content column: the left column focuses on maturity level settings, and the right column focuses on PIN requirements and PIN management. The page ends with a thin horizontal separator line at the bottom of the main content block.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The navigation header uses a modest vertical height (roughly 8–10% of the viewport). The profile selection + summary block takes a visually dominant slice of space beneath the nav (around 20–25% of the viewport, including its top and bottom padding). The main settings section (profile-detail-container) occupies the remaining vertical space, with a clear vertical gap (32–48px) between the profile selection area and the section heading. Horizontally, all main content (profile selector, summary, settings columns) is constrained to a centered column at about 60–70% of the viewport width, respecting a 12-column grid with approximately two 6-column sub-columns separated by a generous gutter.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious density: no crowding, but no unnecessary emptiness. Major blocks (profile selector, summary, settings columns) are separated by 32–48px vertically. Individual controls/cards inside the settings columns use 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. Text lines breathe with comfortable line height, and list-like stacks (e.g., fields in a form) have regular 8–16px spacing. This keeps the layout utilitarian and readable, similar to the mockup’s article list.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow starts at the top-left brand in the navigation, scans across the nav items, then is pulled downward to the large, centered profile selector as the primary focal point. From the selector, the eye naturally drops to the centered selected-profile-summary just below (similar to search-helper text). It then moves down to the left-aligned section heading above the settings area, and from there reads through the settings in a top-down, left-to-right order: first the left column (maturity settings), then the right column (PIN controls). Overall, the flow is distinctly vertical with a secondary left-to-right scan across the two columns, matching the behavior of the original ‘Most Popular Articles’ grid.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a full-width, dark background with a centered content strip that feels like a focused, cinematic control panel. At the very top, a flat, full-width navigation bar runs edge to edge; its contents (logo and help-center links) are horizontally aligned within the same centered max-width as the rest of the page, echoing streaming-service UIs. Beneath this, the eye is drawn into a tall, central band that houses a wide, pill-like profile selector sitting in the middle of the viewport, occupying most of the content width but leaving generous left and right margins. Directly under this, a single line (or short block) of helper text summarizing the selected profile sits centered, acting like a confirmation caption and reinforcing that this is the currently active profile. After a substantial vertical gap, a bold, left-aligned heading introduces the main ‘Profile Settings’ section. Within this section, the content is divided into two equal-width columns inside the same centered content frame: the left column contains the maturity level selector; the right column contains the PIN requirement toggle followed by the PIN management form. Each of these controls lives in its own subtly defined block with comfortable padding and crisp white text on the dark background, separated vertically by consistent spacing. The two columns align neatly at the top, separated by a noticeable central gutter, so the user naturally reads down the left column first and then the right. At the bottom edge of the settings section, a thin, low-contrast horizontal rule spans the width of the content column, providing a clean visual endpoint similar to the separator line at the end of the mockup’s article list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-navigation component is positioned at the very top of the page as a full-width horizontal bar. It spans 100% of the viewport width and approximately 8–10% of the viewport height. Within this bar, the content is constrained to the same centered max-width column as the rest of the page (roughly 60–70% of viewport width), aligned on the same vertical grid. The brand logo or help center title is anchored to the left, and navigation links (e.g., Home, Help Topics, Account) align to the right, creating a left-to-right reading path. Vertical padding inside the bar is around 8–12px, giving it a slim, utilitarian look. The bottom edge of the header has a very subtle 1px border or shadow line to delineate it from the main content. It scrolls away with the page (non-sticky), allowing the main content to take visual focus after the initial orientation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document flow, full-width, with a fixed max-width content container centered within it. It does not overlay content and scrolls out of view as the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-settings-loader occupies the main content area beneath the header-navigation whenever loading is in progress. Spatially, it aligns with the centered content column used by the profile-list and profile-detail-container. When active, it either overlays a semi-opaque dark veil over the main content region (from the bottom edge of the header down to just past the current visible content) or replaces the content area with a centered loading indicator. The loader’s focal element (spinner or progress indicator) is vertically centered within the remaining viewport height below the header and horizontally centered within the main content column. It respects the page’s wide left and right margins so that it feels embedded within the same frame as the settings rather than floating edge-to-edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the main content region below the header within the centered content column, using absolute or fixed positioning within that region while preserving the layout of other components underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-list is the dominant interactive element directly below the header-navigation, occupying the hero-like position analogous to the mockup’s central search bar. It is placed with a generous top margin of about 48–64px from the bottom of the header, sitting well within the upper third of the viewport. Horizontally, it is centered and limited to the main content column, spanning roughly 60–70% of the viewport width with equal left and right margins. Visually, it appears as a single, elongated, horizontally oriented interaction zone—a row of selectable profile avatars or a wide dropdown-like selector—running left to right within this width. The component has comfortable internal padding (12–16px vertical, 16–24px horizontal), and its height roughly matches that of a large input field or search bar, giving it substantial presence without feeling bulky. The surrounding vertical whitespace above and below reinforces its status as the primary control before any settings detail appears.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow, always positioned immediately below the header. It expands or contracts slightly in height based on its internal content (number of profiles, text length), but its maximum width remains constrained to the centered main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The selected-profile-summary sits directly underneath the profile-list, acting as a concise, textual confirmation of the currently selected profile—similar to the helper text under the search bar in the mockup. It is center-aligned both horizontally and by text alignment. The component uses a smaller text size than primary headings and spans roughly 40–50% of the viewport width within the same centered content column, creating a narrow, readable block in the middle of the page. A small vertical gap (about 8–12px) separates it from the bottom of the profile-list to keep the association tight while still visually distinct. There is then a larger vertical gap (32–40px) between this summary and the start of the main settings section, clearly segmenting the ‘selection’ zone from the ‘configuration’ zone. If multiple lines of summary text are needed, they stack with generous line height but remain visually compact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column, always positioned directly below the profile-list. Its height flexes with text length, but it remains center-aligned and does not affect the width or alignment of surrounding components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-detail-container is the main structural wrapper for the profile-specific settings, beginning just below the selected-profile-summary and extending down to the horizontal separator at the bottom. It starts with a substantial top margin (approximately 32–48px) from the summary to match the mockup’s clear separation between the search area and the article list. At its top, a bold, uppercase or strong-weight heading like ‘PROFILE SETTINGS’ is left-aligned with the main content column while the content itself remains centered within the fixed max-width, echoing the ‘MOST POPULAR ARTICLES’ heading. The heading spans close to the full width of the content column (around 60–70% of the viewport, centered), leaving consistent side margins. Below the heading, after a modest gap (16–24px), the container splits into two equal-width vertical columns (approximate 6+6 columns of a 12-column grid): the left column houses the maturity-level-selector, and the right column houses the pin-requirement-toggle and pin-management-form stacked vertically. A generous central gutter (24–32px) separates the columns, maintaining the airy, scannable feel of the article list in the mockup. The bottom of the profile-detail-container ends with a thin, low-contrast horizontal rule aligned to the width of the main content column, which visually caps the section and mirrors the mockup’s bottom separator.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a static grid container within the centered content column. On wider screens it maintains a two-column layout; on narrow screens it can stack into a single column while preserving top-to-bottom flow (left-column elements appear above right-column elements).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maturity-level-selector occupies the top of the left column inside the profile-detail-container, aligning horizontally with the first element of the right column. It spans the full width of the left column (roughly 48–50% of the main content width), starting directly beneath the ‘PROFILE SETTINGS’ heading with a vertical gap of about 24px. The component presents as a clearly bounded block of controls, with subtle borders or a slight background tonal shift, and internal padding of approximately 16–24px horizontally and 12–16px vertically. Its internal layout is vertical: a title or label at the top, followed by explanatory text, then the maturity level control (dropdown, slider, or option list) with 8–16px spacing between each element. The overall height is moderate, similar to a compact card, leaving comfortable space below before the bottom of the column or any future left-column elements. Its top edge lines up visually with the top of the pin-requirement-toggle in the right column to preserve a clean horizontal rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, expanding vertically to fit its content. It does not scroll independently and adheres to the shared column and gutter constraints of the profile-detail-container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pin-requirement-toggle is positioned at the top of the right column inside the profile-detail-container, horizontally aligned with the top of the maturity-level-selector. It spans the full width of the right column (approximately 48–50% of the main content width). The component appears as a slim but clearly defined row or small card, with 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding and a subtle boundary to separate it from the background. The main toggle control sits on the right side of the component, with its label and a short explanatory line on the left, following the left-to-right reading pattern. Vertical spacing below the component (roughly 16–24px) separates it from the pin-management-form beneath, mirroring the consistent spacing between items in the article columns from the mockup. When toggled off, the pin-management-form below may visually collapse or reduce, but the toggle’s own position at the top of the right column remains fixed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the right column. Its height is relatively small and constant, but the state of the toggle can control the visibility/height of the pin-management-form directly beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pin-management-form sits immediately below the pin-requirement-toggle in the right column, aligned to the same column width. A vertical gap of about 16–24px separates it from the toggle to maintain distinct but related grouping. It spans the full width of the right column and reads as a larger form card with multiple fields (e.g., enter PIN, confirm PIN, update buttons). The card has 16–24px horizontal padding and 12–16px vertical padding, with its internal elements arranged in a vertical stack. Each field or row inside the form is spaced by approximately 8–16px, preserving the moderate density pattern. The top edge of this form aligns roughly with the mid-lower region of the maturity-level-selector, so as the user scans down the left column, the form appears as the second major group on the right column. If the PIN requirement is turned off, this form may collapse to a minimal state or hide entirely, allowing the right column to compress vertically while the left column height remains unchanged.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, vertically expanding or collapsing based on whether the PIN requirement is enabled. It participates in the same responsive behavior as the profile-detail-container (stacking below other elements on narrow screens).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-synchronizer is a non-visual, behavioral component that does not occupy visible space on the page. Conceptually, it is attached to the main content region (from the profile-list down through the profile-detail-container) and ensures that the current selection state—such as the active profile, selected maturity level, or whether PIN is required—is reflected in and restored from the URL. While it has no physical dimensions or visual footprint, its logical presence aligns with the primary content column, reacting to user interactions within that space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Takes no visual space and does not affect layout; operates in the background to synchronize URL and application state as the user interacts with the visible components.

name: About
title: About Help Center
description: Functional page that explains the Help Center’s role for the video streaming service and provides navigation back to key support sections, using dynamically loaded About content.

core_features: Load and display the About Help Center title and body content using the getAboutHelpCenterContent interface.
Render rich About content (HTML) from the service without hardcoding any static text on the page.
Display a dynamic list of highlighted navigation entries (e.g., Help Center home, specific category, device help) based on highlightedSections returned from getAboutHelpCenterContent.
Enable navigation from the About page to other Help Center pages (home, category, devices) using URL parameters derived from highlightedSections data.
Support optional URL-based state (e.g., source reference or a highlighted target section) to control focus/scrolling behavior and analytics without relying on localStorage.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View About information from footer
1. User clicks the 'About' link in the global footer (navigates to about.html, optionally with a ref query parameter indicating the source page).
2. about.html loads and the About Page Data Loader component calls getAboutHelpCenterContent with no parameters.
3. The page renders the title from the response and injects bodyHtml into the About Body Renderer.
4. Highlighted sections from highlightedSections are rendered as a navigation list.
5. User reads the information and optionally clicks a highlighted section link to go directly to the Help Center home, a category page, or the devices help page.

Workflow: Go back to Help Center home from About
1. User is on about.html and decides they need further help.
2. User clicks a highlighted navigation entry whose targetPageType equals the Help Center home type.
3. The Highlighted Navigation List component computes the target URL as index.html, optionally appending query parameters such as ref=about to indicate the source.
4. The page navigates to index.html using standard browser navigation.

Workflow: Jump from About directly to Account & Billing help
1. User reads About information and sees a highlighted entry representing an account or billing category, where highlightedSections includes an item with targetPageType set to a category type and categoryCode populated.
2. User clicks that highlighted entry.
3. The Highlighted Navigation List component composes a URL for category.html including a categoryId query parameter whose value is taken directly from the clicked item’s categoryCode.
4. The browser navigates to category.html?categoryId=<categoryCode>, and the category page then loads its own content based on that URL parameter.

Workflow: Jump from About directly to device-specific help
1. User reads About information and wants device help, so they locate a highlighted entry whose targetPageType corresponds to the devices page.
2. User clicks that entry.
3. The Highlighted Navigation List component navigates the browser to devices.html (no additional parameters required for this page’s responsibility).
4. The devices page handles any further device/topic selection.

Workflow: Deep-link to a specific highlighted section of the About content
1. An external page or email links to about.html with a highlightTarget query parameter specifying which highlighted section should be emphasized (the value corresponds to a targetPageType or another agreed identifier understood by the client).
2. about.html loads, calls getAboutHelpCenterContent, and renders content.
3. Once highlightedSections are loaded, the About Page State Controller compares highlightTarget from the URL to entries in highlightedSections.
4. If a matching entry is found, the page scrolls to or visually focuses that highlighted entry, improving orientation for the user.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load
- Trigger: Browser navigates to about.html.
- Behavior: The About Page Data Loader calls getAboutHelpCenterContent once, then passes the result to child components (title renderer, body renderer, highlighted navigation list, state controller). No additional API calls are made.

Interaction: Click on a link rendered inside bodyHtml
- Trigger: User clicks an anchor element inside the About Body Renderer’s injected HTML.
- Behavior: If the anchor points to index.html, category.html, or devices.html (with or without query parameters), the browser performs a standard navigation to that URL. Optionally, a click handler can append or preserve a ref query parameter indicating that navigation originated from about.html.

Interaction: Click on a highlighted section entry (Help Center home)
- Trigger: User clicks a highlighted entry whose targetPageType corresponds to the Help Center home type.
- Behavior: The click handler constructs the URL for index.html. It may append query parameters, such as ref=about and/or a value derived from the current page URL (e.g., original ref), and then sets window.location to that URL.

Interaction: Click on a highlighted section entry (Category)
- Trigger: User clicks a highlighted entry where targetPageType corresponds to a category and categoryCode is present.
- Behavior: The click handler constructs the URL for category.html with a categoryId query parameter whose value is exactly the clicked entry’s categoryCode, plus any optional ref parameter. It then navigates the browser to the resulting URL.

Interaction: Click on a highlighted section entry (Devices)
- Trigger: User clicks a highlighted entry where targetPageType corresponds to the devices page.
- Behavior: The click handler navigates directly to devices.html, optionally including a ref query parameter indicating the source as about.

Interaction: URL-based highlight focus
- Trigger: about.html is loaded with a highlightTarget query parameter in the URL.
- Behavior: After getAboutHelpCenterContent resolves, the About Page State Controller searches highlightedSections for an entry that matches the highlightTarget value (matching rules are defined in the client: for example, comparing against targetPageType or another identifier if present). If found, it scrolls that highlighted entry into view and/or applies focus, without modifying any server state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page is primarily stateless with respect to server data and does not accept incoming parameters for getAboutHelpCenterContent; however, it uses URL parameters to manage client-side behavior and navigation context. Typical URL parameters include: (1) ref: optional, indicating the source page type or context that led the user to about.html (for example values aligned with the source_page_type enum, such as 'help_center_home', 'category', 'devices', or 'other'); this value is read on load by the About Page State Controller and can be propagated as a ref parameter when navigating to other pages, but it does not affect the getAboutHelpCenterContent call. (2) highlightTarget: optional, indicating which highlightedSections entry should be emphasized upon load. The About Page State Controller reads this parameter after the getAboutHelpCenterContent response is available and attempts to map it to one of the highlightedSections entries (for example by comparing highlightTarget with the entry’s targetPageType or another agreed property if present); on match, the controller scrolls to that entry. The page itself does not modify the URL parameters except when navigating away: when users click highlighted section links, the click handlers compute destination URLs (index.html, category.html, devices.html) and may append a ref query parameter based on the current URL state. No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client stores; all page behavior is derived from the API response and the current URL.

components.functionality: Initializes the About page by calling getAboutHelpCenterContent, then exposes the returned title, bodyHtml, and highlightedSections to other components via in-memory state or props.
event_handlers: onPageLoad

components.functionality: Displays the About page title in the main heading area and synchronizes the browser document title with the value returned by getAboutHelpCenterContent.
event_handlers: onTitleDataChange

components.functionality: Renders the bodyHtml from getAboutHelpCenterContent into the page using safe HTML injection and delegates link clicks for navigation where appropriate.
event_handlers: onInternalLinkClick

components.functionality: Renders a structured list of highlighted navigation entries derived from highlightedSections, and handles navigation to target pages such as Help Center home, category pages, or the devices help page based on each entry’s targetPageType and categoryCode.
