event_handlers: onHighlightedItemClick

components.functionality: Reads URL query parameters (e.g., ref and highlightTarget) on load, provides the ref context to navigation components, and after content is loaded, scrolls or focuses the appropriate highlighted navigation item that matches highlightTarget.
event_handlers: onPageLoad
onHighlightedSectionsLoaded

components.functionality: Centralizes the logic for constructing destination URLs when navigation actions occur from the About page (e.g., adding or propagating ref parameters when navigating to index.html, category.html with categoryId, or devices.html) and triggers browser navigation.
event_handlers: onNavigateToHelpCenterHome
onNavigateToCategory
onNavigateToDevices

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a centered main content column. The article list appears to use two equal-width columns (approximately 6 + 6) with a central gutter.
layout.layout_pattern: The About page sits inside the same dark, full-width shell as the mockup: a global horizontal top navigation (outside this component list) followed by a centered main content column. The search module in the mockup is reinterpreted here as a hero-style About header block (title + short intro) that occupies the visual focal point in the upper-middle of the page. Beneath this, the About body content is rendered as a single, readable text column. At the lower portion of the main column, the 'highlighted navigation list' behaves like the 'Most Popular Articles' block: a left-aligned section heading, followed by a two-column list of key support section links. The underlying data/state/router components are non-visual and attach to the page root, orchestrating what is displayed without occupying layout space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The About page sits inside the same dark, full-width shell as the mockup: a global horizontal top navigation (outside this component list) followed by a centered main content column. The search module in the mockup is reinterpreted here as a hero-style About header block (title + short intro) that occupies the visual focal point in the upper-middle of the page. Beneath this, the About body content is rendered as a single, readable text column. At the lower portion of the main column, the 'highlighted navigation list' behaves like the 'Most Popular Articles' block: a left-aligned section heading, followed by a two-column list of key support section links. The underlying data/state/router components are non-visual and attach to the page root, orchestrating what is displayed without occupying layout space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the global nav uses a modest height at the very top (~8–10% of the viewport). Below it, the About title + introductory body text act as a hero content block with generous top and bottom padding, occupying roughly 20–25% of the initial viewport height and creating a strong central focus similar to the large search area in the mockup. The main About body content then fills a flexible vertical area, followed by the highlighted navigation section that takes the lower segment of the page. Horizontally, all visible content sits within a centered main column spanning about 70–80% of the viewport width (max-width around 960–1120px), leaving substantial left/right gutters to echo the framed, cinematic feel. Within the highlighted navigation section, the two columns each occupy roughly half of this main column width with a clearly visible central gutter.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains moderate density, prioritizing readability similar to the mockup. The title and section headings have generous breathing room. Paragraphs in the About body have comfortable line-height and clear separation (around 16–24px). The highlighted navigation list uses consistent vertical spacing between links (around 16px) so that the two-column list feels full but not crowded. Major section transitions—between title, body, and the navigation list—are marked by larger vertical gaps (~32–48px) so the page feels organized and scannable rather than text-heavy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye enters at the top-left with the global brand/nav, then drops down into the center of the page where the About title header forms the primary focal point, analogous to the search bar’s role in the mockup. From the centered title, attention flows slightly downward to the introductory lines and then continues top-left into the main body text, which is left-aligned inside the same central column. As the visitor scrolls, their eye is drawn further down to a bold, left-aligned heading for the highlighted navigation section (mirroring the ‘MOST POPULAR ARTICLES’ heading). From there, the eye reads down the left column of navigation links and then shifts to the right column, following a top-to-bottom, left-to-right scan pattern consistent with the original design.

layout.overall_description: The About page appears on a dark, full-bleed background with a slim, horizontal global navigation bar pinned to the top. Beneath this, the main content is contained in a centered column that feels like a focused panel floating within the darkness, leaving generous left and right margins. At the top of this column, the About title is large, bright, and centered, sitting with substantial padding above and below so it becomes the first thing you truly notice after the nav—echoing the visual weight of the mockup’s search bar. Immediately below this title, a concise introductory line or short paragraph is rendered, still within the same centered block, explaining the purpose of the Help Center’s About page. As you scroll down, this transitions seamlessly into the rest of the About body text, which shifts to a more utilitarian, left-aligned reading layout while preserving the same column width. Further down, a distinct section emerges: a bold, left-aligned heading introduces key support navigation, and under it two vertical columns of text-only links extend downward in parallel, similar to a ‘Most Popular Articles’ grid. A wide central gutter prevents crowding between the columns, and a thin, subtle horizontal rule runs across the bottom of this section, visually grounding the content and echoing the mockup’s closing separator line. Invisible behind all of this, data, state, and routing components manage loading and navigation without disturbing the clean, content-first composition.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and attaches logically to the page root, conceptually sitting behind the entire centered main content column. It initializes and fetches the dynamic About content before the visible elements (title, body, and highlighted navigation) are presented. It does not occupy any explicit height or width on the screen, and it does not alter the spatial relationships of other components. From a visual standpoint, it behaves like an invisible layer that completes its work before or alongside the rendering of the main column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual infrastructure; no rendered DOM footprint in the layout. It runs in the background at page load and on relevant state changes, without changing the document flow or taking up any physical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned within the primary content column immediately below the global navigation, this header acts as the hero element of the page. It is centered horizontally on the screen and aligned within a main column that spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width (max-width ~960–1120px). There is a generous top margin of about 64px from the bottom of the nav bar, and a similar amount of padding below before the body content begins, echoing the tall, prominent search area in the mockup. The title text itself is centered, with large, white type on the dark background and minimal surrounding chrome, so it becomes the primary focal point. Visually, it occupies the upper 20–25% of the initial viewport height, giving the impression of a compact hero section without imagery, just typography and negative space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the normal document flow. It scrolls away with the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component sits directly beneath the About title header, sharing the same centered main column and dark background. A vertical gap of roughly 24–32px separates it from the bottom of the title header, maintaining a clean visual break while still feeling connected. The body text spans slightly wider than the title text, but remains within the same main column width (roughly 70–80% of the viewport, capped at around 960–1120px). The first line or short paragraph acts as a subtle explanatory continuation of the header, then the content transitions into standard paragraphs and possibly subheadings, all left-aligned for readability. Paragraphs are stacked vertically with about 16–24px of spacing between them, using a comfortable line-height. The overall block expands vertically as needed, forming the central reading zone of the page between the hero-like header above and the highlighted navigation section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable text block that grows vertically according to content length. It remains within the normal document flow and does not overlap or float over other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component occupies a distinct content section beneath the About body text, visually analogous to the ‘MOST POPULAR ARTICLES’ area in the mockup. It begins after a generous vertical margin of approximately 32–48px from the last paragraph of the body, creating a clear separation of purpose. The section sits within the same centered main column, aligning left with the body text. At the top of this section, a bold, all-caps or otherwise emphasized heading (e.g., ‘KEY HELP SECTIONS’ or ‘BACK TO SUPPORT AREAS’) is left-aligned and spans the full width of the main column, with about 16–24px of space below it. Directly beneath this heading, the navigation items are laid out in two equal-width columns, each column taking roughly 48–50% of the main column width, separated by a visible central gutter of about 24–32px. Each column contains a vertical stack of text-only links styled similar to article titles: white text, minimal decoration, and about 16px vertical spacing between items. Users typically scan down the left column first, then move to the right. At the bottom of this section, a thin, subtle horizontal rule (1px line with low-contrast opacity) stretches across the full width of the main column, echoing the bottom separator seen in the mockup and visually grounding the section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the main document flow. On wide screens, links are presented in two columns; on narrower screens (e.g., mobile), the layout can gracefully collapse to a single column that still respects the same vertical spacing and section heading-to-list relationship.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component functions as an invisible orchestrator for the page’s visual state—loading, error, empty, or content-ready. It conceptually wraps the visible About content (title, body, and highlighted navigation list) but does not render any visible UI by itself. When in a loading state, it might temporarily show or hide a lightweight, in-flow loader placeholder within the main column, but the controller itself does not introduce fixed-position elements or alter the overall alignment of the content. It lives at the same hierarchy level as the main content column, controlling which of those child components are mounted at any given time.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logic wrapper with no inherent dimensions. It influences which visible components appear but does not occupy space directly; any loading or error indicators it enables should appear inline within the main content column, respecting the same centered, top-to-bottom flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual component responsible for handling navigation events, particularly interactions with items in the highlighted navigation list and any contextual links within the About body. It sits logically beneath the entire main content column, listening for click events and updating the route when users select a support section link. It does not render visible elements, so it does not affect the spacing, alignment, or proportions of any on-screen components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely behavioral; no rendered layout footprint. It hooks into the navigation system and reacts to user interactions without altering document flow or introducing fixed or floating UI.

name: Terms of Use
title: Terms of Use
description: Displays dynamically loaded Terms of Use content for the streaming service and Help Center, with contextual navigation to related help categories such as Account & Billing.

core_features: Load the Terms of Use title and HTML body from the getTermsOfUseContent interface when the page is opened.
Render the Terms of Use HTML body, including references to billing, cancellations, and account usage, without hardcoding any content.
Display a list of related help categories returned by getTermsOfUseContent.relatedCategories, each linking to the corresponding category.html page using URL parameters.
Support navigation from contextual links within the Terms of Use body to the Account & Billing help category (and other related categories) via URL parameters.
Allow deep linking to specific sections within the Terms of Use content using URL parameters and/or URL fragments (hash) without assuming any fixed section IDs.
user_workflows:
Accessing Terms of Use from footer:
1. User clicks the global footer link that points to terms.html.
2. terms.html initializes and triggers a call to getTermsOfUseContent.
3. The page shows a loading state until getTermsOfUseContent returns.
4. Once data is returned, the page displays the title from response.title and the full HTML body from response.bodyHtml.
5. If response.relatedCategories contains items, the page displays a related help categories component with links to category.html, each with the appropriate categoryId URL parameter.

Navigating from Terms of Use to Account & Billing help via contextual link in body:
1. User scrolls within the Terms of Use body until they encounter a contextual link embedded in response.bodyHtml that represents Account & Billing help.
2. The body renderer detects that this link is a Help Center category link (e.g., using attributes embedded in the HTML or link href patterns) and attaches a click handler.
3. When the user clicks the link, the handler navigates to category.html with a categoryId URL parameter equal to the categoryCode associated with that link (for example the code corresponding to Account & Billing).
4. The browser loads category.html?categoryId=<categoryCode>, and the associated category page logic takes over (outside the scope of this page).

Navigating to a related help category via the related categories list:
1. User scrolls to or notices the Related help categories section (populated from getTermsOfUseContent.relatedCategories).
2. User clicks a category item in the list.
3. The click handler reads the clicked related category's categoryCode from the bound data.
4. The page navigates to category.html?categoryId=<categoryCode> using a standard URL change.
5. The category page loads with that category selected (handled on category.html).

Deep linking to a specific Terms of Use section:
1. An external page, email, or internal link sends the user to terms.html with optional URL parameters and/or a hash fragment, for example terms.html?sectionId=<dynamicSectionId>#<fragment>.
2. On page load, the controller parses the query string and hash fragment but does not assume any specific values.
3. After getTermsOfUseContent completes and the HTML is rendered, the page searches the rendered DOM for an element whose id or data attribute matches the sectionId parameter or hash fragment.
4. If a matching element is found, the page scrolls into view and may apply a generic highlight behavior (e.g., adding a CSS class) without hardcoding any particular section names.
5. If no matching element is found, the page simply remains at the top of the Terms of Use content.

Returning to Terms of Use from browser history after visiting a category:
1. User navigates from terms.html to category.html via a related category link.
2. After viewing help content, the user uses the browser Back button.
3. The browser returns to terms.html with the original URL parameters intact.
4. The page again calls getTermsOfUseContent (no parameters) to ensure the Terms of Use content is current.
5. If section-related URL parameters or hash fragments are present, the page again attempts to scroll to the relevant section as described in the deep-linking workflow.
interactions:
Page load (initialization):
- Trigger: Browser loads terms.html via navigation from footer or a direct link.
- Behavior: The page controller calls getTermsOfUseContent with no parameters, sets a loading state, then binds the result to title, bodyHtml, and relatedCategories once returned. Errors are handled by displaying a generic error state without hardcoding error messages in a way that conflicts with backend messaging.

Click on contextual category link inside Terms body:
- Trigger: User clicks an anchor element inside the rendered bodyHtml that has been identified as a category link (for example via a data attribute or URL pattern embedded in the HTML by the backend).
- Behavior: A click handler prevents default navigation if necessary, reads the associated categoryCode, and navigates to category.html?categoryId=<categoryCode>. This interaction is used to satisfy the outgoing navigation requirement to the Account & Billing help category and any other categories referenced contextually.

Click on related help category item in side or footer list:
- Trigger: User clicks on an item in the Related help categories component.
- Behavior: The click handler reads the bound item’s categoryCode from getTermsOfUseContent.relatedCategories and navigates to category.html?categoryId=<categoryCode>.

URL parameter / hash-based section navigation:
- Trigger: The page is loaded or reloaded with a query parameter (e.g., sectionId) and/or a hash fragment in the URL.
- Behavior: After the Terms content has been rendered, the page attempts to find a DOM element whose id or data attribute matches the parameter or hash, and scrolls to it if found. All handling is generic; no specific section names are embedded in the client logic.

Error handling for Terms content fetch:
- Trigger: getTermsOfUseContent returns an error or fails.
- Behavior: The page controller sets an error state; the UI displays a generic failure state and may provide a generic retry interaction that re-calls getTermsOfUseContent when activated, without hardcoding any Terms text.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Terms of Use page does not require any mandatory URL parameters to function; it always calls getTermsOfUseContent without parameters when loaded. Optional URL parameters and fragments are used only to adjust the view state:

- Base URL: terms.html
  - Behavior: On every load (including browser back/forward), the page calls getTermsOfUseContent. The returned title, bodyHtml, and relatedCategories fully define the rendered content.

- Optional query parameter: sectionId
  - Type: string (no fixed set of expected values is assumed).
  - Use: After the HTML from bodyHtml is rendered, the page searches the DOM for an element with id equal to sectionId or with a data attribute containing sectionId. If found, the page scrolls to that element and may highlight it. If not found, the page performs no additional scrolling.

- Optional hash fragment: #<fragment>
  - Type: string (no fixed values are assumed).
  - Use: Standard browser behavior will attempt to scroll to an element with a matching id. In addition, the page can read window.location.hash after rendering and, if needed, apply the same generic highlighting logic as for sectionId.

- Navigation to categories:
  - When navigating away from terms.html to a category page, the target URL is category.html with a query parameter categoryId.
  - The value of categoryId is derived from runtime data, not hardcoded: for contextual links, it is taken from attributes within bodyHtml or from associated metadata; for the related categories list, it is taken directly from each item in relatedCategories[].categoryCode.

No state is persisted via localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies by this page. All relevant state is derived from the current URL (for in-page navigation) and from the latest response of getTermsOfUseContent (for content and related categories).

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, high-level page state (loading, success, error), and in-page navigation based on URL parameters and hash fragments for the Terms of Use page.
event_handlers: onInit: Called when terms.html is loaded. Invokes getTermsOfUseContent(), sets loading state to true, and clears previous error state.

onTermsContentLoaded: Triggered when getTermsOfUseContent returns successfully. Sets loading state to false, stores the returned title, bodyHtml, and relatedCategories in memory for child components, and then triggers in-page navigation logic (e.g., sectionId and hash handling).

onTermsContentError: Triggered when getTermsOfUseContent fails. Sets loading state to false, sets an error state flag, and exposes this state so a child component can show a generic error and retry control.
onRetryLoad: Invoked when the user activates a retry control in an error state. Calls getTermsOfUseContent() again and re-enters the loading state.

onAfterRender: Executed after the Terms content has been rendered by the terms-body-renderer component. Reads the sectionId query parameter and URL hash fragment; if present, delegates to a scroll-to-section behavior that attempts to scroll to and highlight the matching element.

components.functionality: Displays the Terms of Use page title using the title field returned from getTermsOfUseContent, without hardcoding the text.
event_handlers: onTitleChange: Invoked when the title value from getTermsOfUseContent changes (for example, on reload). Updates the displayed heading accordingly.

components.functionality: Renders the Terms of Use body HTML returned by getTermsOfUseContent.bodyHtml, attaches click handlers to internal help category links, and exposes hooks for post-render scrolling/highlighting.
event_handlers: onBodyHtmlChange: Called when bodyHtml is updated. Re-renders the HTML content and re-attaches event listeners for internal links.

onClickInternalLink: Generic handler for clicks on anchor elements inside the rendered bodyHtml. If the link is recognized as a help category navigation link (based on attributes or URL patterns supplied in bodyHtml), prevents default navigation and emits a navigation event with the associated categoryCode.
onClickNonInterceptedLink: For links not recognized as help category or internal fragment links, allows default browser navigation behavior (e.g., external legal references) to proceed.
onRendered: Fired after the HTML is inserted into the DOM. Notifies the terms-page-controller so it can perform any scroll-to-section logic based on URL parameters or hash.

components.functionality: Displays a list of related help categories associated with the Terms of Use, using the relatedCategories array from getTermsOfUseContent, and provides navigation to the corresponding category.html pages using URL parameters derived from runtime data.
event_handlers: onRelatedCategoriesChange: Triggered when relatedCategories is updated or first loaded. Rebuilds the list of clickable items based on the current array content.
onClickRelatedCategoryItem: Fired when the user clicks a related category item. Reads the clicked item's categoryCode and navigates to category.html?categoryId=<categoryCode>.

components.functionality: Translates navigation events originating from contextual links inside the Terms body into URL-based navigation to category.html with appropriate categoryId parameters.
event_handlers: onContextualCategoryLink: Receives an event from terms-body-renderer containing the categoryCode of the clicked contextual link. Constructs a URL of the form category.html?categoryId=<categoryCode> and triggers navigation.

onValidateCategoryCode: Optionally checks the categoryCode from the contextual link against the categoryCode values present in relatedCategories to confirm that it is valid before navigation. If not present, it still allows navigation using the provided categoryCode in a generic manner without assuming specific codes.
onNavigateToCategory: Performs the actual navigation to category.html with the categoryId query parameter set to the chosen categoryCode.

components.functionality: Displays loading indicators while getTermsOfUseContent is in progress and a generic error state if the load fails, with a user-triggered retry mechanism.
event_handlers: onLoadingStateChange: Updates the visibility of loading indicators when the loading state changes.
onErrorStateChange: Displays or hides a generic error message and retry control based on the error state flag.
onClickRetry: Invoked when the user clicks a retry control. Calls terms-page-controller.onRetryLoad to attempt to load the Terms content again.

components.functionality: Handles deep linking within the Terms of Use content based on URL query parameters and hash fragments, without assuming any particular section identifiers.
event_handlers: onAfterTermsRender: Triggered by terms-page-controller.onAfterRender. Reads the current URL's query parameters and hash fragment, and attempts to find a matching element in the renderedDom using generic rules (e.g., id matching sectionId or hash, or a data attribute match). If found, scrolls the element into view and applies a generic highlight class.

onUrlChange: Listens for changes to the URL (for example, hash changes while the user is on the page). Re-runs the same matching and scrolling logic to navigate to the newly specified section if one is found.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a centered main content column. The article list appears to use two equal-width columns (approximately 6 + 6) with a central gutter.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom flow inside a centered main content column on a full-width dark background. Below the global top navigation (outside this scope), the Terms of Use page begins with a prominent, vertically padded title/header block. Immediately beneath that, an in-page section navigator is introduced as the main utility element, followed by the long-form Terms body content. At the bottom of the main content column, a related categories area mirrors the mockup’s two-column link list, and the page terminates with a thin horizontal rule. Controllers and handlers sit behind these visual components, managing state but not altering the visual ordering.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom flow inside a centered main content column on a full-width dark background. Below the global top navigation (outside this scope), the Terms of Use page begins with a prominent, vertically padded title/header block. Immediately beneath that, an in-page section navigator is introduced as the main utility element, followed by the long-form Terms body content. At the bottom of the main content column, a related categories area mirrors the mockup’s two-column link list, and the page terminates with a thin horizontal rule. Controllers and handlers sit behind these visual components, managing state but not altering the visual ordering.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The title header consumes a noticeable vertical block (roughly 20–25% of the initial viewport height) to establish hierarchy, similar to the hero-style search area in the mockup. The section navigator and body content together occupy the majority of the vertical space, with the body being the largest area. The related categories block sits below the body with a generous vertical margin (around 32–48px), using a two-column layout to echo the mockup’s article list. Horizontally, all content is constrained to a centered column spanning about 70–80% of the viewport, aligned to an implicit 12-column grid with generous left/right margins for a framed feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Typography and spacing favor readability: medium line length, clear hierarchy, and comfortable vertical rhythm. The Terms content is dense by nature, but is visually moderated with consistent section spacing and clear headings. The section navigator and related categories use two-column or left-column layouts to increase information density without crowding, maintaining at least 16–24px vertical spacing between list items and 32–40px between major blocks.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the top-left brand/navigation (global) down to the centered Terms of Use title as the primary focal point. From there it naturally drops slightly to the in-page section navigator (primary utility), then flows rightward and downward into the Terms body content, reading top-to-bottom. After scrolling through the body, the eye reaches the related categories list, scanning the left column of links top-down, then the right column, mirroring the mockup’s Most Popular Articles pattern. Loading/error states temporarily override this flow by centering feedback within the main content area.

layout.overall_description: The Terms of Use page sits on a full-width, near-black background with a centered main content column that feels similar to a streaming platform UI. Below the site’s horizontal navigation bar, a tall, dark header band introduces the page with a large, white “Terms of Use” title centered on the screen and a concise supporting line beneath it, giving the page a calm, cinematic opening. Under this, the content column splits subtly into a left utility rail and a right reading area: on the left, a vertical in-page section navigator lists the major Terms sections, while on the right the Terms text flows in a comfortable, readable column of off-white copy. As the user scrolls, the section navigator stays visually anchored, creating a stable reference while the Terms content scrolls alongside it. Once the Terms text ends, a wide, dark band continues the main column and introduces a “Related Help Categories” area, using two equal-width columns of compact text links reminiscent of the mockup’s Most Popular Articles grid. The page ends with a thin, low-contrast horizontal divider spanning the content width, subtly marking the visual endpoint. Throughout, spacing is generous but not wasteful, maintaining a focused, utilitarian feel aligned with modern streaming service help centers.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-page-controller is a non-visual container that owns the entire main content region below the global site navigation and above the footer/divider. In spatial terms, it defines the vertical slice starting approximately one standard navigation bar height from the top of the viewport and extending down to the thin horizontal separator at the bottom of the page content. Horizontally, it is centered and constrained to the main content width (about 70–80% of the viewport, following a 12-column grid), and it orchestrates which of its children (loading state, error state, title, body, navigators, related list) are visible within this central column. It does not introduce its own visible padding beyond inheriting the global content column margins, keeping the visual structure consistent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the parent layout context for all Terms of Use components within the centered main content column. It stretches to fit the height of its children and does not scroll independently; scrolling is handled at the page level.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-title-header occupies the first visible block of the Terms page content, immediately below the global navigation. It spans the full width of the centered content column (approximately 12 grid columns within 70–80% of the viewport width) and sits on the dark background with no visible card outline, only relying on vertical padding to define its space. Vertically, it forms a hero-like band roughly 20–25% of the initial viewport height, with 48–64px of padding above the title and 32–48px below the subtitle. The main title text (e.g., “Terms of Use”) is centered horizontally, using large, bold, white typography. Directly beneath, a short, single-line description (e.g., “Read the terms governing your use of our streaming service and help center”) is also horizontally centered and slightly dimmer, creating a focused, stacked composition in the middle of the content column. This header block visually anchors the page and clearly separates the top navigation from the scrolling Terms content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element. It scrolls away normally as the user moves down the page and does not stick or float. On narrow viewports, the text remains centered with responsive font scaling but keeps similar top and bottom padding proportions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-section-navigator is positioned directly below the terms-title-header, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 32–40px to create a clear transition from page introduction to interactive navigation. Within the centered main content column, the layout now suggests a two-column structure: on desktop, the navigator occupies the left portion corresponding to roughly 3–4 grid columns (about 25–30% of the content column width), starting flush with the left edge of the content column. It appears as a vertical list of section labels (e.g., “1. Introduction”, “2. Account Terms”, etc.), each left-aligned, with uniform vertical spacing of around 12–16px between items and subtle separators or hover states. The top of this navigator aligns horizontally with the first line of the Terms body on the right, creating a cohesive, two-column reading structure. On tablet and mobile, where a two-column layout would be cramped, the navigator shifts to a full-width element above the body, framed as either a horizontal pill list or a vertically stacked list that spans nearly the full width of the content column, while maintaining at least 16px padding on the left and right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger tablets, the navigator behaves as a sticky left rail: once the user scrolls past the header, it remains fixed within the viewport’s left side of the content column, starting just beneath the bottom of the global navigation, while the Terms body scrolls on the right. It respects a top offset equal to the nav bar height plus a small margin (about 16–24px). On small screens, it becomes a standard, non-sticky block that scrolls with the page to avoid consuming precious vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-body-renderer forms the primary reading column for the Terms of Use content and is positioned alongside the section navigator. On desktop, it sits to the right of the terms-section-navigator, beginning on the same horizontal baseline, and occupies the remaining width of the centered content column—approximately 8–9 grid columns (about 70–75% of the content column width). The text is left-aligned, with off-white body copy on a near-black background, using a line length of roughly 60–75 characters to maintain readability. There is at least 24–32px of horizontal space (gutter) between the navigator on the left and the text column on the right, echoing the generous central gutter seen in the mockup’s two-column article list. Vertically, the body renderer extends downward for as many screens as needed; headings within the Terms obtain extra top margin (24–32px) to break up sections and to correspond visually with items in the section navigator. On smaller screens, the body renderer becomes a full-width text column within the centered content area, appearing directly beneath the section navigator block with a vertical gap of about 24px, and all horizontal gutters are simplified to consistent side margins (16–20px padding from the viewport edges).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling content area; it does not stick or float. Its top aligns with the navigator’s top in multi-column mode, and it expands vertically based on the dynamic HTML content, pushing the related categories section downward. It gracefully reflows from a two-column-with-rail layout to a single-column layout on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-categories-list appears after the Terms body content and is separated from the final paragraph of the Terms by a generous vertical margin of about 40–48px, signaling a shift from legal content to navigational suggestions. It resides within the same centered main content column and begins with a left-aligned section heading (e.g., “RELATED HELP CATEGORIES”) in all caps or strong uppercase style, mirroring the mockup’s “MOST POPULAR ARTICLES” heading. This heading spans the width of the content column but remains visually aligned with the left edge of the Terms body text. Beneath the heading, after a 16–24px gap, the list entries are arranged into two equal-width vertical columns, each representing about 6 grid columns, with a clear central gutter of 24–32px. Each column contains a stack of text-only links (category names), left-aligned, with 12–16px vertical spacing between items. On desktop, this two-column layout echoes the mockup’s article list, allowing multiple categories to be visible without feeling crowded. On tablet and mobile, the layout gracefully collapses to a single column centered within the content area, maintaining the same vertical spacing between links and a comfortable top margin beneath the heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the bottom of the Terms content area. It scrolls into view after the user passes through the Terms body. It does not stick or overlay. The two-column layout is responsive: two columns on wider screens, a single column on smaller screens, always respecting the central content margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contextual-category-navigation-handler is non-visual and does not directly occupy any discernible space in the layout. Conceptually, it operates within the vertical region owned by the terms-page-controller, coordinating interactions between the Terms body and related navigation elements (such as the related-categories-list and potentially higher-level help center navigation). It may influence which categories appear in the related-categories-list or how links scroll or route the user, but it does so without adding additional padding, margins, or visible components. From a layout perspective, it is simply co-located with the other components in the main content column, without changing their physical positions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible behavioral layer. It does not scroll independently and does not create fixed or sticky regions. Instead, it listens to and triggers navigation actions within the existing scrollable layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-loading-and-error-state occupies the same content territory as the combination of the section navigator, Terms body, and related categories, but only when active. When the Terms are loading, it overlays or replaces this region with a centered loading indicator: a spinner or progress icon positioned in the middle of the main content column, roughly at 40–50% of the viewport height, with a short, single-line status message beneath it, both horizontally centered. The surrounding area remains the same dark background, with sufficient vertical padding so that the loader appears visually grounded within the central column rather than stuck to the top. When an error occurs, the state presents a concise error message and optional retry action centrally within this same region, aligned to the center of the content column with 24–32px of vertical spacing between the message and any button. In both states, the terms-title-header above remains visible, preserving context that the user is on the Terms of Use page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces or overlays the main content zone below the title header. In loading mode, it may either fully replace the navigator/body/related sections or sit above them while dimming them; in either case, it keeps its messaging centered in the viewport. It scrolls minimally—in most implementations it is vertically centered and the page height is limited so that the user is not encouraged to scroll until content is ready.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Page that retrieves and displays the Privacy Policy for the video streaming service Help Center, including how support and Help Center usage data is handled, and provides navigation to relevant account and profile management help.

core_features: Fetch the Privacy Policy content using getPrivacyPolicyContent when the page loads and bind the returned title and bodyHtml to the page.
Render the Privacy Policy body as HTML, supporting internal anchors and in-page navigation (e.g., jumping to specific sections).
Expose links within the Privacy Policy content that, when clicked, navigate to account and profile management help at category.html with categoryId=account_profiles.

Display related topics from getPrivacyPolicyContent.relatedTopics to help users understand which areas of privacy and data handling are covered, and allow users to jump to relevant sections within the policy.
user_workflows: Accessing the Privacy Policy: 1) User opens privacy.html (via footer link or direct URL). 2) On initial render, the page calls getPrivacyPolicyContent with no parameters. 3) While waiting, a loading state is shown. 4) On success, the page displays the title from the response and renders the bodyHtml as the main content. 5) The relatedTopics list is populated from the response.

Navigating to a specific section via URL: 1) User opens privacy.html with an optional section query parameter (e.g., privacy.html?section=<section-id>). 2) The page calls getPrivacyPolicyContent. 3) After bodyHtml is rendered, the page looks for an element whose id matches the section parameter. 4) If found, the page scrolls to that element. 5) If not found, the page remains at the top of the policy without error.

Following account & profile privacy help from the policy: 1) User scrolls through the Privacy Policy until they see a link referring to account or profile privacy settings. 2) This link is represented in bodyHtml and includes an attribute indicating that it targets the account_profiles help category. 3) When the user clicks this link, the click handler intercepts it and navigates the browser to category.html?categoryId=account_profiles. 4) The user now views help articles specific to account and profile management.

Using related privacy topics to navigate the policy: 1) User reviews the relatedTopics list (populated from getPrivacyPolicyContent.relatedTopics). 2) User clicks on a topic in the list. 3) The page derives a section identifier from the clicked topic's topicCode (for example by prefixing or otherwise transforming the code) and attempts to find a matching section anchor in the rendered bodyHtml. 4) If a matching anchor is found, the page scrolls to that section and updates the section query parameter in the URL accordingly.
interactions: Initial load (no user action): On page mount, a request to getPrivacyPolicyContent is triggered. Success populates the page state with title, bodyHtml, and relatedTopics. Failure triggers an error state that can be retried by the user.

Click on in-body links: When a user clicks any anchor inside the rendered bodyHtml, a generic click handler inspects the element. If the anchor indicates a target help category for account and profile management (for example via a data-target-category attribute with value account_profiles), the default browser navigation is prevented and the handler programmatically navigates to category.html?categoryId=account_profiles. Other links that do not match this pattern fall back to normal browser behavior.

Click on related topic item: When a user clicks an item in the relatedTopics list, the handler uses the associated topicCode to construct an expected section id (for example, by combining a fixed prefix with the topicCode). It then attempts to scroll to the corresponding anchor in the policy body. If the section is found, the handler also updates the URL’s section query parameter to the selected section id.

Retry after load error: If getPrivacyPolicyContent fails, the page shows an error state that includes a retry control. When the user activates this control, the page re-invokes getPrivacyPolicyContent and re-runs the standard success/error flow.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page itself has a single primary route: privacy.html. No required URL parameters are needed to fetch content. Optional URL query parameters are used to manage in-page state and deep-linking: 1) section: If present (e.g., privacy.html?section=<section-id>), after getPrivacyPolicyContent completes and bodyHtml is rendered, the page attempts to scroll to an element with id equal to the section value. When the user navigates between sections using the relatedTopics component or other in-page controls, this parameter is updated to reflect the current section, keeping the URL shareable. 2) from: An optional context parameter (e.g., privacy.html?from=<source>) can be accepted and stored in transient page state for analytics or debugging purposes only; it does not change the functional behavior of the page. Navigation away from this page to category.html always sets categoryId=account_profiles for the account and profile management help path, in line with the outgoing navigation definition. No other persistent state is stored; no localStorage or sessionStorage is used. All dynamic content is driven by the response from getPrivacyPolicyContent and current URL parameters.

components.functionality: On page mount, call getPrivacyPolicyContent to retrieve the Privacy Policy content. Manage loading, success, and error states, and expose the loaded data (title, bodyHtml, relatedTopics) to child components via a shared state or context mechanism.
event_handlers: onMount
onRetryClick

components.functionality: Display the Privacy Policy title returned from getPrivacyPolicyContent and synchronize it with the document title for the browser tab.

Render the Privacy Policy body using the HTML content returned in bodyHtml. Support in-page anchor links and intercept clicks on anchors that are intended to open account and profile management help, redirecting those to category.html with the appropriate URL parameter.
event_handlers: onAnchorClick

components.functionality: Render the list of related privacy topics returned from getPrivacyPolicyContent.relatedTopics. Each topic item can be used to navigate to a corresponding section within the policy by mapping the topicCode to an anchor id in the rendered bodyHtml, and to update the section query parameter in the URL.
event_handlers: onTopicClick

components.functionality: Synchronize the in-page scroll position with the section query parameter. On initial load or URL change, if a section parameter is present, scroll to the corresponding element in the policy content once bodyHtml has been rendered. When in-page navigation changes the active section (for example via the related topics list), update the section parameter without reloading the page.
event_handlers: onUrlChange
onSectionChange

components.functionality: Detect and handle clicks on links inside the Privacy Policy that are meant to direct users to account and profile management help. When an anchor element within bodyHtml is clicked and it indicates that its target category is account_profiles, prevent the default navigation and programmatically route the user to category.html with categoryId=account_profiles.
event_handlers: onAnchorClick

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a centered main content column. The article list appears to use two equal-width columns (approximately 6 + 6) with a central gutter.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom flow within a centered main content column on a full-width dark background. The global Help Center navigation (outside this component list) sits at the very top. Immediately beneath it, the Privacy Policy page content begins with the page title, followed by the long-form policy body text. Below the body, a clearly separated section presents privacy-related help topics in a two-column text-link layout that mirrors the mockup’s ‘Most Popular Articles’ pattern. Non-visual components (data loader, URL state handler, navigation handler) sit logically at the top/infrastructure level without taking visual space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom flow within a centered main content column on a full-width dark background. The global Help Center navigation (outside this component list) sits at the very top. Immediately beneath it, the Privacy Policy page content begins with the page title, followed by the long-form policy body text. Below the body, a clearly separated section presents privacy-related help topics in a two-column text-link layout that mirrors the mockup’s ‘Most Popular Articles’ pattern. Non-visual components (data loader, URL state handler, navigation handler) sit logically at the top/infrastructure level without taking visual space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The navigation (global, outside scope) uses modest vertical space (~8–10% of viewport). The Privacy Policy title and top-of-page header block take a noticeable but not oversized chunk (~10–15% of viewport height on first load), giving the title clear prominence. The policy body occupies the majority of the vertical space as scrollable content. The related topics section sits below the main body with a generous top margin (roughly 32–48px) and its own internal spacing to breathe. Horizontally, all visible content is constrained to a central column spanning about 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop (max-width around 960–1120px), with sizable dark margins on the left and right to maintain the framed feel of the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Typography and spacing favor readability: comfortable line lengths, clear separation between headings and paragraphs, and consistent vertical rhythm based on an 8px scale. The policy body is a single column for clarity, while the related topics leverage a two-column layout to increase information density in a controlled way, with a generous gutter and 16–24px vertical spacing between individual links. Section-to-section gaps (title → body, body → related topics) are 32–48px to avoid crowding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the top navigation down to the left-aligned ‘Privacy Policy’ title, which acts as the main entry point. From the title, the eye naturally travels downward into the policy content, scanning headings and paragraphs in a top-to-bottom flow. Upon reaching the end of the policy body, a bold but unobtrusive section heading for privacy-related topics pulls attention further down, where the user then scans through the two columns of links from top to bottom, typically reading the left column first, then the right. The overall flow remains a strong top-to-bottom, left-aligned reading pattern, with a slight content-heavy emphasis in the lower part of the page where related help links are presented.

layout.overall_description: The Privacy Policy page sits on a full-width, dark background. At the very top is the site-wide Help Center navigation (logo and links on a horizontal bar), after which the page’s main content column begins, centered on the screen and framed by generous left and right margins. Near the top of this column, the ‘Privacy Policy’ title appears as a prominent, left-aligned heading with comfortable breathing room above and below, clearly establishing the page context. Directly beneath this title, the policy’s HTML body runs as a single, readable column of white text on black, with well-spaced headings, paragraphs, and lists that extend downward and can be scrolled through. After a noticeable vertical gap, a distinct section heading introduces privacy-related topics, echoing the visual treatment of ‘MOST POPULAR ARTICLES’ from the mockup but tuned to this context. Below this heading, the content splits into two equal-width columns of text-only links, separated by a wide gutter; each column stacks links vertically with consistent spacing, providing many navigation options without feeling crowded. The page concludes with a subtle, thin horizontal divider line that visually closes the content in keeping with the cinematic, minimal aesthetic, while the non-visual functional components (data loading, URL state handling, navigation handling) operate behind the scenes without affecting the clean layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component initializes at the very top of the page’s logical structure, conceptually sitting just beneath the global Help Center navigation bar and above the visible Privacy Policy title. It does not render any visible UI. Spatially, it occupies no height or width; instead, it acts as an invisible layer that prepares and injects the policy data into the centered main content column before the user sees the title and body. It follows the page’s linear flow in terms of processing order but has no visual footprint, ensuring the dark, minimalist layout remains uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered infrastructure component; no visible spatial behavior. Executes before visible components mount and does not affect layout spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page title ‘Privacy Policy’ sits at the top of the main content column directly beneath the global navigation, aligned flush left within the centered column. Horizontally, this column spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop (max-width ~960–1120px), centered with equal dark margins on both sides to create a strong central frame. Vertically, there is a generous top margin from the bottom edge of the navigation bar to the title—approximately 32–48px—to give the heading room to breathe and to make it a clear focal point when the page loads. The title text is a high-contrast white, with a large, bold type size, and no surrounding box or card, matching the flat, content-first feel of the mockup. A smaller vertical gap (about 16–24px) appears beneath the title before the body content begins, keeping the hierarchy clear without feeling detached.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally with the page as part of the main flow; anchored at the top of the main content column and not sticky or fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-body component appears directly below the page title within the same centered main content column. It spans the full width of this column (minus standard internal text padding if applied by the HTML renderer), creating a single-column block of readable text. There is a modest vertical gap of about 16–24px between the bottom of the title and the first line of body text to maintain hierarchy. The text is left-aligned, white on a dark background, with line length tuned to the column width for comfortable reading (no extra columns). Headings inside the HTML content create natural sub-sections, each separated by 24–32px of vertical space, while paragraphs and lists use about 16–24px spacing to maintain a consistent rhythm. The component extends downward as long-form content, occupying the majority of the page’s scrollable height. There is a clearly larger vertical margin (roughly 32–48px) at the end of the body before the related topics section begins, visually signaling the transition from policy text to navigational links.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as standard, scrollable content within the main column; expands vertically with content length while maintaining fixed horizontal constraints of the centered layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-related-topics component forms a distinct section below the privacy-policy-body. It begins after a clear vertical gap of approximately 32–48px from the end of the body, matching the mockup’s separation between the search area and ‘Most Popular Articles’. At the top of this section, within the same centered main column, a left-aligned section heading (e.g., ‘Privacy-related help topics’) appears in a bold, all-caps or strong-weight style reminiscent of ‘MOST POPULAR ARTICLES’. This heading is aligned to the left edge of the main text column and does not span the full viewport, reinforcing the centered grid. Below the heading, after a smaller gap of around 16–24px, the content splits into two equal-width text columns on desktop: each column occupying roughly 50% of the main column width minus a wide central gutter of about 32–48px. Both columns are aligned to the same top baseline. Each column contains a vertical stack of text-only links (related help topics), left-aligned within its column, with 16–24px of vertical spacing between each link to maintain the airy, readable density from the mockup. The overall section feels balanced: dense enough to show multiple links, but visually calm due to the consistent spacing and wide gutter. On smaller screens, these two columns should collapse into a single column that maintains the same left alignment and vertical spacing, preserving the linear reading flow. The bottom of this component leaves a small to moderate vertical margin (about 24–32px) before any page-level bottom rule or footer element, echoing the thin horizontal separator the mockup uses to close the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard content section within the main flow. Layout is responsive: two columns on wider viewports; gracefully collapses to a single column on narrow screens while keeping the same vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-section-router component operates behind the scenes to react to the URL state (such as anchors or query parameters) and potentially scroll or focus to specific sections of the privacy-policy-body. It is conceptually positioned alongside the page-init logic at the top of the content stack but does not render any visible UI or occupy any explicit region on the page. Its presence does not alter spacing between the title, body, or related topics; instead, it influences which part of the existing layout is brought into view, preserving the minimalist, uninterrupted dark canvas.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; no physical space. It may programmatically adjust scroll position within the existing layout but does not itself have spatial behavior like fixed or sticky positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This navigation-handler component is responsible for intercepting and managing navigation when users click on account- or profile-related links, particularly within the privacy-related-topics section and possibly within the policy body. It does not render any visible elements; instead, it attaches behavior to existing text links. Spatially, it is distributed wherever those links appear, but it does not change their position or spacing—links remain within the established two-column related-topics layout and single-column body layout. Thus, the handler preserves the clean, flat text-link appearance while enabling smooth transitions to account help pages.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered behavioral layer; no independent spatial footprint. It travels logically with the clickable links but does not affect layout or positioning.

name: Accessibility
title: Accessibility
description: Accessibility page that loads accessibility help content, highlights subtitle and audio options across devices, and provides ways to contact support about accessibility needs.

core_features: Fetch and display accessibility help content (page title, descriptive body, and guidance) using getAccessibilityHelpContent.
List subtitles, captions, and audio help articles grouped by device, based on subtitleHelpArticles from getAccessibilityHelpContent, with navigation to device-specific articles.
Provide a navigation link to a device-focused help area (devices.html) for users who prefer browsing accessibility topics by device.
Display accessibility-related contact options returned in contactOptionsForAccessibility, including type, label, availability, and estimated wait time.
Enable navigation to contact_support.html using an accessibility-specific entry point when any accessibility contact option is selected.
user_workflows: View general accessibility information: 1) User opens accessibility.html (via footer or direct link). 2) Page controller calls getAccessibilityHelpContent. 3) On success, the page shows the accessibility title and bodyHtml content, including general guidance about subtitles, audio, and accessibility features. 4) If a focus-related URL parameter is present (for example, indicating subtitles or contact), the page scrolls to the corresponding section after content is rendered.

Open subtitles help for a specific device: 1) User scrolls to the subtitles/captions section where subtitleHelpArticles are listed. 2) User selects a device-specific article from the list. 3) The click handler reads the selected articleId from subtitleHelpArticles and the current UI language from the URL (for example, lang parameter if present, or a default from global app state). 4) Browser navigates to article.html with articleId and lang set as URL parameters.

Browse device-specific accessibility help: 1) User is on accessibility.html and wants more device-focused help. 2) User activates a link or button labeled to browse device-specific accessibility help. 3) Click handler navigates to devices.html without additional parameters, where the user can continue exploring accessibility topics by device.

Contact support about accessibility: 1) User reviews the contact options list populated from contactOptionsForAccessibility. 2) User selects a preferred contact option (for example, chat, email, phone, or web form), provided it is marked as available. 3) Click handler navigates to contact_support.html with URL parameters source=generic and sourcePageType=other. 4) The contact_support.html page uses those parameters to pre-contextualize the request as accessibility-related (actual contact method selection can be reconfirmed or refined on that page).

Handle unavailable or limited contact options: 1) If some contactOptionsForAccessibility entries are not available (available = false), the page displays them as disabled and non-clickable. 2) User can only click available options. 3) If no options are available, the component shows a generic message or alternative guidance using bodyHtml, still without hardcoded IDs or labels, while avoiding navigation to contact_support.html.
interactions: Initial content load: On page load, the controller component calls getAccessibilityHelpContent. While loading, the page shows a loading state. On success, it binds title, bodyHtml, subtitleHelpArticles, and contactOptionsForAccessibility into the respective components. On failure, it shows a non-technical error state with an option to retry the request.

Subtitle help article click: When the user clicks a device-specific subtitles/captions/audio help entry, the onClick handler reads that entry’s articleId and uses the current lang URL parameter (if present) to construct a navigation to article.html?articleId={articleId}&lang={lang}.

Device help navigation click: When the user clicks the link to browse device-specific accessibility help, the onClick handler navigates directly to devices.html, preserving any global parameters (such as lang) if needed.

Accessibility contact option click: When the user clicks an available contact option, the onClick handler navigates to contact_support.html?source=generic&sourcePageType=other. If the design includes propagating the user’s chosen contact type, this can be appended as an additional URL parameter without altering the required source and sourcePageType values.

Section focusing via URL: When the page loads, the controller checks optional URL parameters (for example, a parameter indicating a target section such as subtitles or contact). If present, after getAccessibilityHelpContent resolves and the DOM is updated, the controller scrolls focus to the matching section component.

Retry load interaction: If getAccessibilityHelpContent fails, the user can activate a retry control. The onClick handler re-invokes getAccessibilityHelpContent and refreshes the bound data in all dependent components.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Accessibility page does not require any URL parameters to function, but it reads and propagates certain optional parameters for consistency and deep-linking. A lang URL parameter, if present, is treated as the current UI language code; it is not passed to getAccessibilityHelpContent (which has no parameters) but is used when forming navigation URLs to article.html (article.html?articleId={id}&lang={lang}) and optionally to devices.html and contact_support.html to keep language consistent. The page may also support an optional section parameter, whose value is used purely on the client to decide which section to focus or scroll to after getAccessibilityHelpContent has loaded (for example, focusing the subtitles list or contact options). All state relevant to the content (title, bodyHtml, subtitleHelpArticles, contactOptionsForAccessibility) is derived solely from getAccessibilityHelpContent and is not persisted in localStorage; state transitions (like opening an article or going to contact support) are represented via URL navigation per the outgoing_connections definitions.

components.functionality: Coordinates the lifecycle of the Accessibility page: triggers getAccessibilityHelpContent on load, manages loading and error states, distributes returned data to child components, and handles URL-derived state such as current language and target section.
event_handlers: onLoad: Invoke getAccessibilityHelpContent, store the resolved data in page state, and notify child components to render.
onLoad: Read optional lang and section parameters from accessibility.html’s URL and store them in controller state for use by navigation and focusing logic.
onDataLoaded: After successful data fetch, trigger focus/scroll behavior based on the section parameter, if present.
onError: Set an error state if getAccessibilityHelpContent fails and instruct a feedback component to display a retry option.
onRetryClick: Re-invoke getAccessibilityHelpContent and reset error/loading indicators.

components.functionality: Displays the main accessibility page heading using the title from getAccessibilityHelpContent, ensuring it is exposed as the primary page heading for assistive technologies.

Renders the descriptive accessibility content returned as bodyHtml, including any structured sections about accessibility options, using sanitized HTML and ensuring internal anchors (if present) can be navigated to.
event_handlers: onInternalAnchorClick: If bodyHtml contains internal anchors, smoothly scroll to the corresponding section component within the page.

components.functionality: Displays a list of subtitles, captions, and audio help entries for each device, sourced from subtitleHelpArticles. Each entry allows the user to open a device-specific article in article.html.
event_handlers: onRender: Iterate over subtitleHelpArticles and create one subtitle-help-article-item component per entry.
onItemClick: Delegate click events from subtitle-help-article-item to the navigation handler that opens article.html with the proper articleId and lang parameters.

components.functionality: Represents a single subtitles/captions/audio help entry for a particular device; shows the device-specific label and article title, and handles navigation to the full article.
event_handlers: onClick: Read articleId from this item and lang from the controller’s state (if present in URL); construct and navigate to article.html?articleId={articleId}&lang={lang} (omitting lang if no language is set).

components.functionality: Provides a direct navigation control for users to move from the Accessibility page to the broader device help page (devices.html) to browse accessibility topics by device.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate to devices.html, optionally preserving the lang parameter from the current URL (for example, devices.html?lang={lang}) when present.

components.functionality: Presents a list of accessibility-specific contact methods (chat, email, phone, web form, etc.) using contactOptionsForAccessibility, including availability and estimated wait times. Only available options are interactive.
event_handlers: onRender: Build one accessibility-contact-option-item per entry in contactOptionsForAccessibility, applying disabled state where available is false.
onItemClick: For available entries, delegate to the navigation handler that opens contact_support.html.

components.functionality: Represents a single contact option in the accessibility context; shows its label, type, and estimated wait time, and triggers navigation to contact support when activated.
event_handlers: onClick: If available is true, navigate to contact_support.html?source=generic&sourcePageType=other, optionally appending a parameter to indicate the selected contact type while preserving the required values for source and sourcePageType.
onClick: If available is false, do nothing and prevent navigation, ensuring the option is non-interactive for keyboard and pointer input.

components.functionality: Indicates that accessibility content is being loaded or that an error occurred; provides controls for the user to retry the load when necessary.
event_handlers: onRetryClick: Invoke the controller’s onRetryClick to re-call getAccessibilityHelpContent.
onStatusChange: Show or hide the loading indicator and error message based on controller-managed loading/error state.

components.functionality: Manages focus and scrolling to specific sections of the page based on URL parameters (for example, focusing the subtitles list or contact options when the page is opened via a deep link).
event_handlers: onDataLoaded: After getAccessibilityHelpContent has resolved and components are laid out, check the section parameter and move focus/scroll to the matching component (such as subtitle-help-articles-list or accessibility-contact-options).

onAnchorClick: When internal links (for example, within bodyHtml) refer to specific sections, update the section parameter in the URL using history manipulation so the state is bookmarkable without reloading.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a centered main content column. The article list appears to use two equal-width columns (approximately 6 + 6) with a central gutter.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict, linear, top-to-bottom flow inside a centered main content column over a full-width dark background. After the global site navigation (outside this spec), the Accessibility page opens with a hero-style header block (title + short description) that visually replaces the mockup’s large search module as the main focal element. Immediately beneath, a body text area presents general accessibility information in a narrower, left-aligned column. Below that, a full-width section heading introduces the Subtitle & Audio help area, followed by a device-specific navigation row and then a two-column list of subtitle help articles, echoing the mockup’s 'Most Popular Articles' layout. The page ends with a distinct contact options section that offers accessibility support CTAs, with a subtle horizontal separator leading into or out of this section.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict, linear, top-to-bottom flow inside a centered main content column over a full-width dark background. After the global site navigation (outside this spec), the Accessibility page opens with a hero-style header block (title + short description) that visually replaces the mockup’s large search module as the main focal element. Immediately beneath, a body text area presents general accessibility information in a narrower, left-aligned column. Below that, a full-width section heading introduces the Subtitle & Audio help area, followed by a device-specific navigation row and then a two-column list of subtitle help articles, echoing the mockup’s 'Most Popular Articles' layout. The page ends with a distinct contact options section that offers accessibility support CTAs, with a subtle horizontal separator leading into or out of this section.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top hero header consumes a comparatively large vertical block, roughly 20–25% of the initial viewport height, with generous padding above and below to make the page title and description dominant. The body content occupies a moderate vertical span immediately below, sized by its text length but with clear top and bottom breathing room (~32–48px). The subtitle help articles section uses a full-width band of content with a left-aligned heading, then vertical spacing, then a reasonably tall two-column list. The contact options section at the bottom is visually lighter but still prominent, separated by ~48–64px from the article list and framed by a thin horizontal separator. Horizontally, all main content is constrained to a centered column (roughly 70–80% of viewport width, max ~1040–1140px) with substantial left/right margins on large screens, giving a framed, cinematic feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderately spacious. Text blocks use comfortable line height with 16–24px spacing between paragraphs. Major sections (header, body, subtitle articles, contact section) are separated vertically by about 32–64px. The two-column article list uses a wide central gutter and 16–24px vertical spacing between items so that the list feels scannable rather than crowded. Buttons in the contact section have ample internal padding (around 12–16px vertical, 24–32px horizontal) and clear gaps between them, making touch/click targets generous while still feeling compact and utilitarian.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey starts at the top-left with the page title set within the centered hero header, then the eye is drawn to the short description just beneath it. From there, the user naturally moves downward into the left-aligned body content. The next strong anchor is the left-aligned 'Subtitle & Audio...' style heading, which establishes the main help content area. The eye then follows top-to-bottom within that section: first across the horizontal device navigation links (scanned left-to-right), then down through the two columns of subtitle help article links, typically reading top-down in the left column before scanning the right column. Finally, attention drops to the bottom contact section where the CTAs are visually grouped and centered, supported by a thin horizontal divider that signals the page’s conclusion. Overall motion remains predominantly top-to-bottom, left-to-right, with clear focal points anchoring each major section.

layout.overall_description: The Accessibility page sits on a full-width, near-black background with a centered main content column that never touches the viewport edges. At the top of the content area, a broad hero block presents the 'Accessibility' title in bold, white type, centered horizontally and vertically padded so it feels like the page’s main interactive focus, similar in visual weight to a streaming service’s search hero. A concise, muted description line sits directly beneath the title, also centered, reinforcing the page purpose. Below this hero, the layout shifts to left-aligned reading: a narrower column of body text introduces the service’s accessibility philosophy and key policies. Further down, a strong left-aligned section title introduces subtitle and audio help, sitting at the same horizontal alignment as the body text. Immediately under that heading, a slim horizontal row of device navigation links allows switching between platforms, still hugging the left edge. Beneath this, the content opens into a structured two-column grid of text-only subtitle help article links that runs down the page, with a generous central gutter and comfortable vertical spacing, echoing the mockup’s two-column article layout. After the last article, a visible gap and a subtle horizontal divider lead into a final support section: a short heading prompts users who still need help, followed by a horizontally centered cluster of substantial, high-contrast buttons offering different ways to contact accessibility support. The overall effect is a calm, utilitarian, dark-themed help page with a clear vertical rhythm and emphasis on scannable content and straightforward action paths.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component acts as the invisible structural wrapper for the entire Accessibility page content. It occupies the full viewport width and at least the full viewport height (min-height: 100vh) with a solid dark background, setting the overall cinematic, streaming-service feel. Inside it, all visible components are constrained to a centered main content column that uses an implicit 12-column grid: the usable content area is roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, capped at about 1040–1140px, with equal left and right margins forming a dark frame on large screens. Vertically, the controller stacks its child components in a linear order: header at the top, then body content, then subtitle help section (device navigation + two-column articles), and finally the contact options section at the bottom. The controller also hosts the full-page loading overlay and manages non-visual behaviors (focus management) layered above or within this content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width page container with min-height equal to the viewport; establishes the central grid and background. It also serves as the positioning context for overlays like the loading state (fixed or absolute relative to viewport).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The accessibility-header sits at the top of the main content column, directly beneath the global navigation (if present), and functions as a hero block analogous to the mockup’s large centered search area. It is centered horizontally within the page, spanning about 60–70% of the content column width so that it feels substantial but still framed by dark margins on both sides. Vertically, it uses generous padding: approximately 64–96px of top padding and 48–64px of bottom padding, making it the dominant visual band in the upper third of the page. The main 'Accessibility' heading is horizontally centered, set in large, high-contrast white text.
