Immediately below, a single line of supporting copy (short explanation or tagline) appears, also centered, with about 16–24px of vertical gap from the heading, similar to the helper text under the search bar in the mockup. The entire header sits on the same dark background as the page, with no card borders, just space and typography defining the area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow hero section at the top of the content column. It does not stick on scroll; it scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The accessibility-body-content is positioned directly beneath the header hero, separated by a clear vertical gap of roughly 32–40px. It is aligned to the same horizontal center as the header but with left-aligned text to support comfortable reading. The width of this content is slightly narrower than the hero title block, about 60–65% of the viewport (up to ~760–840px), creating a strong central column of text with even dark margins on both sides. Within this area, multiple paragraphs of HTML content are stacked vertically with approximately 16–24px of spacing between paragraphs. There are no heavy boxes or card borders; the content appears as clean, white text on dark background with ample line-height for readability. The bottom of this body section is separated from the next section heading ('Subtitle & Audio...' area) by a generous margin of roughly 40–48px, establishing it as a distinct conceptual block in the vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow text section whose height grows with content. It scrolls normally and respects the central grid, remaining within the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component defines the main subtitle and audio help section and closely mirrors the mockup’s 'Most Popular Articles' layout. It begins with a left-aligned section heading (for example, 'Subtitle & Audio Help') that sits at the same horizontal start as the body text, approximately one base gutter (16–24px) inset from the content column’s left edge. The heading appears about 48px below the bottom of the body content, establishing a clear new section. Under this heading, separated by about 16–24px of vertical space, sits the device-accessibility-navigation row. Below the device navigation and another 16–24px of vertical gap, the list itself expands into a two-column grid of article links. On medium to large screens, the list area spans the full width of the content column and is divided into two equal-width columns (roughly 6 + 6 in a 12-column grid), separated by a central gutter of about 48–64px. Each column contains a vertical stack of subtitle-help-article-item entries, aligned to the top so that rows in each column roughly correspond in height as content allows. The vertical spacing between article items in each column is about 16–24px, giving a comfortable rhythm. On narrow screens (e.g., mobile), the layout collapses to a single column where items stack vertically using the full content width, but the spacing and left alignment remain consistent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that uses an internal grid to create two columns on wider viewports and a single column on narrow ones. It expands vertically based on the number of articles and scrolls normally as part of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each subtitle-help-article-item is a text-only link sized and spaced to be easily scannable, mimicking the list items in the mockup’s 'Most Popular Articles' section. Within its assigned column, each item spans the full column width. The clickable region uses approximately 8–12px of internal vertical padding and aligns its text flush-left with the column edge. Article titles are displayed in bright white text, with no icons or thumbnails, and may wrap onto two lines if necessary, maintaining a maximum width defined by the column. The vertical space between the baseline of one item and the next is about 16–24px, so items feel clearly separated but not sparse. Hover and focus states can introduce subtle underlines or slight brightness changes, but the structural layout remains flat and minimal. The first item in each column starts close to the top of the list grid under the device navigation area, and subsequent items stack directly underneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow list item within its parent column. It does not change surrounding layout; it only contributes to the column height. The entire row is clickable but remains text-only, with no special floating or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The device-accessibility-navigation sits directly beneath the 'Subtitle & Audio' section heading and above the two-column subtitle-help-articles-list. It is left-aligned to the same vertical axis as the heading and body text, occupying the full width of the content column but only using as much horizontal space as its links require. Within this row, individual device links (e.g., 'TV', 'Mobile', 'Web') are laid out horizontally in a straight line, each separated by about 16–24px of horizontal gap, following the minimalist, flat style. The text is smaller than the section heading but still clearly legible, using white or slightly muted white for inactive states and brighter or underlined styling for the active device. Vertically, this navigation row has a slim profile with around 8–12px of top and bottom padding, ensuring it feels light and filter-like, not heavy. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space between this row and the top of the article list grid. On narrower screens, the links may wrap to a second line or become horizontally scrollable, but they remain aligned to the left edge of the content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow horizontal navigation row sitting inside the subtitle section. It does not stick or float; it scrolls away with the section. On very small viewports, it may allow horizontal scrolling within its row if the links overflow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The accessibility-contact-options component forms the closing support section of the page. It begins after a noticeable vertical gap of about 48–64px from the bottom of the subtitle-help-articles-list. At its top, a thin, subtle horizontal separator line (1px, low-contrast grey) can span the width of the content column, echoing the mockup’s bottom separator and signaling the transition to the final section. Below this line, after about 24–32px of vertical space, a short heading or lead-in snippet (e.g., 'Need more help with accessibility?') is placed, aligned to the left edge of the main text column. Underneath that, separated by around 16–24px, the contact-option buttons are visually grouped in the center of the page: they appear in a horizontal row centered within the content column, with even horizontal spacing between them (around 16–24px) and wrap to additional lines if there are many. This layout creates a clear, inviting band of CTAs at the bottom of the page without feeling heavy or card-like. The entire section uses the same dark background as the rest of the page, relying on space, the subtle divider, and button styling to separate it visually.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow bottom section. It anchors the end of the vertical content stack and grows vertically if more options are added. Buttons are centered using flexbox or similar layout within this section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each accessibility-contact-option-item is rendered as a clear, high-contrast button inside the contact-options section. Buttons are arranged in a horizontal row centered within the content column. Each button has a consistent minimum width (around 180–220px) and uses comfortable internal padding of approximately 12–16px vertically and 24–32px horizontally, following the spacing system’s component padding guidance. The button text is centered within the button and uses bright white text on a dark or slightly elevated dark background, maintaining the minimalist, flat aesthetic. Horizontal spacing between adjacent buttons is about 16–24px; if the viewport is too narrow to hold them in one row, they wrap to the next line while maintaining a vertical gap of around 16px between rows. The overall silhouette is clean and rectangular with slightly softened corners if desired, but no heavy shadows, consistent with the mockup’s flat UI style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow interactive element within a flex container. Buttons remain centered as a group; they do not float or stick. They respond to hover and focus states but do not affect surrounding layout beyond their own space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The accessibility-loading-state appears above all other content when the page is fetching accessibility information. Visually, it takes the form of a full-screen or full-page overlay that covers the entire viewport area, dimming the underlying dark background slightly with a semi-transparent black scrim. At the precise center of the viewport, a compact status block is displayed: a minimalist spinner or progress indicator accompanied by a short, single-line status message in white text (e.g., 'Loading accessibility information…'). This central status block is horizontally and vertically centered, with around 24–32px of internal padding and no heavy borders or card styling, in keeping with the clean, utilitarian look. The underlying page layout (header, body content, lists, and buttons) remains in place but visually recessed beneath the overlay.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay anchored to the viewport, spanning 100% width and 100% height with a high z-index. It sits on top of the accessibility-page-controller’s content and prevents interaction with underlying elements while active. It does not alter the underlying layout; it simply covers it temporarily.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The accessibility-section-focus-manager is entirely non-visual and does not occupy any visible space on the page. Conceptually, it is attached to key sections such as the header, subtitle help articles, and contact options to manage keyboard focus and scroll positioning when users navigate via internal links or device filters. When triggered (for example, after selecting a device in the device-accessibility-navigation or jumping to the contact section), it programmatically moves focus to the relevant heading or section container and may smoothly scroll that section to the top portion of the viewport. This ensures that keyboard and screen reader users perceive the same clear top-to-bottom flow that the visual design suggests, without introducing any extra boxes, gaps, or visual elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering behavioral layer with no spatial footprint. It is logically anchored to section elements but does not affect layout size or positioning; it only adjusts focus and scroll behavior.
